topics.title: Introduction to Nanotechnology - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Nanotechnology - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Nanotechnology** is the systematic manipulation, characterization, production, and application of structures, devices, and systems by controlling the shape and size of matter at the **nanometer scale** (typically **1 to 100 nanometers**), wherein novel physical, chemical, and biological properties—fundamentally different from their bulk counterparts—emerge.

A **nanometer (nm)** is one-billionth of a meter:
$$1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 10^{-3} \text{ \mu m} = 10 \text{ \AA (Angstrom)}$$

For context:
- Diameter of a human hair ≈ **80,000 nm**
- Diameter of DNA strand ≈ **2.5 nm**
- Size of a typical atom ≈ **0.1 to 0.3 nm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Textbook Standard):** The term *Nanotechnology* was first introduced by **Professor Norio Taniguchi (1974)** of Tokyo University of Science, who defined it as: *"The processing of separation, consolidation, and deformation of materials by one atom or one molecule."*
> 
> A more functional and widely accepted definition by the **US National Nanotechnology Initiative (NNI)** states that nanotechnology involves research and development at the **atomic, molecular, or macromolecular levels** in the sub-100 nm length scale, to provide a fundamental understanding of phenomena and materials at the nanoscale and to create and use structures, devices, and systems that have novel properties and functions because of their small size.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine taking a **gold wedding ring** (the classic bulk material). It is shiny, chemically inert, and yellow. Now, if you were to shrink that ring down to particles of about **10 nm** (without changing the material), the resulting nano-gold would appear **ruby red** in solution, become catalytically active, and even melt at a much lower temperature!

**The Analogy — "The LEGO Building Block Principle":**
Think of atoms and molecules as **LEGO bricks**. Bulk material is like a large, pre-assembled LEGO castle—rigid and limited in form. Nanotechnology, however, gives you the power to:
1. **Pick up individual LEGO bricks** (manipulate single atoms/molecules).
2. **Reassemble them** into completely new structures (e.g., a nano-bots, a drug-delivery capsule).
3. **Discover that the new structure has powers** the original castle never had (quantum effects, super-elasticity, targeted delivery in food matrices).

This "power to redesign matter from the bottom-up" is the heart of nanotechnology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Nano" is a Magic Number:**
> Below 100 nm, two extraordinary things happen:
> 1. **Surface Area to Volume Ratio skyrockets** → more atoms are exposed on surfaces, dramatically changing reactivity (critical for food packaging and antimicrobial action).
> 2. **Quantum Mechanical Effects dominate** → electrons are confined, leading to altered optical, magnetic, and electrical properties.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Three Pillars" of Nanotechnology

Nanotechnology is not a single discipline—it rests on three foundational pillars. Every KTU question on "Introduction to Nanotechnology" usually revolves around these:

**Pillar 1 — Nanoscale Science (The "Why")**
- Studies the unique phenomena (quantum confinement, surface effects) that arise only when dimensions shrink below 100 nm.

**Pillar 2 — Nano-Fabrication (The "How")**
- **Top-Down Approach:** Breaking bulk material into smaller pieces (e.g., ball milling, lithography). *Analogy: Carving a statue from marble.*
- **Bottom-Up Approach:** Assembling atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule (e.g., self-assembly, chemical vapor deposition). *Analogy: Building a house brick-by-brick from the ground up.*

**Pillar 3 — Nano-Characterization (The "Proof")**
- Using instruments like **AFM (Atomic Force Microscopy)**, **SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy)**, **TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy)**, and **DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering)** to visualize and measure nanoscale features.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Nano-Scale Behavior — Key Concepts

**(a) Surface Area to Volume Ratio (SVR)**
- As a particle shrinks, a greater fraction of its atoms reside on the surface.
- **Surface atoms are chemically more active** because they have fewer neighboring atoms and unsaturated bonds.
- This is why nano-silver has powerful antimicrobial action in food packaging, and why nano-encapsulated flavors dissolve faster.

**(b) Quantum Confinement**
- When particle size approaches the **de Broglie wavelength** of electrons, electron movement is restricted.
- This changes the **band gap** of semiconductors, altering their color and conductivity (e.g., quantum dots used in smart food sensors).

**(c) Dominance of Brownian Motion & Intermolecular Forces**
- At the nano-scale, **van der Waals forces, electrostatic forces, and Brownian motion** dominate over **gravity** and **inertia**—this is critical in food matrices where nano-emulsions remain stable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Key Parameter** | **Real-World Utility** |
|-------------|--------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Length unit conversion | $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ | Size scale definition | Standardizing particle size in food nanoscience |
| SVR of a sphere | $\text{SVR} = \dfrac{3}{r}$ where $r$ is radius | Surface dominance | Explains high reactivity of nano-additives |
| SVR of a cube (side $a$) | $\text{SVR} = \dfrac{6}{a}$ | Geometric surface | Explains dissolution of nano-nutrients |
| Number of surface atoms (approx.) | $N_s \approx 4N^{2/3}$ (cubic cluster of $N$ atoms) | Surface atom fraction | Catalysis, antimicrobial packaging |
| Fraction of surface atoms | $f_s = \dfrac{N_s}{N} \approx 4N^{-1/3}$ | Quantum size effect onset | Predicts quantum dot behavior |
| Approx. atoms in nano-particle | $N \approx \left(\dfrac{d}{d_{\text{atom}}}\right)^3$ | Atomic packing | Estimating synthesis yield |
| De Broglie wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{mv}$ | Quantum confinement onset | Identifying quantum dot regimes |
| Band gap (effective mass approx.) | $E_g^{\text{nano}} \approx E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$ | Quantum confinement | Designing nano-sensors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why nano-materials differ from bulk?", always mention at least **two of these three**: (1) Increased SVR, (2) Quantum confinement, (3) Dominance of intermolecular forces. Mentioning only one is the most common reason for losing 1–2 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Surface Area to Volume Ratio (SVR) for a Cube

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions for Module 1.

**Step 1: Setup the Geometry**
Consider a cube with side length $a$. Its surface area and volume are elementary geometric quantities.

**Step 2: Write the Volume**
$$V = a^3$$

**Step 3: Write the Total Surface Area**
A cube has 6 identical square faces, each of area $a^2$.
$$A = 6a^2$$

**Step 4: Form the Ratio**
$$\text{SVR} = \frac{A}{V} = \frac{6a^2}{a^3} = \frac{6}{a}$$

**Step 5: Interpret**
The SVR is **inversely proportional to the side length $a$**. As $a$ decreases (nanoscale), the SVR increases dramatically.

For a sphere, the analogous derivation gives $\text{SVR} = \frac{3}{r}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration — Why Nano-Size Matters

**Problem:** A food-grade particle is roughly spherical. Compare the percentage of surface atoms for a particle of diameter **1 µm** vs a particle of diameter **10 nm** (assume atomic diameter $d_{\text{atom}} \approx 0.3 \text{ nm}$).

**Step 1: Total number of atoms in a 1 µm particle**
$$N_{\text{bulk}} = \left(\frac{d}{d_{\text{atom}}}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{1000 \text{ nm}}{0.3 \text{ nm}}\right)^3 \approx (3333)^3 \approx 3.7 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 2: Total number of atoms in a 10 nm particle**
$$N_{\text{nano}} = \left(\frac{10 \text{ nm}}{0.3 \text{ nm}}\right)^3 \approx (33.3)^3 \approx 3.7 \times 10^4$$

**Step 3: Fraction of surface atoms (cubic cluster approximation)**
The fraction of surface atoms is given by:
$$f_s \approx 4N^{-1/3}$$

For the **1 µm particle**:
$$f_s^{\text{bulk}} \approx 4 \times (3.7 \times 10^{10})^{-1/3} \approx 4 \times (1.55 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 6.2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.062\%$$

For the **10 nm particle**:
$$f_s^{\text{nano}} \approx 4 \times (3.7 \times 10^{4})^{-1/3} \approx 4 \times (0.03) \approx 0.12 = 12\%$$

**Step 4: Conclude**
The 10 nm particle has roughly **~200 times more surface atoms** (in fraction) than the 1 µm particle. This is why a tiny amount of nano-ingredient can dramatically change the taste, texture, and shelf-life of a food product.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bulk vs. Nano Behavior

| **Property** | **Bulk Material (µm scale)** | **Nano-Material (1–100 nm scale)** |
|--------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Surface Area / Volume | Low | **Extremely High** |
| % Surface Atoms | < 0.1 % | **5 – 50 %** |
| Optical Properties | Fixed color | Size-dependent color (Quantum Dots) |
| Melting Point | Standard | **Lower** (surface energy effect) |
| Mechanical Strength | Standard | **Higher** (Hall-Petch effect) |
| Chemical Reactivity | Low | **High** (unsaturated surface bonds) |
| Dominant Forces | Gravity / Inertia | **van der Waals / Brownian** |
| Examples in Food | Bulk salt, sugar | Nano-encapsulated curcumin, nano-iron |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define Nanotechnology. What is the size range commonly associated with it?

**Model Answer:**
Nanotechnology is the science, engineering, and technology conducted at the nanoscale, which is about **1 to 100 nanometers**. It involves the manipulation and control of matter at atomic and molecular levels to develop new materials and devices with fundamentally new properties and functions.

*Mark Distribution:*
- 1 mark — Definition
- 1 mark — Size range
- 1 mark — Brief mention of property change

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Differentiate between **Top-Down** and **Bottom-Up** approaches of nanofabrication with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| **Top-Down** | **Bottom-Up** |
|--------------|---------------|
| Starts with bulk material and removes/splits it | Builds nanostructure atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule |
| Example: Ball milling, Photolithography | Example: Self-assembly, Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) |

*Mark Distribution:*
- 1 mark — Each approach explained
- 1 mark — Example for each

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Candidates must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Define Nanotechnology. Discuss the historical evolution of the concept, citing the contributions of **Norio Taniguchi** and **K. Eric Drexler**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **two main reasons** why nanomaterials exhibit properties drastically different from their bulk counterparts. Support your answer with relevant equations. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Nanotechnology is the controlled manipulation of matter at the atomic, molecular, and macromolecular scale (1 to 100 nm) to produce novel materials, devices, and systems with unique properties.

**Historical Evolution (5 marks):**

- **1959** — **Richard Feynman's** seminal lecture *"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"* laid the philosophical foundation, proposing that atoms could be manipulated individually.

- **1974** — **Norio Taniguchi** (Tokyo University of Science) coined the term **"Nanotechnology"** and defined it as *"the processing of separation, consolidation, and deformation of materials by one atom or one molecule."*

- **1981** — **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer** invented the **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)** at IBM Zurich, enabling actual visualization and manipulation of atoms. They won the **Nobel Prize in Physics (1986)**.

- **1985** — **Buckminsterfullerene (C₆₀)**, the "buckyball," was discovered by Kroto, Curl, and Smalley (**Nobel Prize 1996**).

- **1986** — **K. Eric Drexler** published *"Engines of Creation: The Coming Era of Nanotechnology,"* popularizing the concept of **molecular assemblers** and **molecular nanotechnology**.

- **1991** — **Sumio Iijima** discovered **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)**, opening a new chapter in 1D nanostructures.

- **2000s–Present** — The **US National Nanotechnology Initiative (NNI, 2001)** formally launched global funding, and food nanotechnology emerged as a sub-field.

---

**Model Solution for Q (b):**

**Reason 1 — Enhanced Surface Area to Volume Ratio (3.5 marks):**

For a spherical particle of radius $r$, the surface area to volume ratio is:
$$\text{SVR} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$

As $r$ decreases from micrometer to nanometer scale, the SVR increases by **three orders of magnitude**. Surface atoms have unsaturated bonds, leading to higher **chemical reactivity, catalytic activity, and solubility**. In food technology, this allows nano-encapsulated flavors to dissolve rapidly and nano-silver to act as a potent antimicrobial in packaging.

**Reason 2 — Quantum Confinement Effect (3.5 marks):**

When the particle size becomes comparable to the **de Broglie wavelength** of electrons (typically 1–10 nm for many materials), electron motion is restricted. The energy levels become discrete, and the **band gap** widens:
$$E_g^{\text{nano}} \approx E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$$

where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass of the electron-hole pair and $R$ is the particle radius. This causes **size-dependent optical properties** (e.g., quantum dots change color with size) and is exploited in developing **intelligent food sensors** that detect spoilage.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** What is the **Surface Area to Volume Ratio (SVR)**? Derive the SVR for a cube of side $a$ and a sphere of radius $r$. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how nanotechnology is being applied in the **food industry**. Discuss at least two applications. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SVR is the ratio of total surface area of a particle to its volume, indicating how much "exposed surface" is available per unit volume.

**Derivation for Cube (3 marks):**
$$V_{\text{cube}} = a^3$$
$$A_{\text{cube}} = 6a^2$$
$$\text{SVR}_{\text{cube}} = \frac{6a^2}{a^3} = \frac{6}{a}$$

**Derivation for Sphere (3 marks):**
$$V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$
$$A_{\text{sphere}} = 4\pi r^2$$
$$\text{SVR}_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$

**Conclusion:** SVR is inversely proportional to size, becoming enormous at the nanoscale.

---

**Model Solution for Q (b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Food nanotechnology applies nanoscale materials to improve food quality, safety, nutrition, and shelf-life.

**Application 1 — Nano-Encapsulation of Bioactives (3 marks):**
Bioactive compounds like **curcumin, omega-3 fatty acids, or vitamins** are often unstable, poorly soluble, and have unpleasant taste. They are encapsulated inside **nano-emulsions, liposomes, or biopolymer nanoparticles** (typically 50–200 nm). This improves **bioavailability, stability against heat/light**, and enables **controlled release** in the gut.

**Application 2 — Nanocomposite Food Packaging (3 marks):**
Traditional packaging is passive. Modern nano-packaging embeds **nano-silver, nano-zinc oxide, nano-clay, or nano-TiO₂** into polymers. These provide:
- **Antimicrobial action** (Ag, ZnO inhibit bacterial growth).
- **Oxygen and moisture barrier** (nano-clay platelets).
- **UV protection** (TiO₂ nanoparticles).

Example: Nano-silver coated cling film extends the shelf life of fresh meat and cheese by 2–3 times.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **define nanotechnology in a single sentence** and score only 1 out of 3 in Part A. Always add the **size range (1–100 nm)** and **mention of novel properties** to secure full marks.
> - In Part B derivations, many students **forget to state the formula for surface area or volume** before substituting. Always begin with the basic geometric formula and show the substitution.
> - **Confusing "Nano" with "Micro":** A 1000 nm particle is **micro**, not nano. The KTU cutoff is strictly **≤ 100 nm**.
> - When asked about applications in food, do **not** write generic advantages — always tie the property (e.g., SVR) to the application (e.g., faster dissolution, enhanced bioavailability).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Norio Taniguchi, 1974):** Manipulation of materials at the atomic/molecular level.
- **NNI Definition:** Structures, devices, and systems at 1–100 nm with novel size-dependent properties.
- **Size Scale:** 1 nm = $10^{-9}$ m; 1 nm = 10 Å. Atom ≈ 0.1–0.3 nm; DNA ≈ 2.5 nm; Cell ≈ 10,000 nm; Hair ≈ 80,000 nm.
- **Two Pillars of "Why Nano is Different":**
  1. **High Surface Area to Volume Ratio** — $\text{SVR}_{\text{cube}} = \dfrac{6}{a}$, $\text{SVR}_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{3}{r}$.
  2. **Quantum Confinement** — $E_g^{\text{nano}} > E_g^{\text{bulk}}$ as size decreases.
- **Fabrication:** Top-Down (breaking bulk) vs Bottom-Up (assembling atoms).
- **Characterization Tools:** SEM, TEM, AFM, STM, DLS.
- **Key People to Remember for KTU:**
  - **Feynman (1959)** — visionary lecture.
  - **Taniguchi (1974)** — coined the term.
  - **Binnig & Rohrer (1981)** — STM inventors, Nobel 1986.
  - **Drexler (1986)** — popularized molecular nanotechnology.
  - **Iijima (1991)** — discovered carbon nanotubes.
- **Food Applications (must-know):** Nano-encapsulation, nanocomposite packaging, nano-sensors for spoilage detection, nano-emulsions for beverages.
- **KTU Buzzword:** Always use the phrase **"size-dependent novel properties"** when defining nanotechnology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History. Nanoparticles - Physical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History. Nanoparticles - Physical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Nanotechnology? (Formal KTU Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nanotechnology** is the controlled manipulation, design, synthesis, characterization, and application of materials at the **nanoscale (1–100 nm)** in at least one dimension, where size-dependent physicochemical properties emerge that are distinctly different from those of the bulk counterpart.

In the food science context (KTU 2024 Scheme), **Food Nanotechnology** refers to the application of nanoscale engineering to food systems — encompassing production, processing, preservation, packaging, sensing, and nutrient delivery — to improve safety, quality, shelf life, and bioavailability.

### 1.2 Brief History of Nanotechnology

The history of nanotechnology is a fascinating journey of scientific evolution. Below is the chronological timeline aligned with KTU module expectations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Milestones (Mandatory for KTU 3-Mark Questions)**

| Year | Scientist / Event | Landmark Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1959** | Richard P. Feynman | Famous lecture *"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"* — the conceptual seed of nanotechnology. |
| **1974** | Norio Taniguchi | Coined the actual term **"Nanotechnology"** at the International Conference on Production Engineering, Tokyo. |
| **1981** | Gerd Binnig & Heinrich Rohrer | Invented the **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)** at IBM Zurich — Nobel Prize 1986. |
| **1985** | Kroto, Curl, Smalley | Discovered **Fullerene (C₆₀ "Buckyball")** — Nobel Prize 1996. |
| **1986** | K. Eric Drexler | Published *"Engines of Creation"* — proposed molecular nanomachines. |
| **1986** | Gerd Binnig | Invented the **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)**. |
| **1991** | Sumio Iijima | Discovered **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs)**. |
| **2000s–Present** | Various | Commercial nano-food products, nano-encapsulation, smart packaging. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Magic of Scale

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **sugar cube**. Cut it in half — still sugar. Cut it again — still sugar. But keep cutting until the pieces are **1 billionth of a meter** (1 nm) in size. At that point, the sugar is no longer "just sugar" — it dissolves faster, reacts differently, can carry drugs, change color, or block UV light. **Same material, new rules of physics.** That is nanotechnology in essence.

### 1.4 Why the Nanoscale is Special

At the nanoscale, two critical effects dominate:

1. **High Surface Area to Volume Ratio (SA:V):** As particle size decreases, surface atoms become a much larger fraction of total atoms, dramatically increasing reactivity.
2. **Quantum Confinement:** Electrons are spatially restricted, altering optical, electrical, and magnetic properties.

### 1.5 Physical Nanoparticles — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Nanoparticles (Nano-sized materials)** in food science are **inert, inorganic or organic particulate systems** with at least one dimension ≤ **100 nm**, produced primarily by **top-down (physical) fabrication methods** such as high-energy ball milling, high-pressure homogenization, laser ablation, sputtering, and evaporation–condensation.

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus, "Physical Nanoparticles" is **not** just about *physical* synthesis. It broadly refers to **engineered inorganic/physical nanostructures** used in food — *not* biological/chemical nano-emulsions. The distinction is critical for exam answers.

### 1.6 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m = 10⁻⁷ cm = 10 Å (Angstrom)**
- **Diameter range of nanoparticles:** **1 nm – 100 nm**
- **Comparison:** A human hair ≈ 80,000 nm wide; a DNA strand ≈ 2.5 nm wide.
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Physical Nanoparticles in Food

Physical nanoparticles used in food systems are broadly classified into **four families**:

#### 2.1.1 Metal-Based Nanoparticles
- **Silver (Ag-NPs):** Most commercialized. Used for antimicrobial packaging (e.g., nanosilver-coated food containers).
- **Gold (Au-NPs):** Used as colorimetric sensors for detecting food adulterants (e.g., melamine in milk).
- **Zinc Oxide (ZnO-NPs):** UV-blocker in transparent packaging films.
- **Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂-NPs):** Whitening agent (E171) in candies, chewing gums; photocatalytic antimicrobial.
- **Iron Oxide (Fe₃O₄ / γ-Fe₂O₃):** Magnetic nanoparticles — used in food pathogen separation, MRI contrast, and smart packaging.

#### 2.1.2 Carbon-Based Nanoparticles
- **Fullerenes (C₆₀):** Antioxidant activity; radical scavenging in edible oils.
- **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs):** Mechanical strength for food packaging films; sensors for pesticide detection.
- **Graphene Oxide (GO):** Barrier properties in flexible packaging; detection of heavy metals in beverages.

#### 2.1.3 Polymeric Nanoparticles (Physical Assemblies)
- Formed via self-assembly of biopolymers (chitosan, starch, alginate, PLA).
- Used for **encapsulation of nutraceuticals, vitamins, flavors**.

#### 2.1.4 Ceramic / Silica Nanoparticles
- **Silica (SiO₂-NPs):** Anti-caking agent (E551); carriers for bioactive compounds.

### 2.2 Why Are Physical Nanoparticles Different? (The "Why & How")

- **Why are NPs more reactive than bulk?**
  Because the **fraction of surface atoms** follows an inverse relationship with particle diameter. A 10 nm particle has ~**20% of its atoms on the surface**; a 1 nm particle has nearly 100%.

- **How does this matter in food?**
  Higher surface energy → faster dissolution, stronger antimicrobial action, enhanced catalytic degradation of contaminants, controlled release of nutrients.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU ESE — Save This Table**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Unit Conversion** | $1 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-9} \, \text{m} = 10 \, \text{Å}$ | Foundational conversion — must be memorized. |
| 2 | **Surface Area to Volume Ratio** | $\dfrac{SA}{V} = \dfrac{6}{d}$ (for a sphere of diameter $d$) | Higher SA:V for smaller particles. |
| 3 | **Number of Particles per Gram** | $N = \dfrac{6 M}{\pi \rho d^{3}}$ | $M$ = molar mass; $\rho$ = density; $d$ = diameter. |
| 4 | **% Surface Atoms** | $\%\text{Surface} = \dfrac{4 N_s}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_s$ = surface atoms. |
| 5 | **Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) Specific Surface Area** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{m}$ | Used to characterize nanoparticle surface area ($\text{m}^2/\text{g}$). |
| 6 | **Brownian / Diffusion Coefficient (Stokes–Einstein)** | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{3 \pi \eta d}$ | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const; $T$ = temperature; $\eta$ = viscosity; $d$ = particle diameter. |
| 7 | **Plasmon Resonance Condition (for metallic NPs)** | $\lambda_{SPR} \approx \lambda_p \sqrt{1 + 2 \varepsilon_m}$ | $\varepsilon_m$ = dielectric of medium; $\lambda_p$ = bulk plasma wavelength. |
| 8 | **Mie Scattering (small particle limit)** | $Q_{ext} \approx \dfrac{8}{3} x^{4} \left\vert \dfrac{m^{2}-1}{m^{2}+2} \right\vert^{2}$ | $x = \pi d / \lambda$; $m$ = relative refractive index. |
| 9 | **Particle Size from XRD (Scherrer Equation)** | $D = \dfrac{K \lambda}{\beta \cos \theta}$ | $K \approx 0.9$; $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength; $\beta$ = FWHM; $\theta$ = Bragg angle. |
| 10 | **Mass of a single spherical NP** | $m = \dfrac{\pi d^{3} \rho}{6}$ | Used to convert number to mass. |

**Key Constants Used:**

$$k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}, \quad N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}, \quad h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J \cdot s}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Packaging:** Nanosilver/ZnO coatings extend shelf life of perishables (e.g., fresh meat, cheese) by 2–5×.
- **Food Safety Sensing:** Au-NP colorimetric assays detect pathogens like *Salmonella* or *E. coli* within minutes.
- **Nutrient Delivery:** Nano-encapsulation of curcumin, omega-3, vitamin D improves bioavailability up to **5-fold**.
- **Smart Labels:** TiO₂-NPs act as oxygen indicators, changing color when food spoils.
- **Antimicrobial Edible Films:** Chitosan-Ag nanocomposite films inhibit *Listeria monocytogenes* in ready-to-eat meats.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Surface Area to Volume Ratio for a Spherical Nanoparticle

This is the most important derivation for KTU Module 1 — examiners frequently test this.

**Starting Point:** Consider a perfect sphere of diameter $d$ and radius $r = d/2$.

**Step 1: Surface Area of a Sphere**

$$SA = 4 \pi r^{2} = 4 \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi d^{2}$$

**Step 2: Volume of a Sphere**

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{3} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 3: Form the Ratio**

$$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{\frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}} = \frac{\pi d^{2} \cdot 6}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{6}{d}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{6}{d}}$$

> **Interpretation:** If $d$ is in **nm**, then $SA/V$ is in **nm⁻¹**. Halving the particle diameter **doubles** the surface area per unit volume — this is the geometric origin of nanoparticle reactivity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Nanoparticles per Gram of Material

This problem is frequently asked as a numerical in KTU ESE Part B.

**Given:** Material of density $\rho$ (g/cm³) and molar mass $M$ (g/mol). Each particle is a sphere of diameter $d$ (cm).

**Step 1: Mass of a single spherical NP**

$$m = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho = \frac{\pi d^{3} \rho}{6}$$

**Step 2: Number of particles per gram (using 1 g of material)**

$$N = \frac{1 \, \text{g}}{m} = \frac{6}{\pi d^{3} \rho}$$

**Step 3: Expressing in terms of moles (alternative form)**

Since $M$ g contains $N_A$ atoms/molecules, mass per atom = $M/N_A$. The number of NPs in 1 g is:

$$N = \frac{N_A}{M} \cdot \frac{1}{(\text{atoms per NP})}$$

For a single-atom NP (rare; conceptual only), the formula is:

$$N = \frac{6 N_A}{\pi \rho d^{3} M}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> **Problem:** Calculate the number of silver nanoparticles (density = 10.5 g/cm³) per gram, given a particle diameter of 20 nm.

**Solution:**

$$d = 20 \, \text{nm} = 20 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{cm} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{cm}$$

$$N = \frac{6}{\pi (2 \times 10^{-6})^{3} (10.5)} = \frac{6}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-18} \times 10.5}$$

$$N = \frac{6}{2.638 \times 10^{-16}} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{16} \, \text{NPs/g}$$

> **Insight:** **22.7 trillion NPs per gram of silver** — illustrates the enormous reactive surface available even in tiny mass.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Physical Nanoparticles in Food

| Property | Ag-NP | ZnO-NP | TiO₂-NP | SiO₂-NP | Fe₃O₄-NP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Size range (typical)** | 10–80 nm | 20–100 nm | 5–50 nm | 7–80 nm | 10–50 nm |
| **Primary Food Function** | Antimicrobial | UV blocker, antimicrobial | Whitener, photocatalyst | Anti-caking | Magnetic separation |
| **Mechanism of Action** | ROS generation, membrane disruption | Zn²⁺ release, ROS | Photocatalytic ROS | Physical barrier | Magnetic capture of pathogens |
| **Typical Concentration in Food** | 1–100 mg/kg | < 25 mg/kg | < 1% w/w | < 2% w/w | < 50 mg/kg |
| **Key Characterization Tool** | UV-Vis (SPR ~400 nm) | XRD, DLS | XRD (anatase/rutile) | BET, TEM | VSM, TEM |
| **Regulatory Status (FSSAI/EU)** | Restricted | Permitted | Permitted (E171 phased out EU 2022) | Permitted (E551) | Permitted (E172) |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Scherrer Equation (XRD Particle Size)

> **Problem:** A ZnO nanoparticle sample shows an XRD peak at $2\theta = 36.2°$ with FWHM $\beta = 0.4° = 0.00698$ rad. Cu-Kα radiation used ($\lambda = 1.5406 \, \text{Å}$). Calculate crystallite size. ($K = 0.9$)

**Step 1:** Bragg angle

$$\theta = \frac{36.2°}{2} = 18.1°$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\cos \theta$

$$\cos(18.1°) \approx 0.951$$

**Step 3:** Apply Scherrer equation

$$D = \frac{K \lambda}{\beta \cos \theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5406 \, \text{Å}}{0.00698 \times 0.951}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3865 \, \text{Å}}{0.006637} \approx 208.9 \, \text{Å} = 20.89 \, \text{nm}$$

**Conclusion:** Crystallite size ≈ **20.9 nm** — confirms nanoparticle regime.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Briefly explain the history of nanotechnology, highlighting the contributions of Richard Feynman and Norio Taniguchi. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Richard P. Feynman (1959):** In his celebrated lecture *"There's Plenty of Room at the Bottom"* at Caltech, he proposed that atoms and molecules could be manipulated individually to create materials at the nanoscale — laying the **conceptual foundation** of nanotechnology.
- **Norio Taniguchi (1974):** Coined the **actual term "Nanotechnology"** at the Tokyo International Conference on Production Engineering, defining it as the processing of materials at the nanometre level.
- **Significance:** Feynman's vision + Taniguchi's terminology together shaped the modern field of nanotechnology, including food nano-applications. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Define physical nanoparticles and list any four examples used in food packaging. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Physical nanoparticles are engineered particles with at least one dimension in the **1–100 nm** range, synthesized predominantly by top-down physical methods, used in food applications.

**Four examples:**
1. **Silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs):** Antimicrobial packaging.
2. **Zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs):** UV-blocking film.
3. **Titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO₂-NPs):** Whitener and photocatalyst.
4. **Iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄-NPs):** Magnetic separation of pathogens.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition + ½ × 4 for examples)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a) Discuss in detail the historical evolution of nanotechnology from Feynman's lecture to modern food applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **1959 — Feynman's Vision (Conceptual Origin):**
   Feynman's lecture predicted manipulation of matter atom-by-atom; he proposed the idea of "swallowing the doctor" — an early nano-medical concept.

2. **1974 — Taniguchi's Coining (Terminology):**
   Defined nanotechnology as material processing at the nanometre scale for improved precision.

3. **1981–1986 — Microscopy Revolution (Tooling):**
   - STM (1981) and AFM (1986) enabled direct visualization of nanostructures.
   - Nobel Prizes (1986) accelerated research.

4. **1985–1991 — New Nanomaterials Discovered:**
   - Fullerenes (C₆₀, 1985).
   - Carbon Nanotubes (1991).

5. **2000s — Convergence with Food Science:**
   - Nano-encapsulation of bioactives.
   - Antimicrobial nano-packaging.
   - Nanosensors for pathogens.

6. **2010s–Present — Commercialization & Regulation:**
   - Smart packaging, edible nano-coatings.
   - Regulatory frameworks (FSSAI, EFSA, FDA).

7. **Significance for Food Industry:**
   Improved shelf life, safety, bioavailability, traceability, and consumer acceptance.

**(7 marks — ~1 mark per major milestone with brief explanation)**

---

**Q. (b) Classify physical nanoparticles used in food systems and explain the mechanism of action of silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) as an antimicrobial agent. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (4 marks):**

| Type | Examples | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Metal-based | Ag, Au, ZnO, TiO₂ | Antimicrobial, UV block |
| Carbon-based | Fullerene, CNT, GO | Sensors, packaging |
| Silica | SiO₂ | Anti-caking |
| Magnetic | Fe₃O₄ | Pathogen capture |

**Mechanism of Ag-NP Antimicrobial Action (3 marks):**

1. **Ag⁺ Ion Release:** Ag-NPs slowly release silver ions that interact with microbial cell membranes.
2. **Membrane Disruption:** Ions bind to sulfhydryl (–SH) groups of enzymes → loss of membrane integrity.
3. **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) Generation:** NPs catalyze formation of O₂⁻, OH⁻ radicals → oxidative damage to DNA and proteins.
4. **Result:** Bacterial cell death → extended shelf life of food.

**(7 marks complete)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a) With neat diagrams, explain the top-down physical methods for the synthesis of food-grade nanoparticles. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Top-Down Methods:**

1. **High-Energy Ball Milling (Mechanical Attrition):**
   - Bulk material + balls in a rotating jar.
   - Repeated impact, fracturing, and welding reduce particle size to nano range.
   - Used for producing metal and ceramic NPs (e.g., ZnO, Fe₃O₄).

2. **High-Pressure Homogenization (HPH):**
   - Suspension forced through a narrow orifice at high pressure.
   - Cavitation, shear, and turbulence break particles.
   - Used for nanoemulsions in food (e.g., curcumin nanoemulsions).

3. **Laser Ablation:**
   - Pulsed laser vaporizes a solid target submerged in liquid.
   - Condensation forms pure NPs without chemical reagents.
   - Food-grade noble metal NPs.

4. **Sputter Deposition:**
   - Plasma bombards a target; ejected atoms deposit as thin film / NPs.
   - Used for TiO₂-Ag composite coatings on packaging films.

**Diagram (Block Schematic):**

```
[Bulk Material] → [Energy Input: Mechanical/Laser/Pressure]
       ↓
[Particle Size Reduction 1–100 nm]
       ↓
[Physical Nanoparticles]
```

**(7 marks: 1.5 per method + 1 for diagram)**

---

**Q. (b) The diameter of a spherical silver nanoparticle is 15 nm. Density of Ag = 10.5 g/cm³. Calculate (i) surface area to volume ratio and (ii) number of particles per gram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $d = 15 \, \text{nm} = 15 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{cm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{cm}$, $\rho = 10.5 \, \text{g/cm}^3$.

**(i) SA/V ratio:**

$$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{6}{d} = \frac{6}{15 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{cm}} = 4 \times 10^{7} \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

Convert to nm⁻¹: $d = 15 \, \text{nm}$ → $SA/V = 6/15 = 0.4 \, \text{nm}^{-1}$

**(ii) Number of NPs per gram:**

$$N = \frac{6}{\pi d^{3} \rho} = \frac{6}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-6})^{3} \times 10.5}$$

$$N = \frac{6}{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^{-18} \times 10.5} = \frac{6}{1.113 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 5.39 \times 10^{16} \, \text{NPs/g}}$$

**Interpretation:** 15 nm Ag-NPs offer extremely high surface area and ~54 trillion particles per gram — explaining their potent antimicrobial effect.

**(7 marks: 3 for SA/V + 4 for N)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes (Read Before KTU Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing "nanoparticles" with "nanoemulsions":** Nanoemulsions are kinetically stable liquid-liquid systems; physical NPs are solid particulate systems — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing "Richard Feynman coined nanotechnology":** He gave the vision. **Norio Taniguchi coined the term.** Marks are awarded distinctly.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion:** $1 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-9} \, \text{m} = 10^{-7} \, \text{cm}$ — forgetting the cm conversion in numerical problems is the #1 reason for zero in calculation steps.
> 4. **Forgetting K in Scherrer equation:** Always state $K = 0.9$ (shape factor) — full marks require the constant.
> 5. **Writing only "physical = top-down":** Top-down is *one* branch — bottom-up (chemical, biological) also exists. KTU expects students to mention that physical methods are mostly top-down.
> 6. **Missing the 1–100 nm definition:** Always state the size range explicitly in the first sentence of any answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Definition of Nanotechnology:** Manipulation of matter at **1–100 nm**.
- **Father of Nanotechnology (concept):** Richard Feynman (1959).
- **Coined the term:** Norio Taniguchi (1974).
- **First molecule imaged:** Xenon on nickel (STM, IBM 1989).
- **Nobel Prizes:** 1986 (STM), 1996 (Fullerenes).
- **SA/V for sphere:** $6/d$.
- **Scherrer equation:** $D = K\lambda / (\beta \cos\theta)$.
- **Top-down physical methods:** Ball milling, HPH, laser ablation, sputtering.
- **Major food applications:** Antimicrobial packaging, nano-encapsulation, sensors, smart labels.
- **Common NPs in food:** Ag, ZnO, TiO₂, SiO₂, Fe₃O₄, Fullerene, CNT.
- **E-numbers:** E171 (TiO₂), E551 (SiO₂), E172 (Iron oxide).
- **Key constant:** $1 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-9} \, \text{m} = 10 \, \text{Å}$.
- **Why NPs are special:** High SA:V + Quantum confinement.
- **Number of NPs per gram formula:** $N = 6/(\pi d^3 \rho)$.
- **KTU tip:** Always relate theory to food application in long-answer questions.
- **Examiner loves:** Chronological tables, classification diagrams, solved numericals.
- **Avoid:** Vague definitions, missing units, wrong scientist attributions.

---
**End of Module 1 Notes — PEFTT522 Nanotechnology in Food | KTU 2024 Scheme**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chemical Properties of Nanomaterials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Chemical properties of nanomaterials refer to the characteristic behaviors arising from the dramatically increased surface area-to-volume ratio, altered electronic structure, and quantum confinement effects that emerge when bulk materials are reduced to the nanoscale (typically 1–100 nm).

### The Core Chemical Characteristics

| Property | Bulk Material | Nanomaterial |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| Surface atoms (%) | < 1% | 10–60% |
| Surface energy | Low | Very high |
| Reactivity | Predictable | Size-dependent, enhanced |
| Catalytic activity | Modest | Exceptionally high |
| Chemical stability | Stable | Often reactive (oxidation prone) |

### 1.1.1 Surface Area-to-Volume Ratio (SA:V)

For a spherical particle of radius $r$:

$$SA:V = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}} = \frac{3}{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Crumpled Paper Effect":**
> Imagine a flat sheet of paper. It has only one face exposed to air. Now crumple it into a tight ball. The same paper now has hundreds of surfaces exposed. Nanoparticles are like this crumpled paper — most of their atoms are "surface atoms" with unsaturated bonds, making them chemically hungry. A 10 nm silver particle has ~**15% of its atoms on the surface**, while a 1 nm particle has ~**99% surface atoms**.

### 1.1.2 Surface Energy and Reactivity

The high surface energy ($\gamma$) leads to:

$$\Delta G_{surface} = \gamma \cdot A$$

Where $A$ is the total surface area. Nanoparticles thermodynamically seek to reduce this energy, which causes:
- **Agglomeration** (particles clump together)
- **Ostwald ripening** (smaller particles dissolve and redeposit on larger ones)
- **Surface passivation** requirement (capping agents needed)

### 1.1.3 Catalytic Properties

Nanoparticles act as superior catalysts because:
1. Large fraction of active surface sites (corners, edges, kinks)
2. Modified electronic band structure
3. Lower activation energy for reactions
4. **Example**: TiO₂ nanoparticles (< 50 nm) photocatalyze degradation of organic pollutants under UV light.

---

## 1.2 Biological Properties of Nanomaterials

### 1.2.1 Biocompatibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Biocompatibility is the ability of a nanomaterial to perform its desired function without eliciting any undesirable local or systemic effects in the biological system (cells, tissues, organisms).

Key factors affecting biocompatibility:
- **Chemical composition** (e.g., ZnO is generally safe up to certain doses)
- **Particle size** (smaller = more cellular uptake, but also more potential toxicity)
- **Shape** (rods, spheres, fibers behave differently)
- **Surface charge** (positive charges often more toxic)
- **Surface coatings** (PEGylation reduces immune response)

### 1.2.2 Antimicrobial Activity

Mechanisms by which nanomaterials kill microorganisms:

```
Nanomaterial → Microbe Interaction
        │
        ├──► Direct contact damage (membrane piercing)
        │         Example: CNTs, silver nanorods
        │
        ├──► Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generation
        │         Example: ZnO, TiO₂ (photocatalytic)
        │         Formula: TiO₂ + hν → e⁻ + h⁺
        │                  h⁺ + H₂O → •OH
        │                  e⁻ + O₂ → •O₂⁻
        │
        └──► Release of toxic ions
                  Example: Ag⁺ ions from AgNPs
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Trojan Horse":**
> A silver nanoparticle (AgNP) is like a tiny Trojan horse. Once inside a bacterial cell, it slowly releases Ag⁺ ions. These ions jam the cell's machinery — they bind to DNA, block enzyme active sites (especially thiol -SH groups), and generate reactive oxygen species. The bacterium, having welcomed the nanoparticle in, is destroyed from within.

### 1.2.3 Toxicity Considerations

The **Nanotoxicology Triangle** determines biological impact:

$$\text{Toxicity} = f(\text{Physicochemical properties}, \text{Exposure dose}, \text{Biological target})$$

Key nanoparticle toxicity parameters:
- **LD₅₀** (Lethal Dose 50%): Lowest is most toxic
- **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level)
- **ROS generation capacity**
- **Protein corona formation** (proteins adsorb on NP surface, changing identity)

---

## 1.3 Quantum Dots — Introduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Quantum Dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals (typically 2–10 nm in diameter) whose electronic and optical properties are governed by **quantum confinement** — a phenomenon where the particle becomes smaller than the exciton Bohr radius, causing discrete, size-tunable energy levels.

### 1.3.1 Core Concept of Quantum Confinement

The **exciton Bohr radius** ($a_B$) is the natural separation distance between an electron and a hole in a semiconductor:

$$a_{B} = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\hbar^{2}}{m_{e}^{*}e^{2}}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_{r}$ = relative permittivity of semiconductor
- $\hbar$ = reduced Planck's constant = **1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $m_{e}^{*}$ = effective electron mass
- $e$ = electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Particle in a Box":**
> Imagine an electron trapped in a room. In a large room (bulk material), the electron can move freely and has low energy. Now squeeze the room smaller and smaller — the electron's energy levels become quantized (like notes on a piano) and rise in energy. The smaller the "box" (nanoparticle), the higher the energy of emitted light. Change the size, change the color!

### 1.3.2 Famous Example: CdSe Quantum Dots

| Size (nm) | Emitted Color | Wavelength (nm) |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| 2.1 | Blue | ~ 480 |
| 2.5 | Green | ~ 540 |
| 3.0 | Yellow | ~ 570 |
| 4.0 | Orange | ~ 600 |
| 7.5 | Red | ~ 650 |

This is why QDs are sometimes called **"artificial atoms"** — you can tune their "color" by changing their size alone, without altering chemistry.

---

## 1.4 Why QDs Matter in Food Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Applications in Food Industry:**
> 1. **Detection of foodborne pathogens** (Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria) via fluorescence tagging
> 2. **Authenticity verification** (origin, adulteration detection in oils, milk, honey)
> 3. **Smart food packaging** with QD-based sensors
> 4. **Nutrient and pesticide residue detection**
> 5. **Encapsulation of bioactive compounds** (e.g., curcumin, vitamins)
> 6. **Shelf-life monitoring** via real-time color indicators

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Properties — Detailed Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Surface Atom Percentage Derivation Logic

For a spherical nanoparticle of radius $r$ and atomic diameter $d$:

**Step 1: Total atoms in particle**
$$N_{total} = \frac{\text{Volume of NP}}{\text{Volume per atom}} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi (d/2)^{3}} = \left(\frac{2r}{d}\right)^{3}$$

**Step 2: Surface atoms (one layer)**
$$N_{surface} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\pi (d/2)^{2}} = \left(\frac{2r}{d}\right)^{2} \cdot 4$$

**Step 3: Fraction of surface atoms**
$$f_{surface} = \frac{N_{surface}}{N_{total}} = \frac{4\left(\frac{2r}{d}\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{2r}{d}\right)^{3}} = \frac{4d}{2r} = \frac{2d}{2r} = \frac{2d}{D}$$

Wait — corrected properly:
$$f_{surface} \approx \frac{4d}{D}$$

Where $D = 2r$ is the particle diameter.

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> This explains why a **2 nm gold nanoparticle** behaves completely differently from a **20 nm gold nanoparticle** — almost half of its atoms are exposed, leading to **localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)** used in biosensors and colorimetric detection of food contaminants.

### 2.1.2 Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR)

When the nanoparticle is smaller than the wavelength of light, the conduction electrons oscillate collectively. The resonance condition is:

$$\omega_{SPR} = \sqrt{\frac{N e^{2}}{m_{eff}\varepsilon_{0}(\varepsilon_{\infty} + 2\varepsilon_{m})}}$$

Where:
- $N$ = electron density
- $m_{eff}$ = effective electron mass
- $\varepsilon_{\infty}$ = high-frequency dielectric constant of metal
- $\varepsilon_{m}$ = dielectric constant of surrounding medium

> **Food Application:** Gold nanoparticle-based LSPR biosensors detect melamine in milk at parts-per-billion levels.

---

## 2.2 Biological Properties — Detailed Mechanisms

### 2.2.1 Protein Corona Formation

When nanoparticles enter biological fluids, proteins immediately adsorb on the surface, forming two layers:

| Layer | Composition | Stability | Biological Role |
|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Hard corona** | Tightly bound proteins | Long-lived (~hours) | Defines biological identity |
| **Soft corona** | Loosely bound proteins | Rapid exchange | Determines initial interactions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** A nanoparticle's "biological identity" is determined by the corona, NOT its original surface chemistry. This is critical in food-grade nanomaterial safety assessment.

### 2.2.2 Cellular Uptake Pathways

Nanoparticles enter cells via:

```
Endocytosis Pathways
│
├──► Phagocytosis (>500 nm particles)
├──► Pinocytosis
│       ├──► Clathrin-mediated (60–120 nm)
│       ├──► Caveolae-mediated (60–80 nm)
│       └──► Macropinocytosis (>1 μm)
└──► Direct membrane penetration (<30 nm or rod-shaped)
```

Optimal size for cellular uptake: **30–200 nm**.

### 2.2.3 Antimicrobial Mechanism — Detailed

For **AgNPs** (silver nanoparticles):

1. **Attachment** to bacterial cell wall (electrostatic interaction)
2. **Penetration** and disruption of membrane integrity
3. **Release of Ag⁺ ions** (controlled release)
4. **Inhibition of enzymes** (binding to thiol -SH groups)
5. **ROS generation** causing oxidative stress
6. **DNA replication interference**

---

## 2.3 Quantum Dots — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Quantum Confinement Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Behavior |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Weak confinement** | $R > a_{B}$ | Bulk-like properties, weak size effect |
| **Intermediate** | $R \sim a_{B}$ | Modest quantization |
| **Strong confinement** | $R < a_{B}$ | Strong size-dependent optical properties |

> For QDs, **strong confinement** is the relevant regime.

### 2.3.2 Energy Levels in a Quantum Dot

The electron and hole behave as "particles in a spherical box." The total bandgap energy is:

$$E_{QD} = E_{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2R^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}}\right) - \frac{1.8 e^{2}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}R} + E_{Rydberg\ small}$$

The dominant size-dependent term is:

$$E_{confinement} = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2R^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}}\right)$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\frac{1}{R^{2}}$ dependence → smaller particle = higher energy = **blueshift** (shorter wavelength)
- Effective masses of $e^{-}$ and $h^{+}$ both matter

### 2.3.3 The "Particle in a Box" Picture

For an electron in a 1D box of length $L$:

$$E_{n} = \frac{n^{2}\pi^{2}\hbar^{2}}{2mL^{2}}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, ...$$

As $L \to$ small, energy levels become widely spaced and discrete.

> **Food Application — Fluorescence-based detection:**
> When a QD absorbs a photon, an electron jumps from valence band to conduction band, leaving behind a "hole." The recombination of e⁻-h⁺ pair (exciton) emits a photon of wavelength determined by the QD size — this is the basis of QDs as fluorescent tags in food pathogen biosensors.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Surface Atom Fraction of a Gold Nanoparticle

> **Problem:** Calculate the fraction of surface atoms in a gold nanoparticle of diameter **5 nm**, given the atomic diameter of gold is **0.288 nm**.

### Given Data
- Particle diameter, $D = 5\ \text{nm}$
- Atomic diameter of Au, $d = 0.288\ \text{nm}$
- Bulk Au lattice: FCC

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the formula**

$$f_{surface} \approx \frac{4d}{D}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$f_{surface} \approx \frac{4 \times 0.288\ \text{nm}}{5\ \text{nm}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate numerator**

$$4 \times 0.288 = 1.152$$

**Step 4: Divide by denominator**

$$f_{surface} = \frac{1.152}{5} = 0.2304$$

**Step 5: Convert to percentage**

$$f_{surface} = 23.04\%$$

### Interpretation
> For a 5 nm gold nanoparticle, **~23% of atoms are surface atoms**, compared to less than 0.1% in a 1 mm gold particle. This explains the dramatic increase in catalytic and chemical activity at nanoscale.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bohr Exciton Radius of CdSe

> **Problem:** Calculate the exciton Bohr radius of CdSe.
> Given: $\varepsilon_{r} = 9.5$, $m_{e}^{*} = 0.13 m_{0}$, $m_{h}^{*} = 0.45 m_{0}$, $m_{0} = 9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg.

### Given Data
- $\varepsilon_{r} = 9.5$
- $m_{e}^{*} = 0.13 m_{0}$
- $m_{h}^{*} = 0.45 m_{0}$
- $m_{0} = 9.1 \times 10^{-31}$ kg

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Reduced mass of exciton**

$$\mu = \frac{m_{e}^{*} \cdot m_{h}^{*}}{m_{e}^{*} + m_{h}^{*}}$$

$$\mu = \frac{(0.13)(0.45)}{0.13 + 0.45} m_{0} = \frac{0.0585}{0.58} m_{0} = 0.1008\ m_{0}$$

**Step 2: Convert reduced mass to kg**

$$\mu = 0.1008 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} = 9.18 \times 10^{-32}\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3: Apply Bohr radius formula (using reduced mass)**

$$a_{B} = \frac{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\hbar^{2}}{\mu e^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator**

$$4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 9.5 \times (1.054 \times 10^{-34})^{2}$$

$$= 4\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 9.5 \times 1.111 \times 10^{-68}$$

$$= 1.173 \times 10^{-77}$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator**

$$\mu e^{2} = 9.18 \times 10^{-32} \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^{2}$$

$$= 9.18 \times 10^{-32} \times 2.566 \times 10^{-38}$$

$$= 2.355 \times 10^{-69}$$

**Step 6: Final division**

$$a_{B} = \frac{1.173 \times 10^{-77}}{2.355 \times 10^{-69}} = 4.98 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 4.98\ \text{nm}$$

### Interpretation
> For CdSe, $a_B \approx 5\ \text{nm}$. Therefore, any CdSe nanoparticle with diameter **< 5 nm** is in the **strong quantum confinement regime** — and will display strong, size-tunable fluorescence.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandgap of a CdSe Quantum Dot

> **Problem:** Calculate the bandgap of a 3 nm CdSe quantum dot.
> Given: $E_{bulk} = 1.74\ \text{eV}$, $m_{e}^{*} = 0.13 m_{0}$, $m_{h}^{*} = 0.45 m_{0}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Confinement energy term**

$$E_{conf} = \frac{\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}}{2R^{2}}\left(\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Convert units** — Use $R = 1.5\ \text{nm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$

**Step 3: Compute the $\frac{1}{m} + \frac{1}{m}$ term**

$$\frac{1}{m_{e}^{*}} + \frac{1}{m_{h}^{*}} = \frac{1}{0.13 m_{0}} + \frac{1}{0.45 m_{0}} = \frac{1}{m_{0}}\left(\frac{1}{0.13} + \frac{1}{0.45}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{m_{0}}(7.69 + 2.22) = \frac{9.91}{m_{0}}$$

$$= \frac{9.91}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} = 1.089 \times 10^{31}\ \text{kg}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\hbar^{2}\pi^{2}$ term**

$$\hbar^{2}\pi^{2} = (1.054 \times 10^{-34})^{2} \times (3.1416)^{2}$$

$$= 1.111 \times 10^{-68} \times 9.87 = 1.096 \times 10^{-67}\ \text{J}^{2}\text{s}^{2}$$

**Step 5: Compute $2R^{2}$ term**

$$2R^{2} = 2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2} = 2 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-18} = 4.5 \times 10^{-18}\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 6: Combine**

$$E_{conf} = \frac{1.096 \times 10^{-67} \times 1.089 \times 10^{31}}{4.5 \times 10^{-18}}$$

$$= \frac{1.193 \times 10^{-36}}{4.5 \times 10^{-18}} = 2.65 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

**Step 7: Convert to eV**

$$E_{conf} = \frac{2.65 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.654\ \text{eV}$$

**Step 8: Total bandgap**

$$E_{QD} = E_{bulk} + E_{conf} = 1.74 + 1.65 = 3.39\ \text{eV}$$

### Interpretation
> A 3 nm CdSe QD has a bandgap of **3.39 eV**, corresponding to emission wavelength:
> $$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{1240\ \text{eV·nm}}{3.39\ \text{eV}} = 366\ \text{nm (UV)}$$
> A larger 5 nm QD would have a smaller bandgap (~2.4 eV), emitting **green** (~520 nm).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Nanomaterials vs Bulk

| Property | Bulk Material (100 µm) | Nanomaterial (10 nm) | Quantum Dot (3 nm) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Surface/Volume ratio | ~ 0.01 nm⁻¹ | ~ 300 nm⁻¹ | ~ 1000 nm⁻¹ |
| % Surface atoms | < 0.01% | ~ 10% | ~ 30% |
| Bandgap | Fixed | Slightly altered | Strongly size-dependent |
| Optical behavior | Reflects/scatters | Plasmonic effects | Size-tunable fluorescence |
| Melting point | Fixed | Reduced | Strongly reduced |
| Reactivity | Predictable | Enhanced | Highly tunable |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Emission Wavelength of a CdTe QD

> **Problem:** A CdTe quantum dot has a bandgap of 2.4 eV. Calculate the wavelength of emitted light and identify the color.

**Solution:**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

**Numerator:**
$$hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.988 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$$

**Energy in joules:**
$$E = 2.4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.845 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$$

**Wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{3.845 \times 10^{-19}} = 5.17 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} = 517\ \text{nm}$$

**Color:** 517 nm = **Green light** ✓
