> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nanoforms of Carbon. Environmental Toxicology of Engineered Nanoparticles. Monitoring Nanoparticles - Risk Governance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Nanoforms of Carbon — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Nanoforms of carbon* are allotropic configurations of elemental carbon (atomic number **Z = 6**) in which at least one external dimension lies in the **1–100 nm** range, exhibiting quantum confinement, high surface-area-to-volume ratio (SA:V), and altered sp²/sp³ hybridization compared to bulk graphite or diamond.

The principal nanoforms recognized in food nanotechnology and environmental toxicology are:

| Nanoform | Symbol | Hybridization | Typical Dimension | Year of Discovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fullerene (Buckminsterfullerene) | C₆₀ | sp² | Ø ≈ **0.72 nm** | 1985 (Kroto, Curl, Smalley) |
| Single-Walled Carbon Nanotube (SWCNT) | Cₙ | sp² | Ø ≈ 0.4–2 nm, L ≈ µm | 1991 (Iijima) |
| Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotube (MWCNT) | Cₙ | sp² | Ø ≈ 2–100 nm | 1991 (Iijima) |
| Graphene / Graphene Oxide (GO) | Cₙ | sp² | Single atomic layer ≈ **0.34 nm** | 2004 (Geim, Novoselov) |
| Carbon Nanodots (CNDs) | Cₙ | sp²/sp³ | Ø ≈ 1–10 nm | 2004 (Sun et al.) |
| Nanodiamonds | Cₙ | sp³ | Ø ≈ 4–10 nm | 1960s (Soviet Union) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **2024 KTU syllabus** explicitly emphasizes the role of these nanoforms as **food contact materials (FCMs)**, **nano-encapsulants for nutraceuticals**, and **emerging contaminants** in the food chain.

### 1.2 Environmental Toxicology of Engineered Nanoparticles (ENPs) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Environmental nanotoxicology is the scientific study of the **adverse effects** of intentionally manufactured (engineered) nanomaterials on ecological receptors — bacteria, algae, plants, invertebrates, vertebrates, and ultimately humans — across **air, water, soil, and food matrices**.

### 1.3 Monitoring & Risk Governance — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Nanoparticle monitoring* is the systematic detection, characterization, and quantification of ENPs in environmental and biological matrices. *Risk Governance* is the integrated, multi-stakeholder process of **identifying, assessing, managing, and communicating** nanotechnological risks under regulatory frameworks (e.g., **FDA, EFSA, EPA, REACH, OECD**).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

- **C₆₀ Fullerene** is like a **miniature soccer ball** (truncated icosahedron) made of 60 carbon atoms — so small that 10 million of them could fit across the width of a single human hair (~80 µm).

- **Carbon Nanotubes** can be imagined as a **single sheet of graphene rolled into a seamless straw** — if you unrolled it, you would have a one-atom-thick sheet of chicken wire.

- **Engineered Nanoparticles in the environment** behave like **invisible pollutants**: just as microplastics have infiltrated marine ecosystems, ENPs are the *next-generation invisible contaminants*, and traditional water filters cannot catch them.

- **Risk Governance** is analogous to the **air traffic control system** of nanotechnology — multiple agencies (FDA, EPA, EFSA) must coordinate continuously to prevent "collisions" between innovation and public/environmental safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant used throughout:** The **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$ governs the Brownian motion of nanoparticles in environmental media.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of Carbon Nanoforms

The "why" of carbon's nanoforms lies in its unique **electron configuration** $[He]2s^22p^2$ which permits **sp, sp², and sp³** hybridization, giving rise to vastly different geometries:

1. **Diamond** → sp³ → tetrahedral 3D lattice → hardest natural material.
2. **Graphite** → sp² → stacked 2D hexagonal sheets → lubricant.
3. **Fullerene C₆₀** → sp² → closed cage (truncated icosahedron) → 20 hexagons + 12 pentagons following **Euler's formula** for polyhedra:

$$\begin{aligned}
V - E + F &= 2 \\
60 - 90 + 32 &= 2 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces.

4. **CNT** → sp² → graphene cylinder → "rolled" chirality vector $(n,m)$ governs metallic/semiconducting behaviour.

5. **Graphene** → sp² → single 2D sheet → *Dirac cone* electronic structure yielding anomalous quantum Hall effect.

### 2.2 Surface Area to Volume Ratio — The Toxicological Master Key

As particle size shrinks, **surface area scales with $r^2$** but **volume scales with $r^3$**. Therefore:

$$\text{SA:V} \;=\; \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}} \;=\; \frac{3}{r}$$

For a **spherical** ENP of radius $r$ (in nm), SA:V is in **nm⁻¹**. This ratio dictates:
- **Reactivity** (more surface = more catalytic sites)
- **ROS generation** (electron-donor/acceptor surface states)
- **Protein corona formation** in biological fluids

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SA:V} = 3/r$ | Sphere SA:V ratio | nm⁻¹ | Predicts reactivity of ENP |
| 2 | $D_p = k_B T / (3\pi \eta d)$ | Stokes–Einstein diffusivity | m² s⁻¹ | Predicts NP mobility in water |
| 3 | $\eta = \eta_0 (1 + 2.5\phi)$ | Einstein viscosity (dilute) | Pa·s | Predicts NP suspension stability |
| 4 | $BCF = C_{org} / C_{water}$ | Bioconcentration factor | L kg⁻¹ | Aquatic toxicology |
| 5 | $EC_{50} = $ conc. giving 50% effect | Effective concentration | mg L⁻¹ | Dose–response testing |
| 6 | $LD_{50}$ | Lethal dose 50% | mg kg⁻¹ body wt | Acute oral toxicity |
| 7 | $K_{ow} = P_{oct}/P_{wat}$ | Octanol-water partition | dimensionless | Predicts bioaccumulation |
| 8 | $HNP = n \cdot 4\pi r^2$ | Total surface atoms of NP | atoms | Catalytic site estimation |
| 9 | $E_{bandgap} \propto 1/r^2$ | Quantum confinement | eV | Explains CND fluorescence |
| 10 | $H = \sum (P \times C)$ | Risk = Probability × Consequence | score | Risk governance matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> *Avoid using the vertical pipe symbol* — the table above uses standard variables only. For absolute value of a parameter, render as $\lvert x \rvert$ in $\LaTeX$.

### 2.4 Toxicology Mechanism — The "Why"

Engineered nanoparticles exert toxicity through **four primary pathways**:

1. **ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) Generation** → oxidative stress → lipid peroxidation → DNA damage.
2. **Membrane Disruption** → physical piercing (especially for CNTs, analogous to asbestos).
3. **Trojan Horse Effect** → nanoparticles smuggle bound toxicants (PAHs, heavy metals) across biological barriers.
4. **Protein Corona Modification** → misfolding, immunogenic recognition, altered biodistribution.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Packaging**: GO-coated films improve **oxygen barrier** by 90% and tensile strength.
- **Nutraceutical Delivery**: C₆₀ derivatives carry **curcumin, resveratrol, omega-3** with enhanced bioavailability.
- **Sensors**: CNDs act as **fluorescent probes** for detecting pesticide residues at ppb levels.
- **Risk Governance**: SA:V drives **EU CLP hazard classification** thresholds for ENPs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: SA:V Ratio for a Spherical Nanoparticle

> **Goal:** Prove that SA:V of a sphere is inversely proportional to its radius.

**Step 1 — Surface area of a sphere:**

$$A = 4\pi r^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of a sphere:**

$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common terms** ($4\pi r^{2}$ cancels from numerator and denominator):

$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{1}{\tfrac{1}{3}r} = \frac{3}{r}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{A}{V} = \frac{3}{r}\;}$$

> *Engineering interpretation:* Halving the radius **doubles** the surface reactivity per unit mass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stokes–Einstein Diffusivity of a Nanoparticle in Water

> **Goal:** Derive the diffusion coefficient $D_p$ of a spherical ENP in a Newtonian fluid.

**Step 1 — Start with the Einstein relation** (Brownian motion):

$$D_p = \frac{k_B T}{f}$$

where $f$ is the **friction coefficient**.

**Step 2 — For a sphere moving at low Reynolds number, Stokes' law gives:**

$$f = 3\pi \eta d$$

where $d = 2r$ is the hydrodynamic diameter, $\eta$ the dynamic viscosity.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Einstein relation:**

$$D_p = \frac{k_B T}{3\pi \eta d}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for a 50 nm TiO₂ NP in water at 25 °C:**

Given:
- $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$
- $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- $\eta_{water} = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $d = 50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$

$$\begin{aligned}
D_p &= \frac{(1.380649 \times 10^{-23})(298.15)}{3\pi (8.9 \times 10^{-4})(50 \times 10^{-9})} \\
&= \frac{4.1164 \times 10^{-21}}{4.1943 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&\approx 9.81 \times 10^{-12} \;\; \text{m}^{2}\,\text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** A 50 nm particle diffuses ≈ **10⁵× slower** than a small molecule (D ≈ 10⁻⁶ m²/s), explaining its persistence in the water column.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Quantum Confinement in Carbon Nanodots (CNDs)

> **Goal:** Show that bandgap energy $E_g$ increases as particle size $r$ decreases.

**Step 1 — The "particle-in-a-box" effective mass approximation** gives:

$$E_g(r) = E_g^{bulk} + \frac{h^{2}}{8 \mu r^{2}}$$

where:
- $E_g^{bulk}$ = bulk graphite bandgap ≈ 0 eV (zero-gap semiconductor)
- $h$ = Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- $\mu$ = reduced effective mass of electron–hole pair (≈ $0.05 m_0$ for graphene-derived CNDs)
- $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
- $r$ = radius of CND

**Step 2 — For a 2 nm CND:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{h^{2}}{8 \mu r^{2}} &= \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^{2}}{8 \times (0.05)(9.109 \times 10^{-31}) \times (2 \times 10^{-9})^{2}} \\
&= \frac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{1.458 \times 10^{-48}} \\
&= 3.01 \times 10^{-19} \;\; \text{J} \\
&= 1.88 \;\; \text{eV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final bandgap:**

$$E_g(2\text{ nm}) = 0 + 1.88 \approx 1.88 \text{ eV}$$

> *Why this matters:* This corresponds to **blue fluorescence (~660 nm emission from ~410 nm excitation)**, the basis for using CNDs as food-quality sensors.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Toxicity of Carbon Nanoforms

| Nanoform | ROS Generation | Membrane Damage | Asbestos-like Fibrosis | Biopersistence | EU Regulatory Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C₆₀ Fullerene | **Moderate** | Low | None | High (poorly excreted) | REACH registered |
| SWCNT | **High** | **Severe** | **Yes** (needle-like) | Very high | SVHC candidate |
| MWCNT | High | Severe | **Yes** | Very high | REACH restricted |
| Graphene/GO | Moderate | Moderate | Plate-like, not fibrous | Moderate | Under review |
| CNDs | Low | Negligible | None | Low | Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS pending) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Risk Score Calculation

Given a hypothetical **TiO₂ NP** (E171 food-grade) with:
- Probability of human exposure $P = 0.7$ (frequent dietary intake)
- Consequence severity $C = 8$ (out of 10)

Compute the **risk score** $H$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= P \times C \\
&= 0.7 \times 8 \\
&= 5.6
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision rule (KTU/EU paradigm):**

| $H$ range | Action |
|---|---|
| 0 – 2 | Acceptable — routine monitoring |
| 2 – 5 | Tolerable — mitigation needed |
| 5 – 8 | Unacceptable — substitution required |
| 8 – 10 | Intolerable — immediate ban |

**Conclusion:** $H = 5.6$ → falls in the **"Unacceptable"** band; substitution with bulk TiO₂ or non-nano alternatives (e.g., calcium carbonate) is recommended.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Briefly explain the structure of C₆₀ fullerene and highlight two of its potential applications in food systems."**

**Model Answer:**

C₆₀ fullerene (Buckminsterfullerene) is a **truncated icosahedron** composed of **60 sp²-hybridized carbon atoms** arranged in **20 hexagons and 12 pentagons**, with a molecular diameter of **0.72 nm** and a hollow cage. It satisfies **Euler's polyhedral formula** $V - E + F = 2$ (60 - 90 + 32 = 2).

**Two food applications:**
1. **Antioxidant carrier** — its conjugated π-system scavenges free radicals, useful in functional foods.
2. **Encapsulation of hydrophobic nutraceuticals** (e.g., curcumin, CoQ10) to enhance aqueous solubility and bioavailability.

---

#### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Define Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) and state its significance in evaluating nanoparticle ecotoxicity."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** is the ratio of the steady-state concentration of a substance in an organism to that in the surrounding water:

$$BCF = \frac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}} \;\; [\text{L kg}^{-1}]$$

**Significance for nanotoxicology:**
- A **BCF > 2000 L kg⁻¹** signals high bioaccumulation potential, triggering regulatory concern under **REACH** and **EU CLP**.
- For metallic ENPs (Ag, TiO₂, ZnO), BCF is often determined by tracking the **ionic dissolution** plus the **nanoparticulate fraction** via **sp-ICP-MS**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Choice Option 1]

**(a)** Describe the classification, structure, and unique properties of three major nanoforms of carbon. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the environmental toxicology of engineered nanoparticles with special reference to ROS generation, the *Trojan Horse effect*, and trophic transfer. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to Question A(a):**

The three major nanoforms are:

1. **Fullerene (C₆₀)**
   - **Structure:** truncated icosahedron, 0.72 nm diameter, sp²-hybridized.
   - **Properties:** high electron affinity, antioxidant capacity, photostability.
   - **Derivation of carbon atom count:** Each hexagon contributes 1 atom and each pentagon contributes 1 atom; sharing reduces to **60 atoms**.

2. **Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNT and MWCNT)**
   - **Structure:** graphene sheet rolled into a cylinder; SWCNT has single wall (Ø 0.4–2 nm), MWCNT has 2–50 concentric walls (Ø 2–100 nm).
   - **Chirality vector** $\vec{C}_h = n\vec{a}_1 + m\vec{a}_2$ determines electronic type:
     - If $(n-m)$ is a multiple of 3 → **metallic**.
     - Otherwise → **semiconducting**.

3. **Graphene / Graphene Oxide (GO)**
   - **Structure:** single-atom-thick 2D sheet of $sp^2$ carbon in a honeycomb lattice, 0.34 nm thick.
   - **Properties:** exceptional mechanical strength (Young's modulus ≈ 1 TPa), very high electrical conductivity, large theoretical SA ≈ **2630 m²/g**.

**Mark-allocating points (model answer rubric):**
- Correct identification of hybridization — 2 marks.
- Geometry + dimensions — 2 marks.
- Two property highlights per form — 3 marks.

---

**Solution to Question A(b):**

**Environmental toxicology of ENPs — three pillars:**

1. **ROS Generation (Reactive Oxygen Species)**
   - ENPs possess **large surface area : volume** and surface defect sites that catalyze formation of $\cdot OH$, $\cdot O_2^-$, $H_2O_2$.
   - The smaller the $r$, the higher the $\text{SA:V} = 3/r$, the greater the ROS yield.
   - ROS attack lipid bilayers → **lipid peroxidation** (MDA biomarker rises), DNA strands (8-OHdG adducts), and proteins (carbonyl formation).

2. **Trojan Horse Effect**
   - Engineered nanoparticles (e.g., **C₆₀, CNT**) act as **carriers** that adsorb hydrophobic co-contaminants (PAHs, PCBs, DDT) on their surface.
   - They ferry these toxicants across **biological membranes** that would normally exclude them.
   - Example: Fullerene–PAH complexes enhance **planar aromatic uptake** by *Daphnia magna* by 5–10×.

3. **Trophic Transfer (Bioaccumulation & Biomagnification)**
   - Algae $\to$ zooplankton $\to$ fish $\to$ human.
   - **Biomagnification factor (BMF)** = $C_{predator}/C_{prey}$.
   - For TiO₂ ENPs, BMF ≈ 1–3 across trophic levels; for **ionic Ag released from Ag-NPs**, BMF can exceed **100×** in aquatic food webs.

**Mark-allocating points (model answer rubric):**
- Three mechanisms named and explained — 4 marks.
- Quantitative examples (BCF, BMF, SA:V) — 2 marks.
- Food-chain illustration — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Choice Option 2]

**(a)** Explain the principles and instrumentation of four major techniques used to monitor engineered nanoparticles in food and environmental matrices. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Outline the Risk Governance framework for engineered nanomaterials, citing the roles of FDA, EFSA, REACH, and OECD. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to Question B(a):**

| # | Technique | Principle | Detection Limit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TEM** | Electron beam transmission through ultra-thin sample | sub-nm | Size, shape, lattice |
| 2 | **SEM** | Secondary electron emission from coated surface | ≈ 1 nm | Morphology, EDS composition |
| 3 | **DLS** | Brownian velocity via Doppler-shifted light scattering | 0.3 nm – 10 µm | Hydrodynamic radius |
| 4 | **sp-ICP-MS** | Single-particle ion detection in plasma | sub-ppt | Number concentration, dissolved vs particulate |
| 5 | **AFM** | Cantilever tip scans surface, atomic resolution | sub-nm | Topography, height |
| 6 | **FTIR / Raman** | Vibrational fingerprint of molecular bonds | µg/mL | Surface chemistry, functionalization |

**Key point for KTU valuation:** sp-ICP-MS is the **gold standard** for differentiating **dissolved vs nanoparticulate** metal species — examiners expect explicit mention of "single-particle mode + frequency of events → particle number concentration."

**Mark-allocating points:**
- Technique + principle + matrix — 5 marks (≈ 1.25 each for 4 techniques).
- Comparative table / detection limit — 1 mark.
- Application to food — 1 mark.

---

**Solution to Question B(b):**

**Risk Governance — Multi-Tier Framework:**

The four regulatory pillars are:

1. **FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration)**
   - Governs **food contact substances (FCS)** under 21 CFR.
   - Issues **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** notices.
   - 2014 FDA guidance: "Considering Whether an FDA-Regulated Product Involves the Application of Nanotechnology."

2. **EFSA (European Food Safety Authority)**
   - 2011 Scientific Opinion on "Guidance on the risk assessment of the application of nanoscience and nanotechnologies in the food and feed chain."
   - Mandates **toxicological testing** of ENPs separately from bulk forms.

3. **REACH (Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals)**
   - **ECHA (European Chemicals Agency)** requires nanoforms to be **registered separately** from bulk (2018 Annex revision).
   - Substances of Very High Concern (**SVHC**) listing possible for hazardous CNTs.

4. **OECD Working Party on Manufactured Nanomaterials (WPMN)**
   - Coordinates international testing of 13 representative ENPs.
   - Publishes **Test Guidelines (TG)** for nano-toxicology.

**Integrated Risk Governance Lifecycle (8 steps):**

1. Problem formulation → 2. Hazard identification → 3. Exposure assessment → 4. Dose–response analysis → 5. Risk characterization → 6. Risk management → 7. Risk communication → 8. Monitoring & review (loops back to step 1).

**Mark-allocating points:**
- 4 agencies identified with mandate — 4 marks.
- 8-step lifecycle correctly listed — 2 marks.
- Critical insight (e.g., separate nano-registration) — 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing nanoforms with allotropes:** Students often interchange "allotrope" (bulk) with "nanoform" (size-defined). Examiners deduct **½–1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting Euler's formula for C₆₀:** When asked to justify the 60-atom count, merely stating "C₆₀ has 60 atoms" without invoking $V - E + F = 2$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "|x|" inside a table:** KTU digital evaluation systems can mis-render markdown tables if vertical pipes appear in math cells. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.
> 4. **Mixing "Trojan Horse effect" with simple uptake:** The mechanism is specifically about **co-transport of adsorbed toxicants** — not just ENP uptake alone.
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical problems:** Always write "$D_p = 9.81 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2\,\text{s}^{-1}$" — bare numbers fetch **partial** credit only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **A. Nanoforms of Carbon**
> - [ ] C₆₀ = truncated icosahedron, Ø 0.72 nm, satisfies Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2$.
> - [ ] SWCNT (single wall) vs MWCNT (multi-wall); chirality $(n,m)$ governs metallic/semiconducting behaviour.
> - [ ] Graphene = 2D honeycomb, 0.34 nm thick, sp², theoretical SA ≈ 2630 m²/g.
> - [ ] CNDs = 1–10 nm, quantum-confined, exhibit size-tunable fluorescence.
> - [ ] Nanodiamonds = sp³, used in bio-imaging, ~4–10 nm.

> **B. Environmental Toxicology of ENPs**
> - [ ] SA:V = $3/r$ drives reactivity; smaller = more toxic (per unit mass).
> - [ ] Four pathways: ROS, membrane damage, Trojan Horse, protein corona.
> - [ ] BCF, BMF, and trophic transfer define ecological persistence.
> - [ ] Dissolution (ionic release) is critical for Ag-NPs, ZnO-NPs.

> **C. Monitoring Nanoparticles**
> - [ ] sp-ICP-MS = gold standard for number concentration; sub-ppt LOD.
> - [ ] DLS gives hydrodynamic radius (not true size).
> - [ ] TEM/SEM = morphology, lattice.
> - [ ] Sample preparation (digestion, filtration) is the **rate-limiting step**.

> **D. Risk Governance**
> - [ ] FDA — GRAS + 2014 Nano-Guidance.
> - [ ] EFSA — 2011 Scientific Opinion on food/feed nano-applications.
> - [ ] REACH (ECHA) — separate nano-registration since 2018.
> - [ ] OECD WPMN — 13 reference nanomaterials, test guidelines.
> - [ ] 8-step lifecycle: problem formulation → monitoring & review.

> **E. Key Numbers to Memorize**
> - [ ] $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$.
> - [ ] Planck $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$.
> - [ ] Avogadro $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.
> - [ ] SA:V for 1 nm sphere = **3 nm⁻¹**.
> - [ ] E171 (food-grade TiO₂) contains up to 36% nanoparticles by number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
