<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Why is an ultra-thin specimen (≤ 100 nm) mandatory in TEM, whereas SEM does not impose this requirement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In TEM, the image is formed by **electrons transmitted through** the specimen. If the specimen is too thick, electrons undergo excessive multiple inelastic and elastic scattering, resulting in:
1. Loss of spatial coherence and degraded resolution.
2. Inability of the objective lens to focus a sharp image due to chromatic aberration.
3. Severe beam damage and contamination buildup.

In SEM, the image is formed by **secondary or backscattered electrons emitted from the top ~1–5 nm of the surface**, so only the surface needs to be conductive and accessible — the bulk thickness is irrelevant (1 mark for statement, 2 marks for the three consequences).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Distinguish between Bright Field (BF) and Dark Field (DF) imaging modes in TEM. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bright Field | Dark Field |
|---|---|---|
| Aperture selection | Only the direct (undiffracted) beam | One or more diffracted beams |
| Image appearance | Dense/atomic-number-heavy regions = **dark**; thin/light regions = **bright** | Diffracting crystallites = **bright**; matrix = **dark** |
| Contrast origin | Mass-thickness + diffraction | Diffraction (crystallinity) |
| Typical use | Morphological imaging | Locating crystalline phases, precipitates |

(1½ marks for the contrast mechanism, 1½ marks for the typical use case.)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength relation for electrons accelerated through potential $V$, and compute the wavelength of electrons accelerated at **200 kV**. State why the relativistic correction is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the total relativistic energy: $E_{total} = \gamma m_e c^2 = m_e c^2 + eV$, giving $\gamma = 1 + \dfrac{eV}{m_e c^2}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** From $E^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$, derive $p = \dfrac{m_e c}{\lambda}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Combine to obtain:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting $V = 200{,}000$ V, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s:

$$eV = 3.204 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}, \quad m_e c^2 = 8.187 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

$$\gamma = 1 + 0.3914 = 1.3914$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 (9.109 \times 10^{-31})(3.204 \times 10^{-14})(1.3914)}}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{8.12 \times 10^{-44}}} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{2.85 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.0251 \; \text{Å}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Relativistic correction is essential because, at 200 kV, the electron velocity $v \approx 0.7c$. The non-relativistic formula underestimates $\lambda$ by ~28%, leading to incorrect resolution and Scherzer-defocus calculations. The relativistic mass increase lowers the momentum and lengthens the wavelength.

---

**(b)** Explain the principle of **HRTEM imaging**, with a clear definition of **Scherzer defocus** and the **Contrast Transfer Function (CTF)**. State the importance of HRTEM in characterizing food-grade nanomaterials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**HRTEM Principle (2 marks):** HRTEM is a phase-contrast imaging mode in which the objective lens is defocused to the Scherzer condition so that a broad range of spatial frequencies is transferred with a near-uniform phase. Multiple diffracted beams interfere to produce **lattice fringes** with spacing equal to the interplanar distance $d_{hkl}$ of the crystal.

**Scherzer Defocus (2 marks):** The defocus value $\Delta f_{Sch} = -1.2 \sqrt{C_s \lambda}$ at which the CTF passes through a broad plateau of negative sign with the first zero at the highest interpretable spatial frequency. It defines the optimum compromise between contrast and resolution.

**CTF Definition (1 mark):** $\text{PCTF}(u) = \sin \chi(u) = \sin \left( \pi \Delta f \lambda u^2 + \dfrac{\pi}{2} C_s \lambda^3 u^4 \right)$; it dictates how object spatial frequencies map into image contrast.

**Importance in Food Nanomaterials (2 marks):**
- Atomic-scale confirmation of crystalline additives (e.g., TiO₂ E171, SiO₂ E551).
- Distinguishing amorphous vs. crystalline states of biopolymer nanocrystals (CNC, starch nanocrystals).
- Validation of lattice spacing of nano-encapsulated bioactives (e.g., curcumin polymorphs, quercetin nanocrystals).
- Critical for regulatory submissions under EFSA/FSSAI to demonstrate the absence of unintended crystalline impurities.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope** with a neat labelled block diagram. Explain the role of the objective lens as the "heart of the microscope." **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Electron gun:** FEG or LaB₆ thermionic emitter; provides coherent electron source.
2. **Condenser system:** C1 and C2 lenses with condenser aperture; demagnify the source and produce a parallel beam.
3. **Specimen stage:** Eucentric side-entry goniometer with ± 70° tilt; allows precise positioning in ultra-high vacuum.
4. **Objective lens:** Most critical lens; forms first intermediate image and the diffraction pattern in its back focal plane.
5. **Intermediate and projector lenses:** Magnify the image (or diffraction pattern) up to ~10⁶×.
6. **Detectors:** Phosphor screen for direct viewing; CCD/CMOS or direct electron detector for digital recording.
7. **Vacuum system:** Ion + turbomolecular pumps maintain ~10⁻⁷ Pa.

**Role of Objective Lens — "Heart of the TEM" (3 marks):**
- Forms the **first magnified image** that all subsequent lenses only further enlarge.
- Generates the **electron diffraction pattern** in its back focal plane.
- Its aberrations (spherical $C_s$, chromatic $C_c$) directly limit the **point resolution** of the entire instrument.
- The objective aperture inserted at its back focal plane controls BF/DF contrast and limits beam convergence.
- Defocus of the objective lens (e.g., Scherzer defocus) is the primary HRTEM tuning parameter.

---

**(b)** Discuss **sample preparation techniques** for TEM analysis of food and biological nanomaterials. Why is heavy-metal staining required? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sample Preparation Steps (5 marks):**
1. **Fixation:** Glutaraldehyde (2.5%) cross-links proteins and preserves native nanostructure.
2. **Dehydration:** Graded ethanol series (30% → 100%) removes water.
3. **Infiltration & Embedding:** Resin (e.g., Spurr's, LR White) penetrates the specimen; thermal polymerization at 60 °C for 48 h.
4. **Ultramicrotomy:** Diamond knife cuts 60–90 nm sections; sections floated onto carbon-coated Cu grids.
5. **Staining:** Uranyl acetate (3%) + lead citrate (Reynolds' stain) added for ~5–10 min.

**Heavy-Metal Staining — Why Required? (2 marks):**
- Biological specimens (proteins, lipids, polysaccharides) are composed of light elements (C, H, O, N) which **scatter electrons weakly**, producing poor inherent contrast.
- Heavy metals (U, Pb) deposit selectively on proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids, increasing **mass-thickness contrast** by enhancing elastic scattering.
- The metal stains preferentially bind to certain functional groups (uranyl → phosphate and carboxyl; lead → glycogen and basic proteins), enabling **selective visualization** of sub-cellular and nano-structures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the relativistic correction** at ≥ 100 kV is the most common 2-mark loss. Always state the exact voltage.
> 2. **Confusing HRTEM with SEM** — TEM is *transmission*; SEM is *surface scanning*. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this.
> 3. **Mismeasuring the objective lens role** — never describe it as "just a lens"; emphasize that it forms the first image AND the diffraction pattern.
> 4. **Missing units** in formula derivations (especially Å vs. nm) loses 1 mark per instance.
> 5. **In Part A**, do not exceed 60–80 words; board examiners penalize padding that doesn't add to the answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TEM = Transmission electron microscopy** → image from electrons *transmitted through* ultra-thin (≤ 100 nm) specimen.
- **Resolution advantage:** Electron wavelength at 200 kV ≈ **0.0251 Å** → ~10⁵× smaller than visible light.
- **Always use the relativistic wavelength formula** for ≥ 100 kV: $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_e eV(1 + eV/2m_ec^2)}$.
- **Objective lens** = heart of TEM; forms the first image and diffraction pattern; its $C_s$ limits resolution.
- **HRTEM** = phase-contrast imaging; uses **Scherzer defocus** $\Delta f = -1.2\sqrt{C_s \lambda}$ for optimum contrast.
- **Scherzer resolution** = $0.66 \, C_s^{1/4} \lambda^{3/4}$ — the smallest directly interpretable lattice spacing.
- **Bragg's Law** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ governs HRTEM lattice-fringe formation.
- **Specimen requirements:** ≤ 100 nm thickness, vacuum-stable, electron-transparent.
- **Sample prep for food/biological samples:** Fixation → dehydration → resin embedding → ultramicrotomy → heavy-metal staining (uranyl acetate + lead citrate).
- **Application pillars in food nanotechnology:** Encapsulation verification, crystal-phase ID, nano-additive QC, nanoplastic detection, biopolymer nanocrystal characterization.
- **BF vs DF:** BF uses direct beam (mass-thickness contrast); DF uses a diffracted beam (crystallinity contrast).
- **Vacuum** is essential (~10⁻⁷ Pa) to prevent electron-gas scattering and specimen contamination.
- **CTF** $\sin \chi(u)$ governs which spatial frequencies are transferred with interpretable contrast.
- **Aberration-corrected TEMs** (with $C_s$ correctors) can reach sub-50 pm resolution.
- **KTU numerical recall:** 200 kV → 0.0251 Å; 300 kV → 0.0197 Å; 100 kV → 0.0370 Å.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Analysis Techniques - AFM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Analysis Techniques - AFM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

**Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution type of **scanning probe microscopy (SPM)** that uses a sharp physical probe (tip) to mechanically scan a sample's surface and generate topographical maps at the nanometer (and even sub-nanometer) scale. It works by measuring the interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, etc.) between the tip and the sample as the tip raster-scans across the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AFM was invented in 1986 by Gerd Binnig, Calvin Quate, and Christoph Gerber** as a successor to the Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM). Unlike STM, AFM can image **non-conductive samples** (like biological tissues, food polymers, and insulating materials), making it indispensable in food nanotechnology.

---

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Nano Phonograph"

Imagine you are blindfolded in a dark room and asked to map the shape of a record player disc. You are given a thin needle attached to a spring. As you drag the needle across the disc, the spring deflects up and down following the grooves. A laser and a light sensor measure how much the spring is bent. By recording those tiny up-and-down movements while moving the needle systematically, you reconstruct the entire 3D surface of the disc.

**AFM works exactly the same way:**
- The **needle** = a nanometer-sharp tip on a flexible cantilever.
- The **spring** = the microfabricated silicon cantilever (spring constant ~ 0.01 to 50 N/m).
- The **laser + sensor** = a photodiode detector that measures cantilever deflection.
- The **systematic movement** = a piezoelectric scanner that moves the tip across x, y, and z with sub-Ångström precision.

In food nanotechnology, AFM allows us to "feel" the surface of starch granules, protein aggregates, lipid bilayers, bacterial cell walls, and nano-encapsulated food ingredients in their **native, hydrated state** — something electron microscopes cannot do without harsh dehydration.

---

### Key Standard Metrics & Constants in AFM

- **Cantilever spring constant (k):** typically **0.01 – 50 N/m**
- **Tip radius of curvature:** typically **2 – 30 nm** (sharp tips < 5 nm)
- **Lateral resolution:** **~ 1 – 10 nm**
- **Vertical (z) resolution:** **< 0.1 nm** (sub-Ångström)
- **Scan range:** typically **~ 100 µm × 100 µm × 10 µm**
- **Interatomic force range measured:** **~ 10⁻¹¹ N to 10⁻⁶ N** (pN to µN)
- **Young's Modulus of silicon cantilever:** **E ≈ 130 – 185 GPa**
- **Hooke's Law constant of proportionality:** defined by the cantilever's geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AFM matters in Food Science:** It is the *only* technique that can image food biopolymers (e.g., amylose chains, casein micelles, gluten networks) at nanometer resolution *under ambient or aqueous conditions*, preserving their native structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle: The Force Loop

AFM operates on a closed feedback loop:

1. The **piezoelectric scanner** moves the tip (or sample) in x, y, z with sub-nanometer precision.
2. As the tip approaches the surface, **interatomic forces** (attractive van der Waals at long range, repulsive Pauli exclusion at short range) cause the cantilever to bend.
3. A **laser beam** reflected off the back of the cantilever strikes a **position-sensitive photodetector (PSPD)**. The position of the laser spot indicates cantilever deflection.
4. The PSPD signal is fed back to a **PID controller** that adjusts the z-piezo to keep the deflection (and hence force) constant.
5. The z-piezo voltage needed to maintain constant force is recorded at each (x, y) point and converted into a **3D topographical image**.

---

## 2.2 The Force–Distance Curve (F–Z Curve)

The fundamental measurement in AFM is the **force–distance (F–Z) curve**, which plots cantilever deflection (∝ force) versus tip-sample separation.

**Regimes of the F–Z curve:**

- **Long range (Z >> R):** Zero force — tip is in free air.
- **Approach (A → B):** Attractive **van der Waals** force pulls the tip down. Snap-in may occur (jump-to-contact) if gradient of attractive force exceeds the cantilever spring constant.
- **Repulsive regime (B → C):** The tip pushes into the **Pauli exclusion / ionic repulsion zone**. The cantilever bends upward; force is repulsive. This is the **contact mode** regime.
- **Retract (C → D → E):** The tip is pulled back. Adhesion (capillary, meniscus, chemical) holds the tip to the surface, producing a **pull-off (snap-out) point** that quantifies adhesion force.
- **Free (E → F):** The tip detaches; the cantilever returns to zero deflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pull-off force measured on retract is used to calculate **adhesion energy** and **work of adhesion** between the tip and the food matrix — a critical parameter in food texture and stability studies.

---

## 2.3 Modes of AFM Operation

### (A) Contact Mode
- Tip is in **constant repulsive contact** with the surface (repulsive regime, force ~ 1 – 10 nN).
- Highest lateral resolution; fastest scanning.
- **Disadvantage:** Can damage soft samples (e.g., protein gels, bacterial cells, lipid bilayers).
- **Used for:** Hard food materials like crystalline sugar, starch granules, mineral additives.

### (B) Non-Contact Mode
- Tip oscillates **above** the surface (separation ~ 1 – 10 nm) at its resonant frequency.
- Senses **long-range van der Waals / electrostatic forces** that shift resonant frequency or amplitude.
- Force applied to sample is negligible (pN level); suitable for very soft samples.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower lateral resolution; requires ultra-clean surfaces (capillary forces from adsorbed water layer disrupt signal).

### (C) Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact Mode)
- Tip oscillates at resonance, **intermittently tapping** the surface.
- Amplitude is maintained constant by feedback loop.
- Combines high resolution of contact mode with non-destructive nature of non-contact mode.
- **Most widely used mode in food nanotechnology** for imaging biopolymers, microbial cells, and emulsions.

> [!NOTE]
> In tapping mode, the **phase signal** (lag between driving oscillation and cantilever response) provides information about **viscoelasticity, adhesion, and stiffness** — perfect for differentiating soft protein matrices from hard crystalline regions in food.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Symbols & Units | Application in Food Nanotech |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's Law (cantilever) | $F = -k \cdot \Delta x$ | F: force (N); k: spring constant (N/m); Δx: deflection (m) | Converts cantilever deflection to interatomic force |
| 2 | Cantilever spring constant (rectangular) | $k = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$ | E: Young's modulus (Pa); w: width (m); t: thickness (m); L: length (m) | Design of microfabricated Si/Si₃N₄ cantilevers |
| 3 | Resonant frequency (cantilever) | $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$ | k: spring constant (N/m); m*: effective mass (kg) | Determines oscillation frequency in tapping / non-contact mode |
| 4 | Lennard-Jones Potential | $U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$ | ε: potential well depth (J); σ: zero-crossing distance (m); r: separation (m) | Models tip–sample interatomic potential |
| 5 | Van der Waals force (sphere-flat) | $F_{vdW} = -\dfrac{H \cdot R}{6 \cdot d^{2}}$ | H: Hamaker constant (J); R: tip radius (m); d: tip-sample distance (m) | Calculates long-range attractive force |
| 6 | Quality factor (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{f_{0}}{\Delta f}$ | f₀: resonant frequency; Δf: FWHM bandwidth (Hz) | Indicates energy dissipation in liquid imaging |
| 7 | Force gradient sensitivity | $\Delta f \approx -\dfrac{f_{0}}{2k} \cdot \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | ∂F/∂z: force gradient (N/m) | Detects tip–sample force gradient in NC-AFM |
| 8 | Work of adhesion (pull-off) | $W = \dfrac{F_{adh}}{1.5 \pi R}$ | F_adh: pull-off force (N); R: tip radius (m) | Quantifies adhesion in food interfaces |
| 9 | Piezo displacement resolution | $\Delta z = V \cdot d_{33}$ | V: applied voltage (V); d₃₃: piezo coefficient (m/V) | Scanner positioning accuracy |
| 10 | Mean roughness (R_a) | $R_{a} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_{i} - \bar{z} \vert$ | z_i: height at point i; $\bar{z}$: mean height (m) | Quantitative surface roughness of food coatings |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food

- **Food Packaging:** Mapping the nano-roughness of biodegradable nanocomposite films (e.g., chitosan + nanoclay) to evaluate gas barrier performance.
- **Edible Coatings:** Measuring adhesion force between coating layers and fruit surfaces.
- **Nano-Encapsulation:** Imaging the shape, size, and stiffness of nanoliposomes, nanoemulsions, and biopolymer nanoparticles loaded with bioactive compounds (curcumin, vitamins, omega-3).
- **Food Safety:** Visualizing bacterial cell wall morphology, biofilm architecture, and antimicrobial nanoparticle interactions.
- **Food Texture:** Nano-mechanical mapping (PeakForce QNM, Force Volume) of starch granules, gluten networks, and dairy protein matrices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AFM is the only technique that provides simultaneous topographical, mechanical, adhesive, and electrical data on a single food sample — without staining, coating, or vacuum.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Spring Constant from Beam Mechanics

A standard AFM cantilever is modeled as a **rectangular beam** with one end fixed and the other free. By **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**, the static deflection δ at the free end under a point load F applied at the tip is:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where I is the area moment of inertia of the rectangular cross-section:

$$I = \frac{w \cdot t^{3}}{12}$$

Substituting I into the deflection equation:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{w \cdot t^{3}}{12}\right)}$$

Simplifying the denominator (multiplying numerator and denominator by 12):

$$\delta = \frac{12 \cdot F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}} = \frac{4 \cdot F \cdot L^{3}}{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}$$

By definition of the spring constant, $k = F / \delta$. Inverting the above expression:

$$\boxed{\,k = \frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The spring constant scales **linearly with Young's modulus (E)** and **width (w)**, but **cubically with thickness (t)** and **inversely cubically with length (L)**. This is why soft cantilevers (low k) are made **long and thin**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonant Frequency of the Cantilever

A cantilever behaves as a **simple harmonic oscillator** with effective mass m* and spring constant k. The equation of motion is:

$$m^{*} \cdot \ddot{x} + k \cdot x = 0$$

Assuming a harmonic solution $x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the second derivative is $\ddot{x} = -\omega^{2} x$. Substituting:

$$m^{*}(-\omega^{2} x) + k x = 0$$

$$(k - m^{*}\omega^{2})x = 0$$

For non-trivial solutions ($x \neq 0$):

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{k}{m^{*}}$$

$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m^{*}}}$$

Converting angular frequency to ordinary frequency ($\omega = 2\pi f$):

$$2\pi f_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m^{*}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_{0} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m^{*}}}\,}$$

**In terms of material & geometry** (for rectangular cantilever, $m^{*} \approx 0.24 \rho L w t$):

$$f_{0} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 L^{3} \cdot (0.24 \rho L w t)}} = \frac{t}{2\pi L^{2}} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \rho}}$$

This shows the resonant frequency is governed primarily by **thickness-to-length-squared ratio** and the **ratio of Young's modulus to density**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lennard-Jones Force from the Potential

The Lennard-Jones potential models the interatomic interaction:

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$

The interatomic force is the negative gradient of potential:

$$F(r) = -\frac{dU}{dr}$$

Differentiating term by term:

$$\frac{dU}{dr} = 4\varepsilon \left[ 12 \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}}\right) \cdot (-1) - 6 \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}\right) \cdot (-1) \right]$$

$$\frac{dU}{dr} = 4\varepsilon \left[ -\frac{12\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + \frac{6\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$F(r) = -\frac{dU}{dr} = 4\varepsilon \left[ \frac{12\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - \frac{6\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$$

$$\boxed{\,F(r) = \frac{24\varepsilon}{r}\left[ 2\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]\,}$$

**Equilibrium separation** (F = 0):

$$2\left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{0}}\right)^{12} = \left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{0}}\right)^{6}$$

$$r_{0} = 2^{1/6} \cdot \sigma \approx 1.122\,\sigma$$

**Minimum potential depth:**

$$U(r_{0}) = -\varepsilon$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean Surface Roughness (R_a) for a Food Film

For a 2D topographical image with N sampled points along a line, with measured heights $z_i$ and mean height $\bar{z}$:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_{i}$$

The mean roughness (R_a) is the arithmetic average of absolute deviations:

$$\boxed{\,R_{a} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_{i} - \bar{z} \vert\,}$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):** A nano-coated food film was scanned and the following 5 height values (in nm) were recorded: $z_1 = 12$, $z_2 = 15$, $z_3 = 10$, $z_4 = 18$, $z_5 = 14$.

Step 1 — Mean height:

$$\bar{z} = \frac{12 + 15 + 10 + 18 + 14}{5} = \frac{69}{5} = 13.8 \text{ nm}$$

Step 2 — Absolute deviations:

$$\vert 12 - 13.8 \vert = 1.8,\quad \vert 15 - 13.8 \vert = 1.2,\quad \vert 10 - 13.8 \vert = 3.8,\quad \vert 18 - 13.8 \vert = 4.2,\quad \vert 14 - 13.8 \vert = 0.2$$

Step 3 — Mean roughness:

$$R_{a} = \frac{1.8 + 1.2 + 3.8 + 4.2 + 0.2}{5} = \frac{11.2}{5} = 2.24 \text{ nm}$$

**Engineering meaning:** A food packaging film with $R_a < 5$ nm is considered **ultra-smooth**, indicating a homogeneous nanocomposite dispersion (low agglomeration of nanoclay).

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Pull-off Adhesion Force on a Dairy Protein

A colloidal AFM probe (tip radius R = 20 nm) was retracted from a casein micelle surface. The pull-off (snap-out) force recorded was $F_{adh} = 2.5$ nN. Calculate the **work of adhesion** (W).

Using the JKR/DMT-based formula (for small, hard tip — DMT limit):

$$W = \frac{F_{adh}}{1.5 \pi R}$$

Substituting:

$$W = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N}}{1.5 \pi \times 20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$W = \frac{2.5}{1.5 \pi \times 20} \text{ J/m}^{2} = \frac{2.5}{94.25} \text{ J/m}^{2}$$

$$W \approx 0.0265 \text{ J/m}^{2} = 26.5 \text{ mJ/m}^{2}$$

**Interpretation:** A work of adhesion ~ 26 mJ/m² is typical for protein–hydrophobic tip interactions, indicating moderate adhesion — useful for understanding **casein stabilization in milk products**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AFM Modes

| Property | Contact Mode | Non-Contact Mode | Tapping Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tip-sample distance | < 0.5 nm | 1 – 10 nm | 0.5 – 2 nm |
| Force regime | Repulsive (nN) | Attractive (pN) | Intermittent (nN–pN) |
| Resolution | High lateral | Lower lateral | High lateral |
| Sample damage | High (shear, friction) | None | Low |
| Imaging in liquid | Possible | Difficult | Excellent |
| Viscoelastic data | Limited | No | **Yes (phase imaging)** |
| Best for food samples | Hard sugar, starch | Atomically flat surfaces | **Biopolymers, bacteria, emulsions** |
| Cantilever stiffness needed | Low k (0.01 – 0.5 N/m) | High k (20 – 50 N/m) | Medium k (1 – 10 N/m) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AFM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU[PSU POWER SUPPLY] --> CTRL[FEEDBACK CONTROLLER PID]
    PIEZO[PIEZOELECTRIC XYZ SCANNER] --> STAGE[SAMPLE STAGE HOLDER]
    STAGE --> SAMP[FOOD SAMPLE BIOFILM POLYMER]
    TIP[AFM TIP 2 TO 30 NM] --> CANT[MICROFABRICATED CANTILEVER]
    CANT --> SAMP
    LASER[LASER DIODE 650 NM] --> CANT
    CANT --> PSPD[POSITION SENSITIVE PHOTODETECTOR]
    PSPD --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PIEZO
    CTRL --> DATA[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM]
    DATA --> MON[TOPOGRAPHY MAP 3D RENDER]
    DATA --> FZ[FORCE DISTANCE CURVE]
    DATA --> PHASE[PHASE IMAGE VISCOELASTIC]
    OSC[OSCILLATOR DRIVE] --> CANT
    OSC --> CTRL
    USER[OPERATOR SOFTWARE] --> CTRL
    USER --> MON

    classDef hw fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow of information is a **closed feedback loop**. The PSPD senses cantilever deflection → the controller computes error signal → the piezo adjusts z-height → the new cantilever position is sensed again. The data acquisition system simultaneously constructs topography, force–distance curves, and phase images from the recorded signals.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AFM. List its three modes of operation. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | CO2 | Bloom: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution scanning probe technique that uses a sharp tip (radius ~ 2 – 30 nm) on a flexible cantilever to map the topography of a sample surface by measuring interatomic forces between the tip and the sample. Resolution: lateral ~ 1 – 10 nm; vertical < 0.1 nm.
>
> **Three modes of operation:**
> 1. **Contact Mode** — tip remains in continuous repulsive contact with the surface.
> 2. **Non-Contact Mode** — tip oscillates above the surface, sensing long-range van der Waals forces.
> 3. **Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact)** — tip oscillates at resonance and intermittently taps the surface; widely used for soft food samples.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the piezoelectric scanner in AFM? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | CO2 | Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoelectric scanner** is a ceramic actuator (typically lead zirconate titanate, PZT) that produces precise, controlled mechanical displacement in response to an applied voltage (via the inverse piezoelectric effect). It performs three critical functions in AFM:

1. **X–Y raster scanning** — moves the tip (or sample) in the horizontal plane with sub-nanometer precision to scan across the surface.
2. **Z-axis positioning** — adjusts the vertical distance to maintain a constant tip–sample force (regulated by the feedback loop).
3. **Atomic-scale resolution** — provides displacements as small as 0.01 nm, enabling sub-Ångström vertical resolution.

Mathematically, the displacement is $\Delta z = V \cdot d_{33}$, where $d_{33}$ is the piezo coefficient (~ 300 – 600 pm/V for PZT).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of Atomic Force Microscopy** with a labeled block diagram. Describe how a force–distance curve is obtained and interpreted. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** Derive the **spring constant of a rectangular cantilever** from Euler–Bernoulli beam theory. A silicon cantilever has the following dimensions: $L = 200\ \mu m$, $w = 40\ \mu m$, $t = 1\ \mu m$. Given $E = 169$ GPa, calculate its spring constant. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | CO2 | Bloom: Apply / Analyze]**

**Model Solution (a):**

The AFM operates on the principle of measuring **interatomic forces** between a sharp tip and the sample surface. The major components are:

- **Cantilever with sharp tip** — the force sensor.
- **Piezoelectric scanner** — provides nanometer-precision XYZ motion.
- **Laser and Position-Sensitive Photodetector (PSPD)** — detects cantilever deflection optically.
- **Feedback controller (PID)** — maintains constant force by adjusting piezo Z-voltage.

**Procedure:**
1. The tip is brought close to the sample surface.
2. As the tip approaches, interatomic forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, repulsive) cause the cantilever to deflect.
3. A laser beam is reflected from the back of the cantilever onto the PSPD; cantilever bending shifts the laser spot.
4. The PSPD voltage difference is converted to deflection (using Hooke's Law, F = kΔx), then to force.
5. The feedback loop adjusts the Z-piezo to keep the force constant during X–Y rastering. The Z-piezo voltage is recorded to generate a **3D topographic image**.

**Force–Distance Curve Interpretation:**
- **Approach portion (A→B→C):** Attractive van der Waals regime (B), snap-in (jump to contact) due to surface forces exceeding cantilever stiffness, followed by repulsive regime (C) where tip pushes into electron cloud.
- **Retract portion (C→D→E):** Adhesion (capillary, chemical bonds) holds tip; maximum pull-off force (D) quantifies adhesion energy. At snap-out (E), tip detaches.
- The pull-off force is used to calculate **work of adhesion** in food interfaces.

**Model Solution (b):**

From Euler–Bernoulli beam theory, the static deflection δ of a rectangular cantilever under point load F at the free end is:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 E I},\quad I = \frac{w t^{3}}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$k = \frac{(169 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (40 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4 \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}$$

$$k = \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \cdot 40 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{-18}}{4 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$k = \frac{6.76 \times 10^{-13}}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.0211 \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{\,k \approx 0.021 \text{ N/m}\,}$$

This low spring constant classifies the cantilever as a **soft contact-mode lever**, suitable for imaging soft food biopolymers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three modes of AFM operation** — contact, non-contact, and tapping. Compare their suitability for food samples. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** What is the **Lennard-Jones potential**? Derive the **interatomic force** from it and find the **equilibrium separation** $r_0$. Given $\varepsilon = 0.05$ eV and $\sigma = 0.34$ nm (typical for tip–food biopolymer interaction), calculate the minimum potential energy in joules. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | CO2 | Bloom: Apply / Analyze]**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Mode | Tip–Sample State | Force Range | Best Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Continuous repulsive contact | 1 – 10 nN | Hard crystalline food (sugar, starch granules) |
| **Non-Contact** | Oscillating above surface | ~ pN | Atomically flat surfaces; rarely used in food |
| **Tapping** | Intermittent tapping at resonance | nN–pN | **Soft food biopolymers, bacteria, emulsions, biofilm** |

**Contact Mode:** The tip is dragged across the surface; the cantilever deflection is held constant by feedback. High lateral resolution but exerts lateral (shear) forces that can damage soft samples.

**Non-Contact Mode:** The cantilever is oscillated ~ 1 – 10 nm above the surface. Tip–sample force gradient shifts the resonant frequency or amplitude; the feedback loop maintains this shift constant. The tip never touches the surface, so there is no damage — but lateral resolution is lower and the technique is highly sensitive to contamination.

**Tapping Mode:** The cantilever oscillates near resonance with large amplitude (~ 20 – 100 nm). At the bottom of each oscillation cycle, the tip briefly contacts the surface, "tapping" it. The amplitude is monitored by the PSPD. Because vertical forces dominate, lateral (frictional) forces are eliminated, and the **phase lag** between the driving signal and cantilever response reveals viscoelastic/mechanical contrast. **This is the preferred mode for food nanotechnology.**

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Lennard-Jones (LJ) potential** is a pairwise potential modeling van der Waals attraction ($1/r^{6}$) and short-range Pauli repulsion ($1/r^{12}$) between two atoms:

$$U(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$

**Derivation of interatomic force:**

$$F(r) = -\frac{dU}{dr} = \frac{24\varepsilon}{r} \left[ 2\left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$

**Equilibrium separation** is found by setting F = 0:

$$2\left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{0}}\right)^{12} = \left(\frac{\sigma}{r_{0}}\right)^{6} \;\Rightarrow\; r_{0} = 2^{1/6}\,\sigma$$

$$r_{0} = 2^{1/6} \cdot 0.34 \text{ nm} \approx 1.122 \cdot 0.34 \approx 0.381 \text{ nm}$$

**Minimum potential energy** at $r_0$:

$$U(r_{0}) = -\varepsilon$$

Convert to joules: $1$ eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J

$$U_{\min} = -0.05 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = -8.01 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} \approx -8 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{\,U_{\min} \approx -8.01 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J}\,}$$

This energy scale (~ tens of $k_B T$ at room temperature) is typical for weak physisorption in food biopolymers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make on AFM questions (costing 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing AFM with STM** — AFM does NOT require conductive samples; do not write "needs conductive coating."
> 2. **Forgetting the cantilever spring constant derivation** — examiners specifically look for $I = w t^3 / 12$ and the substitution step.
> 3. **Mixing up modes** — non-contact mode does NOT touch the surface; tapping mode intermittently contacts it. Many students swap the two.
> 4. **Skipping the snap-in / snap-out explanation** in the F–Z curve — these features are mandatory for full marks on the 7-mark question.
> 5. **Not converting units** — $1\ \text{GPa} = 10^{9}\ \text{Pa}$, $1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$. A correct formula with wrong unit conversion loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the equilibrium condition** in LJ derivation — examiners expect the explicit $2(\sigma/r)^{12} = (\sigma/r)^{6}$ step.
> 7. **Writing "AFM uses electron beam"** — WRONG. AFM uses a mechanical probe, not an electron beam (that is SEM/TEM). This error alone can cost full marks on definition questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AFM (1986, Binnig–Quate–Gerber)** = high-resolution scanning probe technique using a sharp tip on a cantilever to measure interatomic forces and produce nanometer-scale topography.
- **Core equation:** $F = -k \Delta x$ (Hooke's Law for cantilever).
- **Cantilever spring constant (rectangular):** $k = E w t^{3} / (4 L^{3})$.
- **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{k / m^{*}}$.
- **Lennard-Jones potential:** $U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}]$; equilibrium $r_0 = 2^{1/6}\sigma$; minimum $U = -\varepsilon$.
- **Van der Waals force (sphere–flat):** $F_{vdW} = -H R / (6 d^{2})$.
- **Work of adhesion:** $W = F_{adh} / (1.5 \pi R)$.
- **Three modes:** Contact (repulsive, nN, hard samples); Non-Contact (attractive, pN, very flat surfaces); Tapping (intermittent, soft food biopolymers).
- **Force–distance curve features:** snap-in (jump to contact), repulsive regime, pull-off (snap-out) → measures adhesion.
- **Components to remember:** cantilever, tip, piezoelectric scanner, laser, PSPD, feedback controller, data acquisition.
- **Food applications:** nano-roughness of packaging films, imaging of nanoliposomes, biopolymers, bacterial cell walls, biofilm analysis, mechanical mapping of starch and protein matrices.
- **AFM advantage over SEM/TEM:** works in air, vacuum, **and liquid**; no coating required; preserves native hydrated state of food samples.
- **Resolution:** lateral ~ 1 – 10 nm; vertical < 0.1 nm.
- **Tip radius:** typically 2 – 30 nm; sharper = higher resolution but more fragile.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scanning Near-field Optical Microscopy (SNOM / NSOM)**
> SNOM is a high-resolution scanning probe microscopy technique that uses a sharp, sub-wavelength aperture tip (or apertureless metallic tip) placed in the **optical near-field** of a sample — at a distance much smaller than the wavelength of the illuminating light — to generate images with spatial resolution that **defies the classical Abbe diffraction limit** of conventional far-field optical microscopy.

The technique exploits the **evanescent (near-field) electromagnetic components** that exist only within a few nanometres of a sample surface and decay exponentially with distance. By raster-scanning this near-field probe across the specimen, an optical map of surfaces, nanostructures, biomolecules, and food-related nanomaterials can be reconstructed with lateral resolutions typically in the range of **20–100 nm** — far superior to the **~200 nm** diffraction barrier of standard optical microscopy.

| Parameter | Conventional Optical Microscopy | SNOM (Near-Field) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating regime | Far-field | Near-field (evanescent) |
| Resolution | λ / 2 ≈ **200 nm** | **~20–100 nm** |
| Probe-sample distance | ≫ λ | ≪ λ (typically < 10 nm) |
| Information carrier | Propagating waves | Evanescent (non-propagating) waves |
| Sample environment | Often vacuum-free | Often ambient / aqueous |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Hearing a Whisper in a Loud Stadium"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture for B.Tech Students**
> Imagine trying to listen to a friend whispering in a packed cricket stadium during a match. If you stand far away in the stands (this is the *far-field*), the cheering crowd drowns the whisper, and you can only hear coarse sounds — just like a standard optical microscope only resolves features down to ~200 nm.
>
> However, if you walk right up next to your friend and place your ear within a few centimetres of their mouth (this is the *near-field*, distance ≪ wavelength), you can hear the whisper clearly — even distinguishing individual words. SNOM works exactly like this: a tiny fibre-optic or metal-coated tip is brought within nanometres of the sample to "hear" the *evanescent whispers* of light that carry the finest nanoscale details.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Board Examinations**
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - **Refractive index of glass fibre (SiO₂):** $n \approx 1.46$
> - **Visible wavelength range:** $\lambda \approx 400$–$700$ nm
> - **Abbe diffraction limit (visible):** $d_{min} \approx \lambda / 2 \approx 200$ nm
> - **SNOM tip aperture diameter:** $d_{ap} \approx 50$–$100$ nm
> - **Typical probe-sample gap:** $z \approx 2$–$10$ nm
> - **Near-field decay length:** $\ell_{d} \approx 10$–$50$ nm
> - **Lateral SNOM resolution:** $\Delta x \approx 20$–$50$ nm

## 1.4 Classification of SNOM Techniques (KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Principal SNOM Modalities**
> 1. **Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM / PSTM):** A metal-coated tapered fibre-optic tip with a sub-wavelength aperture delivers and/or collects light.
> 2. **Apertureless SNOM (s-SNOM / Scattering-type SNOM):** A sharp metallic tip (e.g., Au, Pt-Ir) acts as a local scattering centre, enhancing the near-field interaction with the sample.

In food nanotechnology, both modalities are used to study nanostructured food matrices, biopolymer nanofibres, nanolaminates, and nanocapsule distributions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — The Diffraction Barrier

In conventional (far-field) optical microscopy, the smallest resolvable feature $d_{min}$ is set by Abbe's diffraction limit:

$$d_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, n \sin\theta} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, \mathrm{NA}}$$

where $\mathrm{NA}$ is the numerical aperture of the objective lens. For visible light ($\lambda \approx 550$ nm) with a high-NA oil-immersion objective ($\mathrm{NA} = 1.4$), the limit is still $\approx 200$ nm — too coarse to image individual nanostructures used in food science (e.g., engineered nanoliposomes, $\beta$-lactoglobulin fibrils, nanoemulsion droplets).

**Why SNOM Breaks the Limit:**
- Far-field optics only collects *propagating* electromagnetic waves.
- *Evanescent* (non-propagating) waves carry the sub-wavelength information but decay exponentially within $\sim \lambda/10$ of the surface.
- A sharp probe placed within this decay zone converts evanescent information into propagating waves that can be detected, effectively bypassing the Abbe barrier.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Aperture SNOM (Step-by-Step)

1. **Laser light** is coupled into a single-mode optical fibre.
2. The fibre is tapered (heated and pulled) to a sharp tip of diameter $\sim 50$–$100$ nm.
3. The sidewalls of the tapered region are coated with **aluminium (Al)** to confine light.
4. The metal cladding forms a sub-wavelength **aperture** at the apex — the only optical exit/entry point.
5. The tip is brought within $z \approx 2$–$10$ nm of the sample (regulated by shear-force or tapping-mode AFM feedback).
6. Evanescent fields at the aperture interact with the sample, and the transmitted/reflected/scattered light is collected by a photodetector.
7. The tip is raster-scanned via a piezoelectric stage; the photodetector signal is mapped as a function of $(x, y)$ to form the SNOM image.

## 2.3 Operational Logic of Apertureless (s-SNOM)

- A sharp **metallic tip** (Au, Pt-Ir) oscillating at frequency $\Omega$ illuminates the sample.
- The tip acts as an **antenna**, locally enhancing the field by orders of magnitude ("lightning-rod effect").
- The non-linear near-field interaction generates harmonics at $n\Omega$; **demodulation at higher harmonics** (typically $n \geq 2$) isolates the near-field signal from the far-field background.
- Resolution is determined by the **tip apex radius** ($r_{tip} \approx 10$–$30$ nm), not the wavelength.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All High-Yield Equations for SNOM (Use these in board answers)**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\,\mathrm{NA}}$ | Abbe diffraction limit | Sets the floor for far-field resolution |
| 2 | $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2z/\ell_d}$ | Evanescent intensity decay vs tip-sample gap $z$ | Defines the required tip proximity |
| 3 | $\ell_d \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi \, n_1 \, \sqrt{\sin^2\theta_c - \sin^2\theta}}$ | Decay length of evanescent wave | Determines probe-sample working distance |
| 4 | $T \approx \left(\dfrac{d_{ap}}{\lambda}\right)^{4}$ | Bethe–Bouwkamp transmission of a sub-wavelength aperture | Power-throughput bottleneck of a-SNOM |
| 5 | $P_{trans} \approx \dfrac{64}{27\pi^{2}} \, k^{4} \, a^{6} \, I_0$ | Power through a circular aperture of radius $a$ | Predicts extremely low signal levels |
| 6 | $\Delta f_{shear} = -\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{2k_{s}} \langle F_{ts}^{2} \rangle$ | Shear-force feedback shift (a-SNOM) | Used for tip-sample distance regulation |
| 7 | $E_{loc} \approx E_0 \cdot \dfrac{r_{tip}}{(r_{tip} + z)^{2}}$ | Local field enhancement at s-SNOM tip | Controls signal strength in apertureless mode |
| 8 | $S_{nf}(\Omega_n) = \int I_{nf}(t) \, e^{-in\Omega t} \, dt$ | nth-harmonic demodulated near-field signal | Isolation of near-field from background |

> **Note on notation:** All magnitudes use **SI units**. Aperture diameter $d_{ap}$, wavelength $\lambda$, refractive index $n_1$, and tip radius $r_{tip}$ must be substituted in metres for numerical evaluation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Nanotechnology

- **Nanoliposome & nanoemulsion imaging:** Maps size distribution and surface coverage of lipid nanocarriers used for curcumin, vitamin D, and $\beta$-carotene encapsulation.
- **Biopolymer nanofibre characterisation:** Visualises individual protein fibrils (e.g., amyloid-like fibrils from whey protein) used as texture modifiers.
- **Active packaging inspection:** Probes distribution of nano-Ag or nano-TiO₂ antimicrobial coatings on polymer films.
- **Quality control in nanoencapsulated flavours:** Verifies the homogeneity of delivery systems at sub-100 nm scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Abbe Diffraction Limit (Far-Field Optical Resolution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the minimum resolvable distance $d_{min}$ between two point sources using a far-field lens of numerical aperture NA.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**
Consider two incoherent point emitters separated by a distance $d$ on the sample plane. Light from each point is collected by an objective lens and focused to form an **Airy disc** in the image plane. The first zero of the Airy pattern occurs at an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the lens aperture diameter.

**Step 2 — Rayleigh criterion.**
Two points are just resolved when the central maximum of one Airy disc coincides with the first minimum of the other. The corresponding spatial separation in the image plane is:

$$r_{Rayleigh} = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\mathrm{NA}}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to object-plane resolution.**
Using the magnification $M$ of the optical system, the smallest resolvable *object* distance is:

$$d_{min} = \frac{r_{Rayleigh}}{M} = \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{M \cdot \mathrm{NA}}$$

**Step 4 — Approximation for high-NA, unit-magnification limit.**
For a microscope objective working at near-unit magnification, the textbook Abbe form becomes:

$$d_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{2\,\mathrm{NA}}$$

> **Numerical check:** For green laser ($\lambda = 550$ nm) and oil-immersion objective ($\mathrm{NA} = 1.4$):

$$d_{min} = \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1.4} \approx 1.96 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \approx 200 \, \text{nm}$$

This **~200 nm** barrier is precisely what SNOM overcomes.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Bethe–Bouwkamp Transmission Through a Sub-Wavelength Aperture

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Show why the optical throughput of an a-SNOM aperture is so small — the "SNOM photon budget" problem.

**Step 1 — Boundary value problem statement.**
A perfectly conducting metal screen of negligible thickness contains a circular aperture of radius $a$, illuminated by a normally incident plane wave of amplitude $E_0$ and free-space wavenumber $k = 2\pi/\lambda$, with $ka \ll 1$.

**Step 2 — Static (Bethe) approximation.**
In the quasi-static limit ($ka \rightarrow 0$), the electric field in the aperture polarises the metal walls, producing equivalent magnetic dipole moments. Bethe (1944) showed the transmitted power scales as:

$$P_{trans} = \frac{64}{27\pi^{2}} \, k^{4} \, a^{6} \, I_{0}$$

where $I_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_0 E_0^{2}$ is the incident intensity.

**Step 3 — Recast in terms of $d_{ap} = 2a$ and $\lambda$.**
Substituting $a = d_{ap}/2$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{trans} &= \frac{64}{27\pi^{2}} \left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\right)^{4} \left(\frac{d_{ap}}{2}\right)^{6} I_0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{64}{27\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{16\pi^{4}}{\lambda^{4}} \cdot \frac{d_{ap}^{6}}{64} \cdot I_0 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi^{2}}{27} \cdot \frac{d_{ap}^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \cdot I_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput ratio $T = P_{trans} / (I_0 \cdot A)$ where $A = \pi a^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{\pi^{2}}{27} \cdot \frac{d_{ap}^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi a^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi^{2}}{27} \cdot \frac{(2a)^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi a^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi^{2}}{27} \cdot \frac{64 \, a^{6}}{\lambda^{4}} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi a^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{64 \pi}{27} \cdot \frac{a^{4}}{\lambda^{4}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{64 \pi}{27} \cdot \left(\frac{d_{ap}}{2\lambda}\right)^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering approximation.**
The full Bethe result is often written in the compact scaling form:

$$T \approx C \left(\frac{d_{ap}}{\lambda}\right)^{4}, \qquad C \approx 0.26 \text{–} 1$$

**Step 6 — Numerical check (a-SNOM working point).**
For $d_{ap} = 80$ nm and $\lambda = 488$ nm (blue Ar⁺ laser):

$$T \approx 0.5 \times \left(\frac{80}{488}\right)^{4} \approx 0.5 \times (0.164)^{4} \approx 0.5 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

That is, only **~0.04%** of the incident light passes through the aperture — a key design constraint for a-SNOM instrumentation and a favourite KTU examiner question.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Evanescent Field Decay (Near-Field Working Distance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Quantify the tip-sample gap $z$ required for the evanescent signal to be detectable.

**Step 1 — Total internal reflection geometry.**
At a dielectric interface, when light is incident beyond the critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$, the transmitted wave becomes evanescent with field:

$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-k_z z}$$

where $k_z$ is the imaginary component of the wavevector normal to the interface:

$$k_z = k_0 \, n_1 \, \sqrt{\sin^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta_c}$$

**Step 2 — Intensity form.**
Since intensity is proportional to the square of the field amplitude:

$$I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2 k_z z} = I_0 \, e^{-z / \ell_d}, \qquad \ell_d = \frac{1}{2 k_z}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution.**

$$\ell_d = \frac{1}{2 k_0 \, n_1 \, \sqrt{\sin^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta_c}} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi \, n_1 \, \sqrt{\sin^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta_c}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check (glass–air interface, $\theta = 60°$, $n_1 = 1.5$, $\lambda = 632.8$ nm He–Ne).**
First, $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/1.5) = 41.8°$. Then:

$$\ell_d = \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}}{4\pi (1.5) \sqrt{\sin^{2} 60° - \sin^{2} 41.8°}} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \approx 112 \, \text{nm}$$

This justifies the **~10 nm tip–sample working distance** rule of thumb: at $z \approx \ell_d$, the signal drops to $1/e \approx 37\%$ of its peak value.

## 3.4 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At-a-Glance Comparison Table for KTU Board Answers**

| Aspect | Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM) | Apertureless SNOM (s-SNOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Probe | Tapered Al-coated fibre | Bare Au / Pt-Ir tip |
| Resolution | $\sim d_{ap} \approx 50$–$100$ nm | $\sim r_{tip} \approx 10$–$30$ nm |
| Throughput | $T \propto (d_{ap}/\lambda)^4$ — very low | No aperture loss; field-enhanced |
| Distance control | Shear-force / tapping AFM | Tapping-mode AFM at $\Omega$ |
| Signal extraction | Direct photodetection | Higher-harmonic demodulation $n\Omega$ |
| Best for food science | Fluorescence of nanocarriers | Mid-IR chemical mapping of packaging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
