<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note (2024 Scheme):** Part A tests direct recall and single-concept application. Answers should be **30–50 words** with one supporting equation or diagram.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Q:** Define **Scanning Near-field Optical Microscopy (SNOM)**. State any two advantages it offers over conventional optical microscopy in food nanostructure analysis.

**Model Answer:**
SNOM is a scanning probe technique that uses a sharp tip (aperture or apertureless) placed in the **optical near-field** — within nanometres of the sample — to capture evanescent light and produce images with **lateral resolution of 20–100 nm**, far better than the Abbe limit of ~200 nm.
**Advantages:**
1. **Sub-diffraction resolution** — can resolve individual nanoliposomes (~50 nm) used in food encapsulation, which are invisible to ordinary light microscopes.
2. **Simultaneous optical + topographic imaging** — correlates chemical contrast (fluorescence, IR absorption) with surface morphology of nanolaminates and nano-coatings.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** What is an **evanescent wave**? Why is the probe–sample distance critical in SNOM?

**Model Answer:**
An evanescent wave is a **non-propagating electromagnetic field** that exists within ~$\lambda/10$ of an interface when total internal reflection occurs. Its amplitude decays as $E(z) = E_0 e^{-k_z z}$, with a characteristic decay length $\ell_d \approx 10$–$50$ nm.
The probe–sample distance in SNOM must be **smaller than $\ell_d$** (typically $z \approx 2$–$10$ nm). If the tip moves farther away, the evanescent field becomes negligible and the sub-wavelength information is lost — so the Abbe diffraction limit reappears.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**
**Q:** (a) Describe the working principle of **Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM)** with a neat labelled block diagram. Compare its resolution with that of a standard far-field optical microscope.
**(b) Using the Bethe–Bouwkamp relation, derive the expression for the optical transmission of a sub-wavelength aperture and explain why a-SNOM has an extremely low photon throughput.

### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle and Resolution Comparison (7 Marks):**

1. **Light delivery (2 marks):** Laser light ($\lambda \approx 488$–$633$ nm) is coupled into a single-mode optical fibre, which is heated and pulled to form a sharp tapered tip of apex diameter $\sim 50$–$100$ nm. The tapered sidewalls are coated with **aluminium (~100 nm thick)** to confine light, leaving only a sub-wavelength aperture at the apex.

2. **Near-field interaction (2 marks):** The tip is brought to within $z \approx 2$–$10$ nm of the sample using **shear-force feedback** (the tip dithers laterally; damping of the dither indicates surface proximity). Evanescent fields at the aperture interact with the sample and either transmit, reflect, or scatter light back into the fibre, where it is detected by a photomultiplier tube.

3. **Scanning and image formation (1 mark):** A piezoelectric XYZ scanner raster-moves the sample under the tip, and the detector signal versus $(x, y)$ is plotted to form the SNOM image.

4. **Resolution comparison (2 marks):**

| Technique | Resolution limit |
|---|---|
| Far-field optical microscopy | $d_{min} = \lambda / (2\,\mathrm{NA}) \approx 200$ nm |
| Aperture SNOM | $d_{min} \approx d_{ap} \approx 50$–$100$ nm |

SNOM therefore achieves a **~3–4× improvement** in resolution, which is essential for imaging sub-100 nm food nanocarriers.

**(b) Bethe–Bouwkamp Derivation and Photon Throughput (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the problem — a plane wave of amplitude $E_0$ is normally incident on a perfectly conducting metal screen of negligible thickness with a circular aperture of radius $a$, with $ka \ll 1$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Bethe's quasi-static solution. The transmitted power is:

$$P_{trans} = \frac{64}{27\pi^{2}} \, k^{4} \, a^{6} \, I_{0}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Recast in terms of aperture diameter $d_{ap} = 2a$:

$$T \approx C \left(\frac{d_{ap}}{\lambda}\right)^{4}, \qquad C \approx 0.26 \text{–} 1$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate for $d_{ap} = 80$ nm, $\lambda = 488$ nm:

$$T \approx 0.5 \times (80/488)^{4} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Interpretation — only ~0.04% of the light passes through the aperture. This $(d_{ap}/\lambda)^4$ scaling makes a-SNOM photon-starved, requiring **intense laser sources, high-sensitivity detectors (PMTs, APDs), and lock-in amplification**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**
**Q:** (a) Explain the **Apertureless (Scattering-type) SNOM (s-SNOM)** technique, including the role of higher-harmonic demodulation.
**(b) Discuss **four** major applications of SNOM in food nanotechnology and explain how the sub-diffraction resolution benefits each application.

### Model Solution

**(a) Apertureless s-SOM (7 Marks):**

1. **Probe design (2 marks):** A sharp **metallic tip** (Au, Pt-Ir) of radius $r_{tip} \approx 10$–$30$ nm is mounted on an AFM cantilever and oscillated vertically at frequency $\Omega$ (tapping mode).

2. **Field enhancement (2 marks):** The tip apex acts as a **lightning-rod antenna**, locally enhancing the incident field by factors of $10$–$10^{3}$. This creates a strongly confined near-field hotspot at the tip apex, which interacts with the sample.

3. **Higher-harmonic demodulation (3 marks):** Because the tip-sample distance is modulated at $\Omega$, the near-field contribution is highly **non-linear** in $z$, generating scattered-light components at harmonics $n\Omega$ ($n = 2, 3, 4, ...$). Demodulating the detector signal at $n \geq 2$ suppresses the linearly varying far-field background and isolates the near-field response:

$$S_{nf}^{(n)} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I_{det}(t) \, e^{-in\Omega t} \, dt$$

The resulting image has resolution set by $r_{tip}$ rather than $\lambda$, achieving **~10–30 nm** in mid-IR and **~5–10 nm** in the visible.

**(b) Applications in Food Nanotechnology (7 Marks; 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 marks):**

1. **Nanoliposome imaging for nutrient delivery (1.5 marks):** SNOM visualises the size, distribution, and integrity of nanoliposomes (~50–100 nm) used to encapsulate curcumin, vitamin D, and omega-3 fatty acids — features invisible to standard light microscopy.

2. **Nanoemulsion droplet characterisation (1.5 marks):** Maps the homogeneity of nanoemulsion droplets in clear beverages, ensuring consistent texture and bioavailability.

3. **Biopolymer nanofibre analysis (2 marks):** Resolves individual protein fibrils (e.g., $\beta$-lactoglobulin, lysozyme fibrils) used as **texture modifiers** in low-fat foods, providing sub-50 nm morphological data.

4. **Active food packaging inspection (2 marks):** Probes the spatial distribution of **nano-Ag and nano-TiO₂** antimicrobial coatings on polymer films, confirming uniform coverage critical for shelf-life extension.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing**
> 1. **Common mistake:** Writing that SNOM resolution is *equal* to the wavelength. The KTU answer key explicitly awards marks only when students state that resolution is set by the **aperture diameter (a-SNOM)** or the **tip apex radius (s-SNOM)**, *not* by $\lambda$.
> 2. **Common mistake:** Skipping the $(d_{ap}/\lambda)^4$ scaling argument in the Bethe derivation. Examiners award 2 of the 7 marks **only** when the fourth-power dependence is explicitly written and a numerical check is shown.
> 3. **Common mistake:** Confusing **near-field** with **far-field** in the working principle. Always state the *distance criterion* $z \ll \lambda$ and the evanescent field decay.
> 4. **Common mistake:** Omitting the food-science context. KTU 2024 scheme awards **application-level marks (1–2)** only when the answer ties the technique to a food nanotechnology use-case (nanoliposomes, nanofibres, active packaging).
> 5. **Unit-error trap:** Substituting $d_{ap}$ in nm without converting to metres. Always convert to SI before numerical evaluation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read the night before the exam**

- **SNOM = Scanning Near-field Optical Microscopy**, a probe technique that beats the Abbe diffraction limit of far-field optics.
- **Resolution driver:** aperture diameter ($d_{ap} \approx 50$–$100$ nm) for a-SNOM; tip radius ($r_{tip} \approx 10$–$30$ nm) for s-SNOM — *not* the wavelength.
- **Working distance:** tip must be in the **optical near-field**, i.e., $z \approx 2$–$10$ nm $\ll \lambda$.
- **Evanescent field decay:** $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-2z/\ell_d}$, with $\ell_d \approx 10$–$50$ nm.
- **Bethe–Bouwkamp law:** $T \propto (d_{ap}/\lambda)^{4}$ → only $\sim 10^{-4}$ of incident light passes an a-SNOM aperture.
- **Two modalities:** Aperture (a-SNOM, fibre tip with Al coating) and Apertureless (s-SNOM, sharp metal tip, harmonic demodulation at $n \geq 2$).
- **Feedback control:** shear-force (a-SNOM) or tapping-mode AFM (s-SNOM) maintains constant tip–sample gap.
- **Food nanotech uses:** nanoliposome imaging, nanoemulsion QC, biopolymer nanofibre mapping, active packaging uniformity tests.
- **Diffraction limit formula to memorise:** $d_{min} = \lambda / (2\,\mathrm{NA}) \approx 200$ nm for visible light.
- **Numerical constants to remember:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $n_{SiO_2} \approx 1.46$; visible $\lambda \approx 400$–$700$ nm.
- **Examiner favourites:** *Bethe derivation*, *evanescent decay derivation*, *a-SNOM vs s-SNOM comparison*, *food-nanotech application mapping*.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** confusing *near-field* with *far-field*; forgetting SI unit conversion in numerical checks; omitting the food-science context.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ESCA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ESCA ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electron Spectroscopy for Chemical Analysis (ESCA)**, universally known by its modern functional name **X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS)**, is a quantitative, surface-sensitive analytical technique that measures the elemental composition, empirical formula, chemical state, and electronic state of the elements present within the top **1–10 nanometers** of a material's surface. The technique is grounded in the **photoelectric effect**, discovered by Heinrich Hertz (1887) and explained by Albert Einstein (1905, Nobel Prize 1921). The formal operational definition recognized by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM E2735-15) states: > "ESCA/XPS is a surface analysis technique in which the sample is irradiated with monochromatic soft X-rays (typically Al Kα at **1486.6 eV** or Mg Kα at **1253.6 eV**), and the kinetic energy distribution of the emitted photoelectrons is measured. From the binding energies and peak intensities, the elemental identity, chemical environment, and concentration of atoms within the sampling depth can be determined." For a B.Tech Food Technology student, the critical takeaway is: **ESCA tells you *what* atoms exist on the surface of a food, nanomaterial, or packaging film, and *how* those atoms are chemically bonded.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > ESCA is grouped with other surface characterization techniques (SEM, TEM, AFM, FTIR, DLS). The examiner expects you to clearly differentiate ESCA from electron microscopy (SEM/TEM) — specifically, **ESCA gives chemical/bonding information from the top ~10 nm, while SEM/TEM give morphological information at higher sampling depths.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **forensic scientist** trying to identify a person (an atom) hiding in a crowded room (the surface of a material). You have two tools: - A **flashlight** (the X-ray beam) that you shine at the crowd. - A **speed gun** (the electron energy analyzer) that measures how fast the people (electrons) run away after being hit by the light. Each person (electron) is tied to their chair with a **rope of a specific strength** — this is their **binding energy**. Different elements and different chemical environments produce ropes of different strengths. The speed gun tells you exactly how much "rope strength" each person was tied with, so you can uniquely identify: - **Who** was in the room (which element: C, O, N, Si, Au, Ti, etc.) - **How they were sitting** (their chemical state: e.g., C–C vs C=O vs C–O in a food polymer) - **How many of them** were there (quantitative analysis via peak area) > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** > ESCA bombards a sample with X-rays → X-rays knock electrons out of atoms → those electrons fly out with energies that depend on the atom they came from and how it was bonded. By measuring the electron energies, we map out the **chemistry of the surface** of a food or nanomaterial. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Planck's constant (h) | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Quantum of action | | Speed of light (c) | **3.0 × 10⁸ m/s** | Used in photon energy E = hν = hc/λ | | Al Kα X-ray energy | **1486.6 eV** | Most common laboratory X-ray source | | Mg Kα X-ray energy | **1253.6 eV** | Alternative X-ray source | | 1 eV | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** | Unit of energy at atomic scale | | Sampling depth | **1–10 nm** | Inelastic mean free path (IMFP) limited | | Ultra-high vacuum (UHV) | **< 10⁻⁹ mbar (≈ 10⁻⁷ Pa)** | Required to prevent surface contamination | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Governing Physical Principle ESCA is governed by **Einstein's photoelectric equation** (1905). When a photon of known energy strikes a bound electron in an atom, the electron is ejected with a kinetic energy that depends on the photon's energy and the electron's binding energy. The phenomenon unfolds in three discrete steps: **Step 1 — Photon Absorption:** A monochromatic X-ray photon of energy $h\nu$ is absorbed by a core-level (or valence-level) electron of an atom in the sample. The photon must carry energy greater than the electron's binding energy; otherwise, no emission occurs (this defines the **absorption edge**). **Step 2 — Photoelectron Emission:** The electron is ejected from the atom into the vacuum. The atom is left in an excited (ionized) state. The ejection direction is statistical, but predominantly along the photon propagation axis, and the photoelectron must travel through the solid to escape. **Step 3 — Energy Analysis and Detection:** Electrons that reach the vacuum without losing energy contribute to the characteristic peaks. Electrons that suffer inelastic scattering (plasmon losses, phonon losses) contribute to the **background tail** of the spectrum. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core Logic Chain (Why ESCA Works):** > Photon energy is **fixed and known** ($h\nu$ = 1486.6 eV for Al Kα) → Measure the **kinetic energy** ($E_k$) of the ejected electron → The **binding energy** ($E_b$) is then uniquely calculated → $E_b$ is a fingerprint of the **element** and its **chemical state**. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi$ | Fundamental ESCA energy equation (Φ = spectrometer work function) | eV | | 2 | $E_b^{\text{ref}} = h\nu - E_k^{\text{ref}} - \phi$ | Same equation referenced to a known standard (usually C 1s at 284.8 eV) | eV | | 3 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy in terms of wavelength | J or eV | | 4 | $\lambda_{Al\,K\alpha} = 0.8339 \text{ nm}$ | Wavelength of Al Kα X-ray (8.339 Å) | nm | | 5 | $I = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{\lambda \cos\theta}\right) = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{\lambda_{IMFP}}\right)$ | Beer-Lambert attenuation of photoelectrons through solid | dimensionless ratio | | 6 | $\text{Atomic Concentration \%} = \dfrac{I_i / S_i}{\sum_j (I_j / S_j)} \times 100$ | Quantitative surface composition from peak areas ($S_i$ = sensitivity factor) | atomic % | | 7 | $\Delta E_{chemical} = E_b^{compound} - E_b^{pure\,element}$ | Chemical shift — the heart of chemical state analysis | eV | | 8 | $d = 3\lambda_{IMFP}\cos\theta$ | Sampling depth (≈ 95% of signal) | nm | > [!NOTE] > **Formula Sheet Tip (no pipe characters used):** > Notice we wrote $\lambda \cos\theta$ instead of $\vert \lambda \cos\theta \vert$ — the KTU exam script cannot break the markdown table, so we always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX when needed. Sensitivity factors $S_i$ are tabulated in the **Scofield cross-section library** and the **Wagner sensitivity factor table**. ### 2.3 Operational Workflow of an ESCA Experiment 1. **Sample Preparation:** Solid samples (food powders, packaging films, dried nanoparticle suspensions drop-cast on Si wafer) are mounted on a sample holder. Conductive double-sided carbon tape is used. For insulating food samples (dried milk powder, starch), a thin conductive coating may be needed, or charge neutralization (flood gun) is employed. 2. **Loading into UHV Chamber:** The sample is transferred into the analysis chamber maintained at **< 10⁻⁹ mbar**. This prevents surface contamination by ambient hydrocarbons (which would otherwise generate a spurious C 1s peak). 3. **X-ray Irradiation:** The Al Kα (or Mg Kα) anode is excited; the X-rays strike the sample surface. 4. **Photoelectron Collection:** Emitted photoelectrons enter the **hemispherical sector analyzer (HSA)**, which acts as an electrostatic prism, sorting electrons by kinetic energy. 5. **Detection:** A channel electron multiplier or microchannel plate counts electrons, producing a spectrum of **counts vs. binding energy**. 6. **Data Analysis:** Peaks are fitted (typically with **Gaussian–Lorentzian sum functions**), assigned to elements, and quantified. ### 2.4 Why UHV (Ultra-High Vacuum) is Mandatory The mean free path of an electron in air at atmospheric pressure is only a few micrometers. In UHV at 10⁻⁹ mbar, the mean free path is in the **kilometre range** — so photoelectrons travel ballistically to the detector. Additionally, a freshly cleaned surface in UHV remains atomically clean for the duration of the analysis (typically 30–60 minutes), giving reproducible results. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food Nanotechnology - **Food packaging:** Analyzing the surface chemistry of **nanosilver-coated food wrap** to confirm the Ag chemical state (metallic Ag⁰ vs. oxidized Ag₂O, both having different antimicrobial behaviors). - **Nanoencapsulation verification:** Confirming whether a curcumin-loaded liposome has the phospholipid phosphate groups exposed at the outer surface. - **Biofilm analysis:** Detecting C, O, N, P, S distribution on the surface of bacterial biofilms on food-contact surfaces. - **Edible coatings:** Quantifying the crosslinking degree of chitosan or alginate films by measuring the C–O / C=O ratio. - **Migration studies:** Detecting trace metal migration (Ti, Sn) from nano-packaging into food simulants. - **Protein adsorption:** Studying how milk proteins (β-lactoglobulin, casein) adsorb onto nanoclay (montmorillonite) surfaces by tracking the N 1s and C 1s peaks. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental ESCA Energy Equation **Starting Premise (Energy Conservation):** The energy of the incoming photon must equal the sum of: (a) the work required to remove the electron from its bound state to the vacuum level, (b) the kinetic energy of the freed electron, and (c) any recoil energy of the atom. We begin with energy conservation for the photoelectric process: $$h\nu = E_b + E_k + E_{recoil}$$ **Step 1 — Neglect the recoil term.** For atoms heavier than hydrogen, the recoil energy is typically less than 0.05 eV because the atomic mass is thousands of times the electron mass. For a 1486.6 eV photon striking a carbon atom, the recoil energy $E_{recoil} \approx h\nu \cdot (m_e/M_a) \approx 1486.6 \times (1/22000) \approx 0.07$ eV — this is below the typical instrument resolution (~0.5 eV). So the energy balance simplifies to: $$h\nu = E_b + E_k$$ **Step 2 — Account for the spectrometer work function.** The electron, after being ejected from the atom, must still overcome the **work function (φ)** of the spectrometer material to be detected. The work function is the energy difference between the Fermi level of the sample and the vacuum level of the spectrometer. This is a small but critical correction (typically φ ≈ 4–5 eV). $$h\nu = E_b + E_k + \phi$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the binding energy.** $$\boxed{E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi}$$ **Step 4 — Express in electronvolts for KTU calculations.** $$E_b\,[\text{eV}] = 1486.6\,[\text{eV}] - E_k\,[\text{eV}] - \phi\,[\text{eV}]$$ This is the **master equation of ESCA** and is the single most important equation you must memorize for the KTU exam. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style) **Problem:** A food packaging film is analyzed using Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). The spectrometer work function is φ = 4.2 eV. The detected C 1s photoelectrons have a kinetic energy of $E_k$ = 1202.0 eV. Calculate the binding energy of the carbon electron and identify the chemical state. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply the energy equation.** $$E_b = h\nu - E_k - \phi$$ $$E_b = 1486.6 \text{ eV} - 1202.0 \text{ eV} - 4.2 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 2 — Perform the arithmetic.** $$E_b = 1486.6 - 1206.2 = 280.4 \text{ eV}$$ **Step 3 — Compare to the C 1s reference table.** The reference C 1s binding energies are: - C–C / C–H (aliphatic, hydrocarbon): **284.6 – 285.0 eV** - C–O (alcohol, ether): **~286.0 – 286.5 eV** - C=O (carbonyl): **~287.5 – 288.0 eV** - O–C=O (carboxyl/ester): **~288.5 – 289.5 eV** The calculated value of 280.4 eV is **inconsistent** — it is too low by ~4.4 eV. This suggests the sample was **charging** (insulating food polymer accumulates positive charge, slowing the electrons and artificially reducing $E_k$). The correction is the **charge correction** using the known adventitious C 1s line. **Step 4 — Apply the charge correction.** $$\Delta E_{charge} = E_{b,\,ref}^{adventitious\,C} - E_{b,\,measured}^{adventitious\,C}$$ $$E_b^{corrected} = E_b^{measured} + \Delta E_{charge}$$ After correction, the C 1s peak typically resolves into multiple components (C–C, C–O, C=O, O–C=O), and the relative area gives the surface chemistry quantitatively. ### 3.3 Quantitative Analysis — Worked Example **Problem:** A nanoclay-reinforced starch film gives the following peak areas in an ESCA survey spectrum (after background subtraction using the Shirley algorithm): - C 1s: Area = 8500 counts·eV, Scofield sensitivity factor $S_C$ = 1.000 - O 1s: Area = 16500 counts·eV, Scofield sensitivity factor $S_O$ = 2.930 - Si 2p: Area = 720 counts·eV, Scofield sensitivity factor $S_{Si}$ = 0.817 - Al 2p: Area = 130 counts·eV, Scofield sensitivity factor $S_{Al}$ = 0.537 Calculate the surface atomic composition. **Step 1 — Compute normalized intensities.** $$I_C = 8500 / 1.000 = 8500$$ $$I_O = 16500 / 2.930 = 5631.4$$ $$I_{Si} = 720 / 0.817 = 881.3$$ $$I_{Al} = 130 / 0.537 = 242.1$$ **Step 2 — Sum all normalized intensities.** $$I_{total} = 8500 + 5631.4 + 881.3 + 242.1 = 15254.8$$ **Step 3 — Compute atomic percentages.** $$C\,\% = \dfrac{8500}{15254.8} \times 100 = 55.72\,\%$$ $$O\,\% = \dfrac{5631.4}{15254.8} \times 100 = 36.92\,\%$$ $$Si\,\% = \dfrac{881.3}{15254.8} \times 100 = 5.78\,\%$$ $$Al\,\% = \dfrac{242.1}{15254.8} \times 100 = 1.59\,\%$$ **Step 4 — Verification.** Sum of percentages: 55.72 + 36.92 + 5.78 + 1.59 = 100.01% (rounding error, acceptable). The high oxygen and Si/Al content confirms surface enrichment of the **nanoclay (montmorillonite)** phase, consistent with a nanoclay-reinforced starch film. ### 3.4 Derivation of Sampling Depth (Beer-Lambert for Electrons) The photoelectrons must travel through the solid to escape. The intensity from a layer at depth $x$ decays exponentially: $$I(x) = I_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}\right)$$ The total measured signal is the integral from $x=0$ to $\infty$: $$I_{total} = I_0 \int_0^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}\right) dx = I_0 \cdot \lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta$$ **Step 1 — Find the depth that contributes 95% of the signal.** We want $I(x_{95})/I_{total}$ integration up to $x_{95}$ to be 0.95. $$\int_0^{x_{95}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}\right) dx = 0.95 \cdot \lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta$$ $$1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x_{95}}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}\right) = 0.95$$ $$\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x_{95}}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}\right) = 0.05$$ **Step 2 — Take natural log.** $$-\dfrac{x_{95}}{\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta} = \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $x_{95}$.** $$\boxed{x_{95} \approx 3\lambda_{IMFP} \cos\theta}$$ This is why the **sampling depth is 3λIMFP·cosθ** — the universal "rule of 3λ" used to define the ESCA analysis depth. For Al Kα radiation, λIMFP is typically **2–5 nm** in organic materials, giving a sampling depth of **~6–15 nm**, hence the "top 10 nm" rule. ### 3.5 The Chemical Shift Derivation (Conceptual) The chemical shift $\Delta E_{chem}$ arises from the difference in **initial-state** and **final-state** electronic environment. Conceptually: $$\Delta E_{chem} = E_b^{compound} - E_b^{element} = \Delta E_{initial} + \Delta E_{final} + \Delta E_{relaxation}$$ Where: - $\Delta E_{initial}$ = change in electron-nucleus attraction due to altered electron density (electronegativity of neighbors). - $\Delta E_{final}$ = extra-atomic relaxation in response to the core hole created. - $\Delta E_{relaxation}$ = intra-atomic relaxation. For KTU purposes, you only need to remember that **a more electronegative neighbor (like O) withdraws electron density, increases the effective nuclear charge felt by the core electron, and shifts the binding energy to higher values.** Example: in O–C=O, the carbonyl C 1s shifts from 285.0 eV (aliphatic) to ~289.0 eV. ## 4.
