## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define "active packaging" and "intelligent packaging". Give two examples of each. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Active packaging** is a packaging system that performs a role beyond an inert barrier by *intentionally* interacting with the food or its headspace to extend shelf-life or maintain quality. Examples: (i) oxygen-scavenging sachet based on iron powder, (ii) silver-nanoparticle coated film for antimicrobial action.

**Intelligent (smart) packaging** is a system that *monitors* the condition of the packaged food and *communicates* information about its freshness, safety, or quality to the consumer or supply chain. Examples: (i) Time-Temperature Indicator (TTI) label, (ii) RFID tag for traceability.

**Distinguishing Point:** Active packaging *acts* on the food; intelligent packaging *informs* about the food. (1 mark for the distinction).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*List four potential health hazards of nanomaterials used in food packaging. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cytotoxicity** — nanoparticles cause reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, damaging intestinal epithelial cells.
2. **Genotoxicity** — TiO₂ and certain metal oxide NPs cause DNA strand breaks (confirmed by EFSA, 2016 for TiO₂).
3. **Gut microbiome dysbiosis** — sub-lethal exposure alters bacterial community structure.
4. **Bioaccumulation** — long-term persistence in liver and spleen following systemic translocation.

*(Bonus acceptable: Endocrine disruption, allergenicity, oxidative stress, placental/BBB transfer).*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU ESE — Module 4 — Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of nanomembranes used in food packaging. Discuss the solution-diffusion and Knudsen diffusion models with their governing equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a neat diagram the four major mechanisms by which active packaging systems interact with food. Provide one commercial example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
Nanomembranes are ultra-thin films (thickness 1–100 nm) fabricated from polymeric (polyamide, polysulfone), ceramic (Al₂O₃, TiO₂), biopolymeric (chitosan, cellulose nanocrystal), or carbon-based (graphene oxide, MXene) materials. They may be dense (non-porous) or porous (microporous, mesoporous, macroporous) depending on application. They are produced by phase inversion, electrospinning, layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly, or track-etching.

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
The mass transport through a nanomembrane is governed by one of two models:

**1. Solution-Diffusion Model (for dense membranes):** The permeant first dissolves into the upstream face of the membrane, then diffuses through the polymer matrix under a chemical potential gradient, and finally desorbs on the downstream face.

Governing equation:

$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}, \quad P = D \cdot S$$

Where $J$ is flux, $P$ permeability, $D$ diffusivity, $S$ solubility, $\Delta p$ partial pressure difference, $L$ thickness. Used in O₂/CO₂ barrier films for MAP and vacuum packaging.

**2. Knudsen Diffusion (for mesoporous membranes):** When mean free path exceeds pore diameter, molecule-pore wall collisions dominate. Governing equation:

$$D_K = \frac{d_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M}}$$

Used for selective gas separation (e.g., CO₂/N₂ in modified-atmosphere packaging of fresh produce).

**Comparative statement (1 mark):** The solution-diffusion model dominates in dense polymer films; Knudsen diffusion dominates in mesoporous ceramic/polymeric membranes with $d_p$ 2–50 nm.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**The four major active packaging mechanisms are:**

**1. Oxygen Scavenging (2 marks):** Removes residual O₂ from the package headspace to inhibit aerobic microbial growth and lipid oxidation. Mechanism: iron-powder oxidation (Fe → Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺) or ascorbic acid oxidation.
*Commercial example:* Ageless® sachet (Mitsubishi Gas Chemical) — iron-based, used in ready-to-eat meals.

**2. Antimicrobial Release (2 marks):** Slow release of bactericidal/fungicidal agents (AgNP, ZnO, essential oils, lysozyme, nisin) to inactivate surface pathogens such as *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli*, and *Salmonella*.
*Commercial example:* Agion® silver-ion containing polymer films; FreshTag® with thymol nano-encapsulated.

**3. Ethylene Absorption (1.5 marks):** Removes C₂H₄ (a plant-ripening hormone) to delay senescence in climacteric fruits (banana, tomato, mango).
*Commercial example:* GreenPod® sachet using KMnO₄ on nano-silica support.

**4. Moisture Regulation (1.5 marks):** Silica gel nano-sponges, calcium oxide, or desiccant polymers absorb excess water vapor, preventing condensation and texture degradation.
*Commercial example:** Desi-Pak® (Sud-Chemie) calcium oxide-based moisture absorber in dried-food packaging.

**Summary table acceptable for full credit.** A labelled diagram showing each mechanism acting on a packaged food is mandatory for the 7-mark allocation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU ESE — Module 4 — Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the components and working of a smart/intelligent packaging system. Explain the role of Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI) and gas sensors with their working principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major health hazards of nano-packaging materials with reference to AgNP, TiO₂, and ZnO. Explain the EFSA risk assessment framework used to evaluate these materials. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Smart packaging is a system that monitors the state of the packaged food and communicates information about its freshness, quality, or safety.

**Components (2 marks):**
1. **Sensors** — detect gases (O₂, CO₂, NH₃, H₂S), pH, biogenic amines, microbial metabolites.
2. **Indicators** — visual/color change in response to a target variable.
3. **Data carriers** — RFID, NFC, QR codes for traceability.
4. **Communication interface** — wireless transmission to consumer or retailer.

**Time-Temperature Indicator (TTI) — 2 marks:**
A TTI is a small label attached to the package that undergoes a **time- and temperature-dependent visual change** (color, diffusion front) reflecting the cumulative thermal history. Working types include:
- **Diffusion-based** (e.g., Lifelines®) — polymer/dye diffusion
- **Enzymatic** (e.g., CheckPoint®) — lipase-catalyzed hydrolysis of a lipid substrate
- **Microbial** — uses a non-pathogenic bacterium with a known growth response
- **Polymerization-based** (e.g., Onvu™) — solid-state photopolymerization

The color-change rate follows Arrhenius kinetics: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$. A well-designed TTI has its $E_a$ matched to the target food's quality-loss kinetics.

**Gas Sensors — 2 marks:**
Gas sensors detect O₂, CO₂, H₂S, or biogenic amines (a sign of spoilage). Types include:
- **Colorimetric** — change color in the presence of target gas (e.g., methylene blue for O₂)
- **Electrochemical** — Clark-type O₂ sensors, amperometric CO₂ sensors
- **Optical/fluorescent** — based on quenching by target gas
- **Metal-oxide semiconductor (MOS)** — SnO₂-based conductivity change

**Conclusion (close the answer with 1 example):** Freshness indicator labels (e.g., RipeSense® for pear ripeness) are now commercialized in major retail markets.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Hazard Identification (2 marks):**
The three main nano-hazards arise from the **small size**, **high surface area**, and **quantum effects** of nano-additives:

1. **Silver nanoparticle (AgNP):** Ag⁺ ion release is the primary toxicity mechanism. Causes mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS generation, and disruption of membrane integrity. Concentrations as low as 10 μg/mL can be cytotoxic to Caco-2 intestinal cells in vitro.
2. **Titanium dioxide (TiO₂):** E171 (microcrystalline, with up to 50% particles < 100 nm) is **genotoxic** — causes DNA strand breaks in cultured human lymphocytes. EFSA (2016, re-evaluated 2020/2021) concluded that TiO₂ **can no longer be considered safe** as a food additive. Withdrawn in the EU in 2022 for food use, but still permitted in food-contact materials with restrictions.
3. **Zinc oxide (ZnO):** Generally considered low toxicity, but at nanoscale generates ROS under UV exposure and releases Zn²⁺ ions. LOAEL (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level) in rodents: 53.8 mg/kg bw/day.

**EFSA Risk Assessment Framework (5 marks):**

EFSA employs a four-step framework:

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification & Characterization:**
Determine physicochemical properties (size, shape, surface charge, agglomeration state, purity) and toxicological profile (in vitro genotoxicity, in vivo sub-acute studies, reproductive toxicity).

**Step 2 — Exposure Assessment:**
Quantify migration from FCM into food using food simulants (e.g., 3% acetic acid, 10% ethanol, olive oil). Determine the **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** under EU Regulation 10/2011.

**Step 3 — Hazard Characterization (Dose-Response):**
Establish **NOAEL** (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) or **BMDL₁₀** (Benchmark Dose Lower Confidence Limit at 10% effect). For AgNP, NOAEL is reported as 30 mg/kg bw/day in rats.

**Step 4 — Risk Characterization:**
Compute **EDI** (Estimated Daily Intake) and **MOE** (Margin of Exposure):

$$\text{MOE} = \frac{\text{BMDL}_{10}}{\text{EDI}}$$

A **MOE > 10,000** is considered low public health concern (EFSA convention). If MOE < 10,000, risk management options (concentration limits, labeling, alternative materials) are imposed.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The precautionary principle (Article 7 of EU 1935/2004) mandates that in the absence of complete data, a *precautionary* approach is taken, often leading to the withdrawal or restriction of the nanomaterial.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Module 4):**
> 1. **Conflating "active" and "smart" packaging** — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks. Memorize: *Active = does*; *Smart = tells*.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention regulation numbers** (e.g., EU 1935/2004, 10/2011) in health-risk questions — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Reporting SML as 0.05 mg/kg for AgNP** without specifying it's a "specific migration limit under 10/2011" — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Using $R = 8.314$ but in calories instead of joules** — Examiner deducts the entire numerical mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to cite at least one commercial example** in active packaging question — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing SML (Specific Migration Limit) with OML (Overall Migration Limit)** — OML = 10 mg/dm² for all FCM; SML is substance-specific.
> 7. **Not labelling axes/units in graphs** of TTI Arrhenius plots — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark per missing axis label.
> 8. **Writing "nanoparticles are toxic"** as a blanket statement — Examiner deducts 1 mark; *toxicity is dose-, size-, and surface-chemistry-dependent*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 (Exam Day Quick-Reference):**

**Nanomembranes:**
- ☐ Definition: thin film with thickness 1–100 nm, selective permeability
- ☐ Three classes: dense, mesoporous, asymmetric
- ☐ Solution-Diffusion: $J = P \cdot \Delta p / L$; $P = D \cdot S$
- ☐ Knudsen: $D_K = (d_p/3) \sqrt{8RT/\pi M}$
- ☐ Common materials: polyamide, polysulfone, chitosan, graphene oxide
- ☐ Key application: O₂/CO₂ barrier in MAP and vacuum packaging

**Active Packaging:**
- ☐ Definition: interacts with food to extend shelf-life
- ☐ Six mechanisms: O₂ scavenge, antimicrobial, ethylene absorb, moisture regulate, CO₂ emit, aroma release
- ☐ Higuchi model: $M_t/M_\infty = K_H \sqrt{t}$ (Fickian, monolithic)
- ☐ Zero-order: $M_t = k_0 t$ (ideal constant release)
- ☐ Commercial examples: Ageless® (O₂), Agion® (antimicrobial), GreenPod® (ethylene)

**Smart/Intelligent Packaging:**
- ☐ Definition: monitors and communicates; does *not* typically act
- ☐ Components: sensors, indicators, data carriers, communication
- ☐ TTI types: diffusion, enzymatic, microbial, polymerization
- ☐ Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$; $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- ☐ Gas sensors: colorimetric, electrochemical, fluorescent, MOS
- ☐ Commercial: RipeSense®, Onvu™, CheckPoint®, Lifelines®

**Health Hazards:**
- ☐ Three primary nano-hazards: AgNP, TiO₂, ZnO
- ☐ TiO₂ (E171) was **withdrawn** from EU food list in 2022 — genotoxicity confirmed
- ☐ Key mechanism: ROS → oxidative stress → DNA damage → inflammation
- ☐ Gut translocation possible at < 100 nm
- ☐ ALARA principle applies

**Regulations:**
- ☐ **EU 1935/2004** — General FCM framework
- ☐ **EU 10/2011** — Plastics, SML = 0.05 mg Ag/kg food
- ☐ **EU 2015/2283** — Novel Food (post-2018)
- ☐ **FDA 21 CFR 174–179** — US FCM
- ☐ **EFSA** — 4-step risk assessment
- ☐ **MOE > 10,000** = low public health concern
- ☐ **BMDL₁₀** — point of departure for dose-response

**Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
- ☐ $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- ☐ O₂ kinetic diameter = 0.346 nm; H₂O = 0.265 nm
- ☐ AgNP NOAEL (rat) ≈ 30 mg/kg bw/day
- ☐ TiO₂ E171 particle fraction < 100 nm ≈ 36–50%
- ☐ EU OML (Overall Migration Limit) = 10 mg/dm²

**Conceptual Distinctions to Avoid Mixing Up:**
- ☐ **Active vs. Smart**: Active *acts*; Smart *tells*
- ☐ **SML vs. OML**: substance-specific vs. total
- ☐ **Nanomembrane vs. Nanocomposite**: membrane is 2D sheet; composite is dispersed NP in matrix
- ☐ **Knudsen vs. Fickian diffusion**: pore-wall collisions vs. concentration gradient
- ☐ **Higuchi vs. zero-order**: $\sqrt{t}$ vs. $t$

**Sample 2-Line Closing Statement for Any 14-Mark Answer:**
> *"Nanotechnology in food packaging — spanning nanomembranes, active systems, and smart indicators — offers transformative potential for shelf-life extension, but its commercialization must be tempered by rigorous toxicological assessment and harmonized global regulation to safeguard consumer health."*

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PEFTT522 — KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.title: Toxicity of Nanoparticles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Toxicity of Nanoparticles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Nanotoxicity** is the branch of nanotoxicology that studies the adverse biological, chemical, and physical effects of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) on living systems, ecosystems, and environmental health, arising from their unique physicochemical properties at the nanoscale (typically **1–100 nm**).

In the context of **food nanotechnology**, nanotoxicity specifically refers to the harmful effects produced by food-grade nanoparticles (e.g., **nano-TiO₂**, **nano-silver (AgNPs)**, **nano-ZnO**, **SiO₂**, **nano-encapsulated nutraceuticals**, **carbon nanotubes (CNTs)**) when they enter the human body through ingestion, inhalation, or dermal contact along the farm-to-fork continuum.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *toxicity of nanoparticles* module requires understanding of **(a) routes of exposure, (b) mechanisms of toxicity, (c) factors influencing toxicity, (d) in-vivo and in-vitro toxicity assays, and (e) risk assessment and regulation**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Invisible Sugar Cubes":**  
> Imagine you dissolve a sugar cube in water — the cube is no longer visible, but the sweet molecules are still there and now spread everywhere, slipping into places the solid cube could never reach (e.g., between cells, inside tissues). Nanoparticles behave similarly: a bulk material like titanium dioxide is biologically inert (used as a food whitener E171), but the same material at **nano-scale** becomes a hyperactive "molecular wanderer" that slips across cell membranes, generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), and damages DNA. **Same chemistry, different size, drastically different biological outcome.**

### 1.3 Why Size Matters — The Nano-Specific Toxicity

| Property | Bulk Material | Nanoparticle (1–100 nm) |
|----------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Surface area / volume ratio | Low | **Extremely high** |
| Surface reactivity | Limited | **Enhanced catalytic & redox activity** |
| Cellular uptake | Negligible | **Endocytosis, passive diffusion possible** |
| Protein corona formation | None | **Forms dynamic biocorona in body fluids** |
| Systemic distribution | Restricted | **Crosses BBB, placental, gut barriers** |

The **specific surface area (SSA)** of a spherical nanoparticle scales inversely with diameter:

$$SSA = \frac{3}{\rho \, r}$$

where $\rho$ is density and $r$ is the particle radius. As $r \to$ nm range, SSA explodes, multiplying the reactive surface sites per unit mass — this is the **root cause of nanotoxicity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Nanoparticles Toxic? — The Core "Why" and "How"

Nanoparticle toxicity arises from a confluence of **physicochemical mechanisms** rather than a single cause. The five pillars of nanotoxicity are:

1. **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) Generation (Oxidative Stress)**
   - *Why:* High surface energy + transition metal content (e.g., Fe, Cu, Zn in metal oxides) catalyze Fenton-like reactions.
   - *How:* Nanoparticle surface donates/conducts electrons to molecular O₂, generating superoxide (O₂•⁻), hydroxyl radical (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂).

2. **Membrane and Organelle Damage**
   - *Why:* Direct physical interaction between rigid, high-aspect-ratio NPs and lipid bilayers.
   - *How:* Lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial membrane depolarization, lysosomal rupture.

3. **Genotoxicity (DNA Damage)**
   - *Why:* ROS + direct nuclear penetration + interference with mitotic spindle.
   - *How:* Single and double-strand breaks, base modifications, chromosomal aberrations.

4. **Inflammation and Immunotoxicity**
   - *Why:* Recognition by immune cells (macrophages, dendritic cells) as "foreign" via pattern recognition.
   - *How:* Pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-6, TNF-α), inflammasome activation (NLRP3).

5. **Protein Corona-Mediated Effects**
   - *Why:* Upon entering biological fluids, NPs adsorb proteins, forming a "biocorona" that dictates biological identity.
   - *How:* Altered biodistribution, exposure of cryptic epitopes, dysregulated signaling.

### 2.2 Routes of Exposure in Food Systems

| Route | Entry Mechanism | Representative NPs in Food |
|-------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Oral (Gastrointestinal)** | Ingestion of nano-additives, nano-encapsulated ingredients | TiO₂ (E171), SiO₂ (E551), AgNPs in packaging |
| **Inhalation (Respiratory)** | Aerosolized dust during processing, spraying | TiO₂, ZnO nanoparticles |
| **Dermal** | Migration from nano-packaging into skin | AgNPs in cosmetic/food contact materials |
| **Trans-placental / Trans-nasal** | Crossing biological barriers | Particularly studied for TiO₂, SiO₂ |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Specific Surface Area (SSA) of sphere** | $SSA = \dfrac{3}{\rho \, r}$ | m²/g; $r$ = radius, $\rho$ = density |
| **SSA of cube** | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \, a}$ | $a$ = edge length |
| **Aspect ratio** | $AR = L/D$ | High-AR (CNTs) → fiber-like toxicity |
| **Surface atom fraction** | $f \approx \dfrac{2 \, d}{D}$ | $d$ = atomic diameter, $D$ = NP diameter |
| **Fenton reaction** | $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$ | Core driver of ROS in iron-based NPs |
| **Zeta potential threshold** | $\vert \zeta \vert < \pm 30$ mV | Predicts colloidal stability; outside this → aggregation |
| **Dose metric (dose per surface)** | $D_{surface} = C_{mass} \times SSA$ | Preferred metric over mass-only dosing |
| **Dose metric (particle number)** | $D_N = C_{mass} / (\rho \, V_{NP})$ | Used for in-vitro assays |
| **Henderson-Hasselbalch (ionizable NPs)** | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Predicts ionization state in GI tract |
| **Henry's law (dissolved NP ions)** | $C = k_H \, P$ | $k_H$ = Henry's constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Critical KTU Note:** Use the **diameter in meters** consistently in SSA equations to obtain SSA in m²/kg. Convert $\rho$ (g/cm³) to kg/m³ by multiplying by **10³** for unit consistency.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food safety regulation:** Quantitative dose-response modeling using SSA-based dosing determines Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI).
- **Packaging design:** Migration studies use Henry's-law-based volatile modeling to predict NP release.
- **Risk assessment:** Aspect ratio and zeta potential are used by EFSA, FDA, and FSSAI for nano-food additive risk categorization.
- **Formulation strategy:** Coating NPs with biocompatible polymers (e.g., chitosan, PEG) reduces biocorona adsorption and toxicity — applied in commercial nutraceutical delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Surface Area of a Spherical Nanoparticle

> *Goal:* Show mathematically why smaller particles have dramatically larger surface area per unit mass.

**Step 1 — Start with definitions**

For a sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho$:

$$V_{particle} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \quad ; \quad A_{particle} = 4\pi r^2$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio (SSA = surface ÷ mass)**

$$SSA = \frac{A_{particle}}{m_{particle}} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$\boxed{SSA = \frac{3}{\rho \, r}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

- For a 1 µm particle: $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- For a 10 nm particle: $r = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- Ratio: $SSA_{10nm} / SSA_{1\mu m} = 100$
- *Conclusion:* A 10-nm particle exposes **100× more surface per gram** than a 1-µm particle of the same material — hence 100× more reactive sites.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparing Dose Metrics: Mass vs. Surface Area vs. Particle Number

> *Goal:* Demonstrate why mass-based dosing alone is misleading in nanotoxicology.

**Step 1 — Define a hypothetical in-vitro dose**

Suppose we add $m = 10 \, \mu g$ of TiO₂ NPs of radius $r$ to $V = 1$ mL of cell culture medium.

$$C_{mass} = \frac{m}{V} = 10 \, \mu g / mL$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of a single particle**

$$m_{NP} = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

For anatase TiO₂, $\rho = 3.9 \, g/cm^3 = 3900 \, kg/m^3$.

**Step 3 — Number of particles in the dose**

$$N = \frac{m}{m_{NP}} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3m}{4\pi r^3 \rho}$$

**Step 4 — Total exposed surface area in the dose**

$$A_{total} = N \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{3m}{4\pi r^3 \rho} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{3m}{r \rho}$$

$$\boxed{A_{total} = \frac{3 \, m}{r \, \rho}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**

Take $m = 10 \, \mu g = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ kg, $r_1 = 50 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $r_2 = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $\rho = 3900$ kg/m³.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{total,\,50\,nm} &= \frac{3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-9} \times 3900} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{1.95 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 1.54 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{total,\,5\,nm} &= \frac{3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-9} \times 3900} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{1.95 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&\approx 1.54 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\frac{A_{total,\,5nm}}{A_{total,\,50nm}} = 10$$

> **Conclusion:** For the same *mass* dose, the **5-nm particles expose 10× more reactive surface** to the cells than 50-nm particles — explaining why equal-mass doses of different-sized NPs produce unequal toxicities.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Atom Fraction and Reactivity

> *Goal:* Show that nano-dimensions put a much larger fraction of atoms on the surface.

**Step 1 — Estimate atoms per particle**

A particle of diameter $D$ has volume $\frac{\pi}{6}D^3$. With atomic volume $v_a$, total atoms:

$$N_{total} = \frac{\pi D^3}{6 \, v_a}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate surface atoms**

A single layer of atoms on the surface:

$$N_{surface} \approx \frac{\pi D^2}{a_s}$$

where $a_s$ is the cross-sectional area per surface atom (≈ $d^2$ for atomic diameter $d$).

**Step 3 — Surface fraction**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{surface} &= \frac{N_{surface}}{N_{total}} \\
&= \frac{\pi D^2 / a_s}{\pi D^3 / (6 v_a)} \\
&= \frac{6 v_a}{a_s \, D}
\end{aligned}$$

For a close-packed solid, $v_a \approx d^3 / \sqrt{2}$ and $a_s \approx d^2$, giving the **engineering approximation**:

$$\boxed{f_{surface} \approx \frac{2 \, d}{D}}$$

**Step 4 — Quick evaluation**

- $D = 100$ nm, $d = 0.3$ nm: $f \approx 0.006 = 0.6\%$
- $D = 5$ nm, $d = 0.3$ nm: $f \approx 0.12 = 12\%$
- $D = 1$ nm, $d = 0.3$ nm: $f \approx 0.6 = 60\%$

> **Insight:** At 1 nm, **majority of atoms are surface atoms**, giving rise to extreme surface energy, dangling bonds, and catalytic reactivity — the molecular origin of nanotoxicity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Toxicity Determinants

| Determinant | Low-Toxicity Configuration | High-Toxicity Configuration | Mechanism |
|-------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|-----------|
| **Size** | >100 nm | <30 nm | Larger SSA, deeper cellular penetration |
| **Shape** | Spherical, low AR | High AR (fibrous, tubular) | Fiber-patheic toxicity (asbestos-like) |
| **Surface charge** | Neutral / near-zero | Strongly +ve (cationic) | Stronger membrane binding, lysosomal rupture |
| **Crystal structure** | Rutile TiO₂ | Anatase TiO₂ | Anatase has higher ROS yield |
| **Composition** | Biodegradable (e.g., Fe₃O₄) | Persistent (Au, TiO₂, CNTs) | Biopersistence drives chronic effects |
| **Aggregation** | Aggregated (low mobility) | Well-dispersed | Dispersed NPs cross biological barriers |
| **Surface coating** | PEG, chitosan | Bare, hydrophobic | Bare surface = more protein corona dysregulation |
| **Solubility** | Soluble (Zn²⁺ release) | Insoluble (TiO₂) | Both toxic — by Trojan-horse effect (soluble) or persistence (insoluble) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**  
*Define nanotoxicity. List any four physicochemical properties of nanoparticles that influence their toxicity.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Nanotoxicity** is the adverse biological response elicited by engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) when they interact with living cells, tissues, organs, or ecosystems, arising from their nanoscale dimensions (1–100 nm) and high surface reactivity.

Four key physicochemical properties influencing nanotoxicity:

1. **Size** — Smaller NPs have larger surface area-to-volume ratio and deeper cellular penetration.
2. **Shape** — High aspect-ratio particles (fibers, tubes) cause asbestos-like pathogenicity.
3. **Surface charge (Zeta potential)** — Strongly cationic NPs damage membranes and disrupt lysosomes.
4. **Chemical composition / crystal structure** — Anatase TiO₂ is more toxic than rutile due to higher ROS yield.
5. *(Any other valid point — surface coating, aggregation, solubility)*

**(3 Marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for properties)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**  
*What is a protein corona? Mention its significance in nanotoxicity.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **protein corona** is the dynamic layer of biomolecules (primarily proteins, but also lipids and metabolites) that adsorbs onto the surface of a nanoparticle immediately upon its entry into a biological fluid such as blood, saliva, or gastrointestinal juice.

**Significance in nanotoxicity:**

- It defines the **"biological identity"** of the NP, which may differ from its synthetic identity.
- It governs **cellular uptake pathway**, biodistribution, and clearance.
- The corona can expose **cryptic epitopes**, trigger **complement activation**, and dysregulate immune signaling.
- The **hard corona** (tightly bound) and **soft corona** (loosely bound) collectively determine the NP's fate and toxic potential.

**(3 Marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for significance)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**  
> • Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by writing only definitions without drawing **labelled mechanisms, equations, or factor tables**.  
> • Avoid the trap of equating **mass-based dose** with **surface-area-based dose** — KTU examiners specifically test the distinction.  
> • In mechanism questions, mention **specific examples** (e.g., Fenton reaction for Fe-NPs, NLRP3 inflammasome for TiO₂) — generic "ROS production" alone yields only partial credit.  
> • Always cite **units** alongside numerical answers.  
> • Do not confuse *cytotoxicity* with *genotoxicity* — these are tested as **distinct endpoints**.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**  
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the various **routes of nanoparticle exposure in food systems** with suitable examples. Discuss how nanoparticles gain entry into the human body and reach systemic circulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **ROS-mediated mechanism of nanotoxicity** in detail with a neat flowchart. List the cellular consequences of oxidative stress. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Routes of Nanoparticle Exposure (7 Marks)**

The principal routes by which food-borne nanoparticles enter the human body are:

1. **Oral (Gastrointestinal) Route — Most relevant for food nanotechnology**  
   - **Source:** Direct ingestion of nano-additives (TiO₂ as whitener E171, SiO₂ as anticaking agent E551), nano-encapsulated nutraceuticals, and AgNP-migrating from food contact materials.  
   - **Fate:** In the GI tract, NPs encounter varying pH (stomach pH 1.5–3.5; intestine pH 6–7.5), digestive enzymes, bile salts, and mucus.  
   - **Uptake mechanism:** Microfold (M) cells of Peyer's patches, paracellular transport, transcytosis via enterocytes, and persorption through gaps at villus tips.  
   - **Systemic entry:** Sub-micron and nano particles can translocate to blood, lymph, liver, and spleen.

2. **Inhalation Route**  
   - **Source:** Workplace dust during milling, spraying, or weighing of nanopowders; aerosol from nano-coating processes.  
   - **Fate:** Alveolar deposition; alveolar macrophages engulf NPs but high aspect-ratio or low-doses can escape phagocytosis, translocate to pulmonary interstitium, and reach systemic circulation via lymphatics.

3. **Dermal Route**  
   - **Source:** Handling of nano-packaged food, cosmetic exposure, contact with nano-treated surfaces.  
   - **Fate:** Healthy stratum corneum is a barrier, but damaged skin, flexure areas, and follicular openings permit NP penetration.

4. **Trans-placental and Trans-nasal Routes**  
   - **Source:** Maternal ingestion of nano-additives, intranasal delivery.  
   - **Fate:** Animal studies show TiO₂ and SiO₂ NPs can cross placental barrier and reach fetal brain (olfactory pathway also documented).

**Diagram (simplified):** Food ingestion → GI lumen → M-cell uptake / transcytosis → Peyer's patches → Mesenteric lymph → Bloodstream → Liver / Spleen / Systemic organs.

**(b) ROS-Mediated Mechanism of Nanotoxicity (7 Marks)**

**Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)** are oxygen-derived free radicals and non-radical species — superoxide (O₂•⁻), hydroxyl (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), singlet oxygen (¹O₂) — whose unpaired electrons make them highly reactive.

**Generation mechanisms by NPs:**

1. **Transition metal catalysis (Fenton / Haber-Weiss):**  
   $Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-$ (Fenton)  
   $Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + \cdot OOH + H^+$ (Fenton-like)

2. **Semiconductor photocatalysis (TiO₂, ZnO):**  
   $TiO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow e^-_{CB} + h^+_{VB}$  
   $e^-_{CB} + O_2 \rightarrow O_2^{\cdot -}$  
   $h^+_{VB} + H_2O \rightarrow \cdot OH + H^+$

3. **Surface redox activity:** Direct electron transfer from NP defect sites to molecular O₂.

4. **Mitochondrial disruption:** NPs entering mitochondria interfere with electron transport chain, leaking electrons to O₂ → O₂•⁻.

**Cellular consequences of oxidative stress:**

| Target | Consequence |
|--------|-------------|
| Lipid membranes | Lipid peroxidation → loss of membrane integrity |
| Proteins | Carbonylation, thiol oxidation → enzyme dysfunction |
| DNA | Base oxidation (8-OHdG), strand breaks → mutagenesis |
| Mitochondria | Permeability transition pore → apoptosis |
| Cytokines | NF-κB activation → IL-6, TNF-α release → inflammation |

**Flow chart (textual):**  
NP internalization → ROS surge → Antioxidant (GSH, SOD, CAT) depletion → Oxidative stress → Lipid peroxidation + Protein oxidation + DNA damage → Mitochondrial dysfunction → Apoptosis / Necrosis → Organ toxicity.

**(End of Question A — 14 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**  
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **in-vitro and in-vivo methods** used for assessing nanoparticle toxicity. Highlight the OECD-recommended assays and their endpoints. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Enumerate the **factors affecting nanoparticle toxicity**. Explain how **size, shape, and surface charge** modify the toxicological profile with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) In-vitro and In-vivo Toxicity Assessment Methods (7 Marks)**

**In-vitro assays (cell-based):**

1. **Cytotoxicity assays**  
   - **MTT / MTS assay:** Mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity → cell viability.  
   - **LDH release assay:** Membrane integrity indicator.  
   - **Neutral red uptake:** Lysosomal integrity.

2. **Genotoxicity assays**  
   - **Comet assay (single-cell gel electrophoresis):** DNA strand breaks.  
   - **Micronucleus test:** Chromosomal damage.  
   - **Ames test:** Mutagenicity in Salmonella.

3. **Oxidative stress markers**  
   - **DCFH-DA fluorescence:** Intracellular ROS.  
   - **GSH/GSSG ratio:** Redox state.  
   - **Lipid peroxidation (MDA-TBA assay).**

4. **Apoptosis / necrosis**  
   - **Annexin V/PI staining**, **caspase-3 activation**, **TUNEL assay.**

5. **Inflammation**  
   - **ELISA** for TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β; **NF-κB reporter assays.**

**In-vivo assays (animal models — OECD Guidelines):**

| Test | OECD No. | Endpoint |
|------|---------|----------|
| Acute oral toxicity | 420, 423, 425 | LD₅₀, clinical signs |
| Sub-acute / sub-chronic (28/90-day) | 407, 408 | Hematology, histopathology |
| Chronic toxicity | 451 | Carcinogenicity |
| Reproductive & developmental | 414, 421, 422 | Teratogenicity, fertility |
| Genotoxicity | 474, 475, 486 | Micronucleus, UDS |
| Sensitization | 406 | Skin allergy |
| Biodistribution | Non-OECD | ICP-MS, radiolabeling |

**In-silico / Computational methods** (emerging): QSAR for NP toxicity prediction, PBPK modeling for biodistribution, read-across approaches.

**(b) Factors Affecting Nanoparticle Toxicity (7 Marks)**

**1. Size and Size Distribution**  
- Smaller NPs (1–30 nm) penetrate cells more easily, exhibit higher SSA, and generate more ROS per unit mass.  
- *Example:* 20-nm TiO₂ induces far greater IL-1β release in macrophages than 250-nm TiO₂.

**2. Shape and Aspect Ratio (AR)**  
- Spherical NPs (low AR) are generally less pathogenic.  
- High-AR particles (fibers, tubes) cause *frustrated phagocytosis* — macrophages cannot engulf long fibers, leading to chronic inflammation, granuloma formation, and asbestos-like pathogenicity.  
- *Example:* Multi-walled CNTs (AR > 100) produce mesothelioma-like lesions in mice, similar to crocidolite asbestos.

**3. Surface Charge (Zeta Potential)**  
- Strongly **cationic** NPs (ζ > +30 mV) bind avidly to negatively charged cell membranes, disrupting lipid bilayers, depolarizing mitochondria, and rupturing lysosomes.  
- Strongly **anionic** NPs are less membrane-active but may be rapidly opsonized and cleared by the reticuloendothelial system.  
- *Example:* Cationic polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers (generation 4, ζ = +40 mV) cause hemolysis and LDH release; anionic variants (ζ = -30 mV) are well-tolerated.

**Additional factors (briefly):**

- **Crystal structure:** Anatase TiO₂ > rutile TiO₂ in ROS yield.
- **Surface coating:** PEGylation reduces corona adsorption and clearance, lowering immune recognition.
- **Aggregation state:** Well-dispersed NPs cross barriers more easily.
- **Composition / dissolution:** Soluble ZnO releases Zn²⁺ (Trojan-horse effect); insoluble TiO₂ persists and accumulates.
- **Route of exposure:** Oral vs inhalation vs dermal yield different organ-specific toxicities.

**(End of Question B — 14 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Toxicity of Nanoparticles**

- ✅ **Nanotoxicity definition** — Adverse effects of ENMs arising from nanoscale (1–100 nm) properties.
- ✅ **Root cause:** Extremely high **specific surface area** (SSA = 3/ρr) leading to enhanced surface reactivity.
- ✅ **Surface atom fraction** scales as $f \approx 2d/D$; approaches 60% at 1 nm size.
- ✅ **Five core mechanisms:** ROS, lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction.
- ✅ **ROS generation routes:** Fenton reaction, semiconductor photocatalysis, surface redox, mitochondrial ETC leak.
- ✅ **Routes of exposure:** Oral, inhalation, dermal, trans-placental/nasal.
- ✅ **Protein corona** dictates *biological identity* and is critical in determining toxicity.
- ✅ **Key intrinsic factors:** Size, shape (AR), composition, crystal structure, surface charge, coating.
- ✅ **Zeta potential rule:** $\vert \zeta \vert < \pm 30$ mV predicts colloidal instability/aggregation.
- ✅ **Dose metric:** Prefer **surface-area-based** or **particle number-based** dose over mass alone.
- ✅ **In-vitro assays:** MTT, LDH, Comet, DCFH-DA, Annexin V, ELISA.
- ✅ **In-vivo assays (OECD):** 420 (acute oral), 407/408 (sub-chronic), 474/475 (genotoxicity), 414 (developmental).
- ✅ **High-AR particles** (CNTs, fibers) cause *frustrated phagocytosis* and asbestos-like pathology.
- ✅ **Cationic NPs** (ζ strongly positive) are more membrane-active and toxic.
- ✅ **Trojan-horse effect** — soluble NPs release ions intracellularly that bypass cellular ion-homeostasis defenses.
- ✅ **Regulatory bodies:** EFSA (EU), FDA (USA), FSSAI (India) require nano-specific risk assessment data.
- ✅ **Mitigation strategies:** PEGylation, chitosan coating, green synthesis, controlled dissolution, use of biodegradable matrices (e.g., Fe₃O₄, PLGA).

> **Final KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, **always carry units** (m, m², kg) through every step — valuation scripts explicitly award the unit-consistency mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Inhaled particles refer to airborne solid or liquid particulates (including engineered nanomaterials used or produced during food processing) that enter the human respiratory tract via the nose and/or mouth during normal breathing. The *effects* constitute the physiological, biochemical, and pathological responses of the respiratory system to these deposited particles, governed by particle size, shape, surface chemistry, dose, and duration of exposure.

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The respiratory system is the principal route of entry for airborne particulates in occupational and environmental settings — including **food processing environments** (grain dust, flour aerosols, spice particulates, and engineered nano-additives). When food-derived nanoparticles (e.g., nano-$\text{TiO}_2$ used as a whitener, nano-$\text{SiO}_2$ as an anti-caking agent, or nano-$\text{ZnO}$ in packaging) become airborne, they may be inhaled, and their deposition pattern and subsequent biological effects depend on **aerodynamic diameter ($d_a$)**, density ($\rho_p$), and surface reactivity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tree and Sieve" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Think of the human respiratory tract as an **inverted tree** standing in a fish tank filled with murky water. The trunk (trachea) is wide, the branches (bronchi) keep splitting, and the leaves (alveoli) are delicate gas-exchange surfaces. As muddy water flows in, **large particles get caught on the bark** (nasal hair, impaction in upper airways), **medium particles settle in the still pools of the branch forks** (sedimentation in bronchioles), and **tiny particles drift all the way to the leaves and get stuck in the wet leaf surfaces** (diffusion in alveoli). Unlike a sieve, the lungs are *alive* — they actively try to sweep debris back up (mucociliary clearance) or engulf invaders with "cleaner cells" (macrophages). But if the particles are *too small* or *too reactive*, the cleaner cells can be overwhelmed — leading to inflammation, like a tree shedding leaves in a storm.

### 1.3 Key Classification Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Particulate Fractions (ISO 7708 / ACGIH):**
> - **Inhalable fraction:** particles with aerodynamic diameter $d_a \leq 100\ \mu\text{m}$ — enters nose and mouth.
> - **Thoracic fraction:** $d_a \leq 25\ \mu\text{m}$ — penetrates beyond the larynx.
> - **Respirable fraction:** $d_a \leq 10\ \mu\text{m}$ (with 50% cut-point at $4\ \mu\text{m}$) — reaches alveoli.
> - **Nanoparticle range:** $d_a \leq 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$ ($100\ \text{nm}$) — highest alveolar deposition efficiency.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Food Nanotechnology

In modern food industries using nanocarriers (liposomes, nanoemulsions, biopolymer nanoparticles), worker exposure during **spray-drying, dry-mixing, and packaging** can release respirable nano-aerosols. KTU 2024 emphasizes that students must understand *system-level biological impact*, not just chemistry of the nanomaterial.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Human Respiratory Tract (Top-Down)

| Region | Anatomical Structure | Key Function | Typical Airway Diameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nasopharyngeal (N-P)** | Nasal cavity, pharynx | Filtration, humidification, warming | $1.5 - 5\ \text{cm}$ |
| **Tracheobronchial (T-B)** | Trachea → bronchi → bronchioles | Conduction, mucociliary clearance | $0.5\ \text{cm} \rightarrow 1\ \text{mm}$ |
| **Alveolar (Gas-exchange)** | Alveolar ducts → alveoli | $\text{O}_2 / \text{CO}_2$ exchange | $0.2 - 0.3\ \text{mm}$ (alveoli) |

Total alveolar surface area $\approx 70 - 80\ \text{m}^2$ (roughly the area of a singles badminton court).

### 2.2 Five Mechanisms of Particle Deposition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** Students must be able to *match* each mechanism to (a) the particle size range, (b) the anatomical region where it dominates, and (c) the airflow regime (laminar vs. turbulent).

**1. Inertial Impaction**
- Dominant for $d_a > 5\ \mu\text{m}$.
- Particles, due to inertia, fail to follow curved airstreams at airway bifurcations and impact on walls.
- Key dimensionless number: **Stokes number ($\text{Stk}$)**.

**2. Sedimentation (Gravitational)**
- Dominant for $0.5\ \mu\text{m} < d_a < 5\ \mu\text{m}$.
- Particles settle under gravity in low-velocity regions of small bronchioles and alveoli.
- Terminal settling velocity given by **Stokes' Law**.

**3. Brownian Diffusion**
- Dominant for $d_a < 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ (especially $d_a < 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$).
- Random thermal motion causes nanoparticles to collide with and deposit on airway walls.
- Characterized by the **diffusion coefficient $D$** and **Peclet number ($\text{Pe}$)**.

**4. Interception**
- Relevant for elongated/fibrous particles (e.g., some cellulose nanofibers) where physical length, not aerodynamic diameter, governs contact.
- Dominant when fiber length $L_f > d_a$.

**5. Electrostatic Precipitation**
- Charged particles are attracted to oppositely charged (or grounded) airway walls.
- Significant for nanoparticles that acquire charge during aerosolization (triboelectric effect).

### 2.3 The ICRP Lung Model (Deposition Fractions)

The **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP) Model** partitions the lung into four regions and provides deposition fractions as a function of $d_a$:

| Region | Volume (mL) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ET₁ (Extrathoracic — anterior nose)** | 20 | Filter coarse dust |
| **ET₂ (Extrathoracic — posterior)** | 20 | Filter fine dust |
| **BB (Tracheobronchial)** | 50 | Mucociliary escalator |
| **bb (Bronchiolar)** | 50 | Clearance transition |
| **AI (Alveolar — Interstitial)** | 3000 | Gas exchange, slowest clearance |

For unit density spherical particles in a reference worker (light exercise, $V_T = 1250\ \text{mL}$, $f = 20\ \text{breaths/min}$):

$$DF_{\text{total}}(d_a) = DF_{ET_1} + DF_{ET_2} + DF_{BB} + DF_{bb} + DF_{AI}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU ESE numerical/derivation questions. Units in square brackets.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_a = d_p \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_0 \chi}}$ | Aerodynamic diameter [$m$] | $\rho_0 = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\chi$ = dynamic shape factor |
| 2 | $V_{ts} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g \, C_c}{18\ \mu}$ | Stokes terminal settling velocity [$m/s$] | Re $< 0.1$, spherical |
| 3 | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{\lambda}{d_p}\left[2.34 + 1.05\, e^{\,-0.39\, d_p/\lambda}\right]$ | Cunningham slip correction [-] | Nanoparticles where $d_p \sim \lambda$ ($\lambda = 0.066\ \mu\text{m}$ for air) |
| 4 | $\text{Stk} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, U \, C_c}{18\ \mu \, D_h}$ | Stokes number [-] | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter of airway |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{k_B \, T \, C_c}{3 \pi \mu \, d_p}$ | Brownian diffusion coefficient [$m^2/s$] | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Pe} = \dfrac{U \, L}{D}$ | Peclet number [-] | $L$ = characteristic length |
| 7 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{air}} \, U \, D_h}{\mu}$ | Airway Reynolds number [-] | Laminar: $\text{Re} < 2000$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{imp}} \approx 1 - \exp(-a \cdot \text{Stk}^b)$ | Impaction efficiency (empirical) | $a, b$ empirical constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cunningham correction $C_c$ is the single most important correction** for nanoparticles — without it, predicted settling velocities are *underestimated* by orders of magnitude, since the no-slip boundary condition breaks down when particle size approaches the mean free path of air molecules ($\lambda \approx 66\ \text{nm}$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Hygiene:** Designing local exhaust ventilation in food nano-powder handling (e.g., spray-dried nano-$\text{SiO}_2$ transfer booths).
- **PPE Selection:** Choosing respirators with appropriate **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** — N95 filters remove $\geq 95\%$ of particles with $d_a \geq 0.3\ \mu\text{m}$.
- **Risk Assessment:** Computing deposited dose (e.g., $\mu$g of $\text{TiO}_2$ per gram of lung tissue per workday) for ISO 31000 occupational risk frameworks.
- **Toxicology Correlations:** Linking deposited dose to biomarkers of inflammation (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-$\alpha$) in exhaled breath condensate studies.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

**Objective:** Derive the steady-state velocity of a spherical particle falling under gravity through a viscous fluid, balancing gravitational force against the viscous drag.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Particle**

The three forces acting on a falling particle are:

1. Weight (downward): $F_g = m_p \, g = \rho_p \, V_p \, g$
2. Buoyancy (upward): $F_b = \rho_{\text{air}} \, V_p \, g$
3. Viscous drag (upward, opposing motion): $F_d$

**Step 2 — Volume of a Sphere**

$$V_p = \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Net Downward Force (Gravity minus Buoyancy)**

$$F_{\text{net}} = (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}})\, V_p \, g = \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6}\, (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}})\, g$$

**Step 4 — Drag Force (Stokes' Law, Re $< 0.1$)**

Stokes derived the drag on a sphere in laminar (creeping) flow:

$$F_d = 3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, V_{ts}$$

**Step 5 — Terminal Velocity Condition**

At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero, so $F_d = F_{\text{net}}$:

$$3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, V_{ts} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_p^3}{6}\, (\rho_p - \rho_{\text{air}})\, g$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{ts}$**

$$\boxed{V_{ts} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g}{18\ \mu}}$$

where we used the approximation $\rho_p \gg \rho_{\text{air}}$ (valid for food particles and engineered nanomaterials).

**Step 7 — Cunningham Slip Correction (Nanoparticle Regime)**

When $d_p$ approaches the mean free path $\lambda$ of air ($\lambda = 66\ \text{nm}$ at STP), the no-slip boundary condition fails. Stokes' drag is reduced by a factor of $C_c$:

$$F_d = \dfrac{3 \pi \, \mu \, d_p \, V_{ts}}{C_c}$$

Re-applying the force balance:

$$\boxed{V_{ts} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g \, C_c}{18\ \mu}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A $100\ \text{nm}$ spherical $\text{TiO}_2$ particle ($\rho_p = 4230\ \text{kg/m}^3$) in air at $T = 298\ \text{K}$. Compute $V_{ts}$.

- $d_p = 100 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$
- $d_p / \lambda = 100 / 66 \approx 1.515$
- $C_c = 1 + (1/1.515) \cdot [2.34 + 1.05 \cdot e^{-0.39 \times 1.515}]$

  $$C_c = 1 + 0.66 \cdot [2.34 + 1.05 \cdot e^{-0.591}] = 1 + 0.66 \cdot [2.34 + 1.05 \cdot 0.554]$$

  $$C_c = 1 + 0.66 \cdot [2.34 + 0.582] = 1 + 0.66 \cdot 2.922 = 1 + 1.929 = 2.93$$

- $\mu_{\text{air}}(298\ \text{K}) = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times (10^{-7})^2 \times 9.81 \times 2.93}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times 10^{-14} \times 9.81 \times 2.93}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times 9.81 \times 2.93}{3.33 \times 10^{10}} = \dfrac{1.216 \times 10^{5}}{3.33 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ts} \approx 3.65 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m/s} = 3.65\ \mu\text{m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This is roughly **10,000× slower** than the same particle would settle in water. Hence, in still alveolar air, a $100\ \text{nm}$ particle takes many hours to settle — making **Brownian diffusion** the dominant deposition mechanism, not sedimentation.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Diffusion Coefficient from Einstein–Stokes Relation

**Step 1 — Einstein's Relation (1905)**

The mean-square displacement of a particle undergoing Brownian motion over time $t$ is:

$$\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 D\, t$$

**Step 2 — Equating to Thermal Energy**

The particle's kinetic energy per degree of freedom (1D): $\tfrac{1}{2} k_B T$. A particle driven by thermal fluctuations experiences a drag force. Equating thermal and viscous dissipation:

$$D = \dfrac{k_B T}{f}$$

where $f$ is the friction coefficient.

**Step 3 — Friction Coefficient for a Sphere (Stokes' Friction)**

$$f = 3 \pi \mu \, d_p / C_c$$

**Step 4 — Combining**

$$\boxed{D = \dfrac{k_B T\, C_c}{3 \pi \mu \, d_p}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Check for $100\ \text{nm}$ $\text{TiO}_2$**

$$D = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 \times 2.93}{3 \pi \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$D = \dfrac{1.205 \times 10^{-20}}{1.744 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 6.91 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Mean Displacement in 1 second**

$$\sqrt{\langle x^2 \rangle} = \sqrt{2 \times 6.91 \times 10^{-10} \times 1} = \sqrt{1.382 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 37.2\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** In $1$ second, a $100\ \text{nm}$ particle diffuses $37\ \mu\text{m}$ — which is comparable to the alveolar diameter ($\approx 200 - 300\ \mu\text{m}$). This is why nanoparticles deposit efficiently in alveoli.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism vs. Particle Size

| Particle $d_a$ (µm) | Dominant Mechanism | Primary Deposition Site | Clearance Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|
| $> 10$ | Impaction | Nasopharynx, large bronchi | Sneezing, nose-blowing |
| $5 - 10$ | Impaction + Sedimentation | Bronchi | Mucociliary escalator ($t_{1/2} \sim$ hours) |
| $1 - 5$ | Sedimentation | Bronchioles → Alveoli | Mucociliary + macrophages |
| $0.1 - 1$ | Sedimentation + Diffusion | Alveolar | Macrophages + lymphatics |
| $< 0.1$ (Nano) | Brownian Diffusion | Alveolar (interstitium) | Slow: weeks to months; **translocation possible** |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Particle Fate in the Respiratory System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inhaled Aerosol] --> B{Aerodynamic Diameter da}
    B -->|da greater than 10 um| C[Impaction in Naso Pharynx]
    B -->|da between 1 and 10 um| D[Sedimentation in Bronchioles]
    B -->|da less than 0.5 um| E[Brownian Diffusion in Alveoli]
    C --> F[Clearance by Sneeze and Nasal Mucus]
    D --> G[Mucociliary Escalator to Pharynx]
    E --> H[Alveolar Macrophage Phagocytosis]
    G --> I[Swallowed and GI Tract Elimination]
    H --> J{Particle Biopersistent?}
    J -->|No| K[Macrophage Carries to Mucociliary Stream]
    J -->|Yes| L[Translocation to Interstitium and Lymphatics]
    L --> M[Inflammation and ROS Generation]
    M --> N[Pathology: Fibrosis, Carcinogenesis]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Health-Effect Cascade for Nanoparticles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Engineered Nanomaterial in Food Industry] --> A2[Aerosolization during Processing]
    A2 --> A3[Inhalation Exposure]
    A3 --> A4[Alveolar Deposition of Nanoparticles]
    A4 --> A5[High Surface Area to Volume Ratio]
    A5 --> A6[Reactive Oxygen Species ROS Generation]
    A6 --> A7[Oxidative Stress in Epithelial Cells]
    A7 --> A8[Pro Inflammatory Cytokine Release]
    A8 --> A9[Chronic Inflammation]
    A9 --> A10[Tissue Remodeling and Fibrosis]
    A9 --> A11[Genotoxicity and Carcinogenesis]
    A10 --> A12[Adverse Health Outcome]
    A11 --> A12
```

### 4.3 Architecture Flow — Mucociliary and Macrophage Clearance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Deposited Particle on Airway Wall] --> Q{Particle Location}
    Q -->|Bronchial| R1[Mucus Layer Traps Particle]
    R1 --> R2[Cilia Beat Coordinated Wave]
    R2 --> R3[Transport to Pharynx]
    R3 --> R4[Swallowed or Expectorated]
    Q -->|Alveolar| S1[Alveolar Macrophage Engulfs Particle]
    S1 --> S2{Particle Biopersistent?}
    S2 -->|No Degradable| S3[Macrophage Migrates to Mucociliary Stream]
    S3 --> R3
    S2 -->|Yes Non Degradable| S4[Particle Laden Macrophage Stays in Alveolus]
    S4 --> S5[Particle Leaks into Interstitium]
    S5 --> S6[Lymphatic Uptake and Systemic Circulation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangle is a state. Diamonds are decision points (size, location, biodegradability). The cascade illustrates that the *same* deposited nanoparticle can lead to *different* outcomes based on its **chemistry** (degradable vs. biopersistent) — a key principle KTU examiners love to test.
