## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`**  
**Distinguish between the inhalable, thoracic, and respirable fractions of airborne particulates. State the corresponding aerodynamic diameter cut-offs as per ISO 7708.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Inhalable fraction* (1 mark): the mass fraction of total airborne particles that enters the body through the nose and/or mouth during breathing. Cut-off: $d_a \leq 100\ \mu\text{m}$.
- *Thoracic fraction* (1 mark): the sub-fraction that penetrates beyond the larynx and enters the tracheobronchial region. Cut-off: $d_a \leq 25\ \mu\text{m}$.
- *Respirable fraction* (1 mark): the sub-fraction that reaches the alveolar (gas-exchange) region of the lungs. Cut-off: $d_a \leq 10\ \mu\text{m}$, with $50\%$ penetration at $4\ \mu\text{m}$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`**  
**Why are nanoparticles more toxic per unit mass than larger particles of the same chemical composition when deposited in the alveoli?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Higher surface-area-to-volume ratio:** at equal mass, $100\ \text{nm}$ particles have $\sim 1000\times$ the surface area of $1\ \mu\text{m}$ particles, exposing more reactive surface atoms per unit mass.
- (1 mark) **Enhanced ROS generation:** the increased surface area catalyzes the formation of reactive oxygen species (·OH, ·O$_2^-$), causing oxidative stress to alveolar epithelial cells.
- (1 mark) **Reduced macrophage clearance efficiency:** alveolar macrophages phagocytose optimally when particle size is $1 - 3\ \mu\text{m}$. Nanoparticles are often "unseen" or incompletely engulfed, allowing persistence in the alveolar/interstitial space.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Formula vs. Substitution Marks:** Examiners allocate ~2 marks for stating the correct formula, ~2 marks for correct substitution, and ~1 mark for unit & significant figures. Skipping the formula statement costs easy marks.
> 2. **Cunningham Correction Trap:** For *any* $d_p < 1\ \mu\text{m}$ calculation, you **MUST** apply $C_c$. Omitting it in a nano-aerosol problem is an automatic 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Anatomical Region Naming:** Don't write "lungs" vaguely. Use "alveolar region" / "tracheobronchial region" / "nasopharyngeal region" — examiners reward precise terminology.
> 4. **Unit Conversion:** Mixing $\mu\text{m}$ and $\text{nm}$ in the same equation is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive the expression for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in still air, including the **Cunningham slip correction factor**. Show the step-by-step force balance clearly.

**(b) (7 marks)** A worker in a nano-$\text{TiO}_2$ packaging unit is exposed to airborne $\text{TiO}_2$ particles (density $\rho_p = 4230\ \text{kg/m}^3$) of diameter $d_p = 50\ \text{nm}$. Air viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$; mean free path of air $\lambda = 66\ \text{nm}$; temperature $T = 298\ \text{K}$.

(i) Calculate the Cunningham correction factor $C_c$.  
(ii) Calculate the terminal settling velocity.  
(iii) Comment on the dominant deposition mechanism for this particle in the alveoli.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)** — Follows the **Derivation I** in Section 3.1. Award breakdown:
- Force balance statement: 2 marks
- Stokes' drag expression: 1 mark
- Buoyancy/gravity terms: 1 mark
- Derivation of $V_{ts}$: 2 marks
- Inclusion and explanation of $C_c$: 1 mark

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Cunningham Correction Factor (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{d_p}{\lambda} = \dfrac{50}{66} = 0.758$$

$$C_c = 1 + \dfrac{1}{0.758}\left[2.34 + 1.05 \cdot e^{-0.39 \times 0.758}\right]$$

$$C_c = 1 + 1.320 \left[2.34 + 1.05 \cdot e^{-0.295}\right]$$

$$e^{-0.295} = 0.7444$$

$$C_c = 1 + 1.320 \left[2.34 + 1.05 \times 0.7444\right]$$

$$C_c = 1 + 1.320 \left[2.34 + 0.7816\right] = 1 + 1.320 \times 3.1216$$

$$\boxed{C_c \approx 5.12}$$

**(ii) Terminal Settling Velocity (2 marks):**

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g \, C_c}{18\ \mu}$$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times (50 \times 10^{-9})^2 \times 9.81 \times 5.12}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 9.81 \times 5.12}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V_{ts} = \dfrac{4230 \times 2.5 \times 9.81 \times 5.12}{3.33 \times 10^{11}} = \dfrac{5.31 \times 10^5}{3.33 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ts} \approx 1.59 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m/s} = 1.59\ \mu\text{m/s}}$$

**(iii) Dominant Mechanism (3 marks):**

Since $d_a = 50\ \text{nm} < 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$, this is in the nanoparticle regime. Sedimentation is negligible ($V_{ts}$ is microscopic, and the residence time in alveoli is too short to allow gravitational settling). Therefore, **Brownian diffusion is the dominant deposition mechanism**. The diffusion coefficient is:

$$D = \dfrac{k_B T\, C_c}{3 \pi \mu \, d_p} = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 \times 5.12}{3 \pi \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$D = \dfrac{2.107 \times 10^{-20}}{8.72 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 2.42 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

The mean displacement in 1 s: $\sqrt{2 D t} = \sqrt{2 \times 2.42 \times 10^{-9} \times 1} \approx 69.6\ \mu\text{m}$ — sufficient to contact alveolar walls ($\sim 200\ \mu\text{m}$ diameter).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 4]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the **five mechanisms of particle deposition** in the human respiratory tract. For each, state the governing particle size range, the dominant anatomical region, and the primary dimensionless number characterizing the process.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the **mucociliary escalator** and the **alveolar macrophage clearance** mechanism. Discuss why biopersistent nanoparticles (e.g., nano-$\text{TiO}_2$, nano-$\text{SiO}_2$) pose a greater long-term health risk than biodegradable nano-carriers (e.g., liposomes, biopolymer nanoparticles) used in food applications.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Five Mechanisms (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**
1. **Inertial Impaction:** $d_a > 5\ \mu\text{m}$; nasopharynx & main bronchi; governed by **Stokes number (Stk)**.
2. **Sedimentation:** $0.5 < d_a < 5\ \mu\text{m}$; bronchioles and alveoli; governed by **gravitational settling time vs. residence time**.
3. **Brownian Diffusion:** $d_a < 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$; alveoli; governed by **Peclet number (Pe)** and **diffusion coefficient $D$**.
4. **Interception:** fibrous/aspect-ratio particles; bronchi/bronchioles; governed by **fiber length $L_f$ vs. airway diameter**.
5. **Electrostatic Precipitation:** charged particles; all regions; governed by **Coulombic image force and particle charge**.

**(b) Clearance Mechanisms (7 marks):**

**Mucociliary Escalator (3 marks):**
- Goblet cells in the tracheobronchial region secrete a **mucus layer** ($\sim 5 - 10\ \mu\text{m}$ thick) that traps deposited particles.
- Ciliated columnar epithelial cells beat in a coordinated, metachronal rhythm (frequency $\sim 10 - 20\ \text{Hz}$) moving mucus upward at $\sim 1 - 2\ \text{mm/min}$ in small airways and up to $\sim 20\ \text{mm/min}$ in the trachea.
- Mucus + trapped particles are delivered to the oropharynx, then swallowed (GI tract) or expectorated.
- Clearance half-time: hours to days.
- **Limitation:** The alveolar region has **NO cilia and NO mucus** — hence a different mechanism is required.

**Alveolar Macrophage Clearance (2 marks):**
- Alveolar macrophages (AMs) — the resident phagocytic immune cells — engulf particles deposited on the alveolar epithelium.
- Optimal phagocytosis size: $1 - 3\ \mu\text{m}$ (smaller particles may be incompletely engulfed; larger ones cannot be internalized).
- Particle-laden AMs either (i) migrate to the mucociliary escalator, or (ii) enter the pulmonary interstitium and lymphatic system.

**Biopersistent vs. Biodegradable Nanoparticles (2 marks):**
- **Biopersistent NPs** (e.g., $\text{TiO}_2$, $\text{SiO}_2$): not metabolized; accumulate in alveolar/interstitial tissue; chronic ROS release → sustained inflammation → fibrosis, genotoxicity, carcinogenicity.
- **Biodegradable NPs** (e.g., liposomes, PLGA, chitosan NPs): degraded by AM enzymes or hydrolyzed into safe metabolites; transient exposure; clearance half-time of days to weeks.
- Hence, KTU 2024 emphasizes that *for food applications, biodegradable nanocarriers are preferred from an occupational-safety perspective.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Inhaled Particles & Respiratory System**

- **Three ISO 7708 Fractions:** Inhalable ($\leq 100\ \mu\text{m}$), Thoracic ($\leq 25\ \mu\text{m}$), Respirable ($\leq 10\ \mu\text{m}$).
- **Five Deposition Mechanisms:** Impaction, Sedimentation, Diffusion, Interception, Electrostatic — match each to particle size, anatomical site, and dimensionless number.
- **Stokes' Law:** $V_{ts} = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, g}{18\ \mu}$ — the foundation of gravitational deposition analysis.
- **Cunningham Correction ($C_c$):** **Mandatory** for $d_p < 1\ \mu\text{m}$ — without it, settling velocity is *wrong* by an order of magnitude.
- **Stokes Number ($\text{Stk}$):** the ratio of particle stopping distance to airway characteristic length — controls impaction efficiency.
- **Brownian Diffusion Coefficient ($D$):** inversely proportional to $d_p$ — small particles diffuse faster.
- **ICRP Lung Regions:** ET₁, ET₂, BB, bb, AI — know each region's clearance mechanism.
- **Mucociliary Escalator:** operates in the tracheobronchial region only — alveoli rely on macrophages.
- **Macrophage Optimal Phagocytosis Size:** $1 - 3\ \mu\text{m}$ — nanoparticles are sub-optimal for clearance.
- **Nanoparticle-Specific Risks:** high surface area, ROS generation, interstitial translocation, lymphatic uptake.
- **Food Industry Relevance:** nano-$\text{TiO}_2$, nano-$\text{SiO}_2$, nano-$\text{ZnO}$ — all biopersistent; need occupational controls.
- **Preferred Design Principle:** biodegradable nanocarriers (liposomes, PLGA, chitosan) reduce long-term respiratory risk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skin Exposure to Nanoparticles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Skin Exposure to Nanoparticles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Skin Exposure to Nanoparticles** refers to the direct dermal contact, percutaneous absorption, and subsequent biological interaction of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) — typically in the size range of **1–100 nm** — with the various anatomical layers of human skin, including the stratum corneum, viable epidermis, and dermis. In food nanotechnology, this exposure pathway is critically significant because nanoparticles employed in **food contact materials (FCMs)**, smart packaging, antimicrobial coatings, and edible films can migrate from the substrate onto the skin of handlers and consumers, raising critical toxicological, allergological, and regulatory concerns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Skin exposure constitutes one of the three primary routes of human nanomaterial exposure, alongside inhalation and oral ingestion. For food technology students, this is essential because **food packaging workers, food handlers, and end consumers** are all susceptible populations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of your skin as a **fortified medieval castle wall**:

- The **outer mortar layer (stratum corneum)** is made of dead, flattened skin cells (corneocytes) embedded in a lipid matrix — this is your **first line of defense**.
- The **guards patrolling the gates (hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands)** — these are the *transappendageal routes*. They are tiny openings, but a sneaky nanoparticle smaller than the gap can slip through.
- The **inner courtyards (viable epidermis and dermis)** house blood vessels, immune cells, and nerves — once a nanoparticle reaches here, it can enter systemic circulation.

> Imagine a **sugar cube (macroscale particle)** trying to enter a sealed brick wall — it cannot. But crush that cube into **fine powder (nanoscale particles)**, and individual grains can squeeze through the **microscopic cracks between bricks (intercellular lipids)** or get carried through the **drainage pipes (hair follicles)**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Particle Size Threshold for Penetration:** Generally, particles **< 4 nm** can penetrate intact skin readily; **4–20 nm** show limited penetration; **> 20 nm** rarely penetrate intact stratum corneum.
- **Skin Surface Area (Adult):** Approximately **1.5–2.0 m²**.
- **Stratum Corneum Thickness:** **10–20 μm** (when dry); **40 μm** (when hydrated).
- **Hair Follicle Density (Scalp):** ~**100–150 follicles/cm²**.
- **Langerhans Cells Density (Epidermis):** ~**500–1000 cells/mm²**.
- **Standard Reference Temperature for In-vitro Skin Diffusion Studies:** **32 °C** (skin surface temperature).

### 1.4 Fundamental Importance in Food Nanotechnology

| Application | Skin Exposure Concern |
|---|---|
| Nano-silver food packaging | Worker dermal contact during packaging fabrication |
| Nano-ZnO in edible coatings | Migration from packaging to skin during handling |
| TiO₂ (E171) in confectionery glazes | Transfer to lips, hands (perioral skin) |
| Nanoemulsion-based topical nutraceuticals | Direct intended dermal application |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Skin is the **largest organ** of the human body and a potentially significant — though often underestimated — portal for nanoparticle entry, especially in occupational and consumer-use scenarios involving food-contact nanomaterials.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Human Skin Structure — A Layer-by-Layer Analysis

The skin is structurally divided into **three primary layers**, each presenting a distinct barrier function and absorption characteristic:

1. **Stratum Corneum (SC) — "The Brick and Mortar Wall"**
   - Thickness: **10–20 μm** (dry) / **40 μm** (hydrated)
   - Composed of ~**15–20 layers** of dead, anucleate corneocytes
   - **"Brick"** = corneocytes (keratin-filled, ~**40 μm** diameter, **0.5 μm** thick)
   - **"Mortar"** = intercellular lipids (ceramides, cholesterol, free fatty acids)
   - Primary barrier: **selective permeability** based on lipophilicity and molecular size

2. **Viable Epidermis (VE)**
   - Thickness: **50–100 μm**
   - Contains **Langerhans cells** (antigen-presenting immune cells) and **Merkel cells** (mechanoreceptors)
   - Active metabolic zone — capable of metabolizing xenobiotics

3. **Dermis**
   - Thickness: **1–2 mm**
   - Contains **blood capillaries, lymphatics, nerve endings, hair follicles, sebaceous and sweat glands**
   - Once a nanoparticle reaches the dermis, **systemic uptake** becomes possible

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** The skin is **not uniform** — different anatomical regions (palms, scalp, scrotum) have vastly different permeabilities. The **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** applies only after a particle bypasses the SC.

### 2.2 The Three Pathways of Nanoparticle Skin Penetration

Nanoparticles traverse the skin via **three distinct mechanisms**, schematically analogous to the multiple entrances of a fortress:

#### Pathway I: Intercellular Route (Lipid Pathway)
- Nanoparticles diffuse **between corneocytes** through the continuous lipid matrix.
- Tortuous, zigzag path through alternating hydrophilic and lipophilic domains.
- Most common route for **lipophilic nanoparticles** of size **< 10 nm**.
- Rate-limiting step: diffusion through the **lipid bilayer** of the SC.

#### Pathway II: Transcellular Route (Intracellular Pathway)
- Nanoparticles pass **directly through corneocytes** (keratin-rich cells).
- Requires crossing both the **lipophilic cell membrane** and the **hydrophilic cytoplasm** — a high-energy barrier.
- Least common pathway for intact NPs; favored by **very small (< 4 nm)** hydrophilic particles.

#### Pathway III: Transappendageal Route (Shunt Pathway)
- Nanoparticles bypass the SC entirely by traveling through **hair follicles, sweat ducts, and sebaceous glands**.
- Hair follicle infundibulum acts as a **reservoir** for topically applied NPs.
- Particle size window: typically **20–200 nm** preferentially accumulates in follicular openings.
- Critical for **rigid, spherical metallic nanoparticles** (e.g., Au, Ag, TiO₂).

> [!NOTE]
> **2017 OECD & SCCS Finding:** The **transappendageal route** has gained significant scientific interest following studies showing that **flexible, non-spherical particles up to 200 nm** can accumulate in hair follicles, creating a long-term depot.

### 2.3 Factors Governing Skin Penetration of Nanoparticles

| Factor | Effect on Penetration |
|---|---|
| **Particle Size (smaller)** | ↑ Penetration (size < 100 nm is the critical threshold) |
| **Particle Shape (elongated, flexible)** | ↑ Follicular penetration |
| **Surface Charge (cationic)** | ↑ Electrostatic interaction with anionic skin lipids |
| **Lipophilicity (log P > 1)** | ↑ Diffusion through intercellular lipids |
| **Surface Functionalization (PEG, peptides)** | Modulates cellular uptake |
| **Skin Hydration** | ↑ Penetration (lipids swell, become more fluid) |
| **Skin Damage (cuts, abrasions, eczema)** | ↑ Penetration (compromised barrier) |
| **Occlusion** | ↑ Hydration → ↑ Penetration |
| **Vehicle (pH, viscosity, surfactants)** | Strongly modulates |
| **Temperature** | ↑ T → ↑ lipid fluidity → ↑ penetration |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid markdown table breaking. All formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and standard in dermal pharmacokinetics.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \frac{D \cdot K \cdot C_0}{h}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion (steady-state flux) | $J$ = flux ($\mu$g/cm²/h), $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $K$ = partition coefficient, $C_0$ = donor concentration, $h$ = SC thickness |
| 2 | $P = \frac{K \cdot D}{h}$ | Permeability coefficient | $P$ = cm/h |
| 3 | $K_p = \frac{J_{ss}}{C_0}$ | Experimental permeability coefficient | $K_p$ = cm/h |
| 4 | $\log P = \log \left(\frac{[S]_{octanol}}{[S]_{water}}\right)$ | Lipophilicity (octanol-water partition) | Dimensionless; $\log P > 1$ = lipophilic |
| 5 | $r_{Stokes\text{-}Einstein} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta D}$ | Stokes-Einstein radius | $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $\eta$ = viscosity |
| 6 | $D_{nano} \approx D_{bulk} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{a}{r}\right)^{-1}$ | Effective diffusion of NP near surface (hydrodynamic) | $a$ = solute radius, $r$ = pore radius |
| 7 | $T_{lag} = \frac{h^2}{6D}$ | Lag time for steady-state diffusion | $h$ = membrane thickness |
| 8 | $Q(t) = A \cdot K \cdot h \cdot C_0 \left[\frac{D \cdot t}{h^2} - \frac{1}{6} - \frac{2}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{n^2}\exp\left(-\frac{D n^2 \pi^2 t}{h^2}\right)\right]$ | Cumulative amount permeated (full solution) | $Q(t)$ = amount at time $t$ |
| 9 | $\zeta\text{-potential} = \frac{4 \pi \eta \cdot v}{E}$ | Zeta potential (surface charge proxy) | mV; $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ = stable colloid |
| 10 | $SA_{specific} = \frac{3}{\rho \cdot r}$ | Specific surface area of spherical NP | $\rho$ = density, $r$ = radius |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are indispensable in:

- **Cosmetics & Dermal Drug Delivery:** Designing nanoemulsions and nanocarriers for transdermal insulin, vaccines, and analgesics.
- **Food Contact Material Safety:** Estimating migration of nano-additives from food packaging into skin during handling — a regulatory requirement under **EU Regulation 1935/2004** and **US FDA 21 CFR**.
- **Risk Assessment:** Calculating systemic exposure dose (SED) for nanomaterial risk dossiers submitted to regulatory bodies.
- **Occupational Hygiene:** Setting **permissible exposure limits (PELs)** for nano-silver workers in food packaging plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law of Steady-State Skin Diffusion

Starting from the fundamental definition of **diffusive flux** through a membrane:

**Step 1: Define the diffusive flux**

By definition, the amount of substance $dQ$ crossing a unit area $A$ of the stratum corneum in time $dt$ is given by:

$$J = \frac{1}{A}\frac{dQ}{dt}$$

**Step 2: Apply Fick's First Law**

Fick's First Law states that flux is proportional to the concentration gradient across the membrane:

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 3: Boundary conditions**

At the outer surface of the SC (donor side): $C(x=0) = C_0$
At the inner surface of the SC (receiver side): $C(x=h) = C_{receiver}$

Assuming a pseudo-steady-state across the SC of thickness $h$:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} \approx \frac{C_0 - C_{receiver}}{h}$$

**Step 4: Introduce the partition coefficient**

The SC is a lipophilic membrane. The concentration **inside** the SC at the surface is related to the **applied** concentration by the partition coefficient $K$:

$$C_{SC,surface} = K \cdot C_0$$

**Step 5: Combine the relations**

Substituting into Fick's Law, with $C_{receiver} \approx 0$ (sink condition, typical of in-vitro Franz cell setup):

$$J = \frac{D \cdot K \cdot C_0}{h}$$

**Step 6: Define the permeability coefficient**

Combining $D$ and $K$ into a single parameter $P$:

$$P = \frac{D \cdot K}{h}$$

Therefore, the **steady-state flux equation** is:

$$\boxed{J_{ss} = P \cdot C_0 = \frac{D \cdot K \cdot C_0}{h}}$$

**Step 7: Compute the lag time**

The time required to reach steady state is the **lag time** $T_{lag}$, derived from the time-dependent solution of Fick's Second Law under membrane boundary conditions:

$$T_{lag} = \frac{h^2}{6D}$$

This means: the **thicker the SC**, the longer the lag; the **larger the diffusion coefficient**, the shorter the lag.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Migration of Nano-ZnO from Food Packaging

> **Problem:** A food packaging film contains **0.5% w/w nano-ZnO** (spherical, d = **30 nm**). A worker handles 100 packages per shift, with each contact lasting 10 seconds, contacting ~25 cm² of film. The SC thickness is 15 μm. Compute the steady-state flux assuming a partition coefficient $K = 2.5$ and a diffusion coefficient $D = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ cm²/s. Comment on the toxicological implication.

**Step 1: Compute concentration at the skin surface**

Assume the nano-ZnO is uniformly released as a thin film; effective surface concentration $C_0 \approx 50 \, \mu g/cm^2$ (typical for 0.5% w/w coating, 10 μm thick).

$$C_0 = 50 \, \mu g/cm^2$$

**Step 2: Compute permeability coefficient**

$$P = \frac{D \cdot K}{h} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-9} \, cm^2/s)(2.5)}{15 \times 10^{-4} \, cm}$$

$$P = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.67 \times 10^{-6} \, cm/s$$

**Step 3: Compute steady-state flux**

$$J_{ss} = P \cdot C_0 = (1.67 \times 10^{-6} \, cm/s)(50 \, \mu g/cm^2)$$

$$J_{ss} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \, \mu g/cm^2/s$$

**Step 4: Compute cumulative dose per shift**

For 100 contacts × 10 s × 25 cm²:

$$Q_{shift} = J_{ss} \times t \times A = (8.33 \times 10^{-5})(10)(25)(100)$$

$$Q_{shift} = 2.08 \, \mu g \, ZnO \, per \, shift$$

**Step 5: Compute lag time**

$$T_{lag} = \frac{h^2}{6D} = \frac{(15 \times 10^{-4})^2}{6 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$T_{lag} = 375 \, s \approx 6.25 \, minutes$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 10-second contact time is **shorter** than $T_{lag}$ of 6.25 minutes — meaning that under normal handling, **nano-ZnO does not reach steady-state flux**. However, **occlusion, hydration, or repeated handling** could substantially increase $T_{lag}$ effectiveness, allowing measurable dermal uptake.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Comparison

| Property | Intercellular | Transcellular | Transappendageal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Barrier crossed** | Lipid matrix | Cell membrane + cytoplasm + lipid | Hair follicle / sweat duct |
| **Tortuosity** | High | Very High | Low |
| **Preferred NP size** | < 10 nm | < 4 nm | 20–200 nm |
| **Preferred NP property** | Lipophilic | Hydrophilic | Rigid, spherical |
| **Surface area available** | ~99% of SC | < 1% | < 1% (but bypasses SC) |
| **Rate contribution** | Dominant for small molecules | Negligible | Dominant for larger NPs |
| **Food nanotech relevance** | Nano-preservatives | Hydrophilic vitamins | TiO₂, ZnO from packaging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define skin exposure to nanoparticles. Mention any two major routes by which nanoparticles can penetrate the skin.**

**Model Answer:**

**Skin exposure to nanoparticles** refers to the direct dermal contact and subsequent percutaneous penetration of engineered nanomaterials (size 1–100 nm) from external sources (such as food contact materials, cosmetics, or occupational settings) into and through the skin layers.

The two major routes of nanoparticle skin penetration are:

1. **Intercellular (lipid) route:** Nanoparticles diffuse through the continuous lipid matrix between the corneocytes of the stratum corneum. This route is favored for lipophilic nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm.

2. **Transappendageal (shunt) route:** Nanoparticles bypass the stratum corneum by entering through hair follicles, sweat glands, and sebaceous ducts. This route is favored for rigid spherical nanoparticles in the size range of 20–200 nm, which preferentially accumulate in the follicular infundibulum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**List any three factors that influence the skin penetration of nanoparticles used in food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

Three factors influencing skin penetration of nanoparticles from food packaging:

1. **Particle size:** Smaller nanoparticles (< 100 nm) penetrate more readily; particles < 4 nm can cross intact skin, while those > 20 nm rarely penetrate healthy stratum corneum.

2. **Skin hydration state:** Hydrated skin swells the intercellular lipid domains, increasing lipid fluidity and thereby enhancing nanoparticle diffusion through the stratum corneum. Occlusion (e.g., wearing gloves) dramatically amplifies this effect.

3. **Surface charge of nanoparticle:** Cationic (positively charged) nanoparticles interact electrostatically with the anionic skin surface (dominated by negatively charged lipids and proteins), increasing residence time and uptake compared to anionic or neutral particles.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **three pathways by which nanoparticles penetrate human skin**. Compare their relative importance for food-packaging-derived nanomaterials.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A nano-silver (nAg) coated food container releases **0.8 μg/cm²** of silver nanoparticles (spherical, d = 25 nm) onto a worker's skin during handling. Given that the partition coefficient K = 3.0, diffusion coefficient D = 5 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm²/s, and SC thickness h = 15 μm, compute:
  (i) The permeability coefficient P
  (ii) The steady-state flux J_ss
  (iii) The lag time T_lag
  (iv) Comment on whether the contact is sufficient to reach steady state if the typical contact duration is 5 minutes.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) The Three Pathways of Nanoparticle Skin Penetration:**

**Diagram:**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
        │   NANOPARTICLE (food packaging) │
        └─────────────┬───────────────────┘
                      ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
        │     STRATUM CORNEUM (15-20 μm)  │
        └─┬───────────────┬───────────┬───┘
          ▼               ▼           ▼
   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
   │INTERCELL │   │TRANS-    │  │TRANS-    │
   │ULAR PATH │   │CELLULAR  │  │APPENDAGEAL│
   │(through  │   │(through  │  │(hair/    │
   │ lipid    │   │corneocyte│  │sweat)    │
   │ mortar)  │   │  cell)   │  │          │
   └─────┬────┘   └─────┬────┘  └────┬─────┘
         └───────┬───────┴───────────┘
                 ▼
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │ VIABLE EPIDERMIS    │
        └──────────┬──────────┘
                   ▼
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │ DERMIS → BLOOD      │
        └─────────────────────┘
```

**Description:**

1. **Intercellular Pathway (~99% of SC area):** Nanoparticles traverse the tortuous lipid matrix between stacked corneocytes. The "bricks" (corneocytes) are impermeable; the "mortar" (ceramide-rich lipids) provides the only diffusion channel. This is the **dominant route for small lipophilic nanoparticles** (< 10 nm), as they can dissolve into the lipid phase. For food packaging NPs that are often metallic and hydrophilic, this route has limited applicability.

2. **Transcellular Pathway (< 1% of SC area):** Nanoparticles pass directly through the corneocytes, requiring sequential crossing of the lipophilic cell membrane, hydrophilic cytoplasm, and opposite membrane. The energetic barrier is high, making this route the **least favorable** for intact nanoparticles. It is theoretically relevant only for very small hydrophilic NPs (< 4 nm).

3. **Transappendageal Pathway (< 1% of SC area, but bypasses SC):** Nanoparticles enter through hair follicle openings, sweat ducts, and sebaceous gland openings. Although these appendages occupy a small surface fraction, they **circumvent the SC entirely**, providing direct access to the viable epidermis. Studies show that **rigid spherical metal-oxide NPs (TiO₂, ZnO, Ag) in the 20–200 nm range** accumulate preferentially in follicular reservoirs. **For food packaging-derived NPs (TiO₂ from glazes, ZnO from coatings, nAg from antimicrobial films), this is the most likely penetration route.**

**Comparison for food-packaging NPs:** Among the three pathways, the **transappendageal route** is most relevant for food-packaging-derived nanomaterials because these NPs are typically rigid, spherical, and in the 20–200 nm size range — ideal for follicular accumulation.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 0.8 \, \mu g/cm^2$
- $K = 3.0$
- $D = 5 \times 10^{-10} \, cm^2/s$
- $h = 15 \, \mu m = 15 \times 10^{-4} \, cm$
- Contact time = 5 minutes = 300 s

**(i) Permeability coefficient P:**

$$P = \frac{D \cdot K}{h} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-10})(3.0)}{15 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$P = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, cm/s$$

**(ii) Steady-state flux J_ss:**

$$J_{ss} = P \cdot C_0 = (1.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.8)$$

$$J_{ss} = 8.0 \times 10^{-7} \, \mu g/cm^2/s$$

**(iii) Lag time T_lag:**

$$T_{lag} = \frac{h^2}{6D} = \frac{(15 \times 10^{-4})^2}{6 \times 5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$T_{lag} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{3.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 750 \, s \approx 12.5 \, minutes$$

**(iv) Comment on steady-state attainment:**

The lag time is **750 seconds ≈ 12.5 minutes**, but the contact duration is only **300 seconds (5 minutes)**.

$$t_{contact} = 300 \, s < T_{lag} = 750 \, s$$

**Conclusion:** The contact duration of 5 minutes is **shorter than the lag time**. Therefore, **steady-state flux is NOT reached** during typical handling. However, in scenarios involving occlusion (e.g., tight gloves), damaged skin, or repeated exposure over a work shift, cumulative penetration may approach the steady-state regime. **From a regulatory standpoint, the worker exposure should be considered potentially significant only under such aggravating conditions.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **physicochemical properties of nanoparticles that govern their skin penetration**. Include a note on how each property affects the three penetration pathways.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **structure of human skin** and explain why the **stratum corneum is considered the principal permeation barrier**. Add a note on how food nanotechnology is altering this barrier paradigm.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Physicochemical Properties Governing Skin Penetration:**

| Property | Effect on Penetration | Pathway Most Affected |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Particles < 100 nm penetrate; optimal follicular accumulation at 20–200 nm; < 4 nm can cross intact SC | All three pathways; size is the master variable |
| **Shape** | Spherical: balanced; elongated/flexible (nanorods, nanotubes): enhanced follicular penetration | Transappendageal (intercalates into hair shaft) |
| **Surface Charge (Zeta Potential)** | Cationic (+): strong electrostatic binding to anionic skin; Anionic (−): minimal binding; Neutral: passive | All pathways; cationic NPs show 4–8× higher uptake |
| **Hydrophobicity (Log P)** | Lipophilic (log P > 1): preferential intercellular lipid solubility; Hydrophilic: limited to shunt route | Intercellular (lipophilic) vs Transappendageal (hydrophilic) |
| **Surface Functionalization** | PEGylation: "stealth" property, reduces protein corona, can paradoxically enhance follicular penetration; peptide coating: enables transcellular uptake | All pathways depending on ligand |
| **Crystallinity / Rigidity** | Rigid, crystalline NPs: high follicular retention; Soft, deformable NPs (nanoemulsions): squeeze through SC lipids | Transappendageal (rigid) vs Intercellular (soft) |
| **Aggregation State** | Agglomerates > 200 nm: behave as microparticles — no penetration; Stable colloids: maintain nano-properties | All pathways |
| **Protein Corona Formation** | In situ formation of biomolecular corona in sweat/SEBUM alters apparent surface properties | Modulates all pathways |

**Analytical insight:** The **"size-charge-shape" triad** is the most critical design parameter. For food packaging NPs, which are often **rigid, spherical, and slightly anionic (e.g., citrate-capped nAg)**, the **transappendageal route** dominates.

---

**(b) Skin Structure and the Stratum Corneum Barrier:**

**Structure of Human Skin (from outermost to innermost):**

1. **Stratum Corneum (SC) — "The Dead Barrier"**
   - 10–20 μm thick (dry); up to 40 μm when hydrated
   - 15–20 layers of corneocytes (anucleate, keratin-filled cells)
   - Embedded in a **lipid matrix** of ceramides (50%), cholesterol (25%), and free fatty acids (25%)
   - pH: ~4.5–5.5 ("acid mantle")

2. **Viable Epidermis (VE) — 50–100 μm**
   - Stratum granulosum, spinosum, basale
   - Contains **Langerhans cells** (antigen presentation)
   - Tight junctions (claudins) form a secondary barrier

3. **Dermis — 1–2 mm**
   - Collagen and elastin network
   - Blood capillaries, lymphatics, nerves
   - **Hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands**

4. **Hypodermis (Subcutis) — variable**
   - Adipose tissue; not a primary permeation barrier

**Why is the Stratum Corneum the Principal Permeation Barrier?**

- **Tortuous diffusion path:** The "brick-and-mortar" architecture forces molecules to take a **zigzag path** through 15–20 layers, increasing the effective diffusion length by a factor of ~**20–30×** compared to a straight membrane.
- **Selective lipophilicity:** Only molecules with **log P between 1 and 4** optimally partition into the SC lipids.
- **Molecular weight cutoff:** Molecules **> 500 Da** penetrate poorly.
- **Low water content (~15%)** in SC limits hydrophilic diffusion.
- **Constant desquamation:** Top layers are continuously shed, removing any surface-bound nanomaterials.

**Food Nanotechnology and the Barrier Paradigm:**

Food nanotechnology is **disrupting the classical barrier paradigm** in three ways:

1. **Particle size reduction to < 100 nm** allows penetration pathways that are essentially closed to microparticles, including the transappendageal route.
2. **Engineered surface coatings** (e.g., chitosan, cyclodextrins) used in nano-encapsulation can either **enhance** penetration (by fluidizing SC lipids) or **inhibit** it (by forming a film).
3. **Chronic low-dose exposure** from food contact materials (TiO₂ in candy glazes, nAg in packaging) creates **cumulative follicular depots** that may be released over days, bypassing the body's natural elimination kinetics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **transappendageal** route with the **intercellular** route. They are distinct: one bypasses the SC, the other traverses it through lipids.
> - **Do NOT** state that "all nanoparticles penetrate skin" — this is false. Only NPs below specific size/shape/charge windows show significant penetration.
> - **Do NOT** confuse **lag time** (time to reach steady state) with **diffusion time** (time to cross the SC). They are inversely related.
> - **Numerical errors:** Forgetting to convert μm to cm (factor of 10⁻⁴) is the most common mistake in Fick's Law problems. Always write out unit conversions explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Skin exposure to nanoparticles** is a critical — but often overlooked — route of nanomaterial entry, especially for food contact material workers and consumers.
- **Three penetration pathways:** Intercellular (lipid, dominant for small lipophilic NPs), Transcellular (rare, for < 4 nm hydrophilic), Transappendageal (bypasses SC, favored for 20–200 nm rigid spheres).
- **Stratum corneum** is the principal barrier; thickness 10–20 μm, "brick-and-mortar" structure.
- **Key size threshold:** NPs < 100 nm can penetrate; < 4 nm can cross intact SC readily.
- **Fick's First Law:** $J = D \cdot K \cdot C_0 / h$ — the master equation for steady-state skin diffusion.
- **Lag time:** $T_{lag} = h^2 / 6D$ — must be considered when evaluating short-contact exposures.
- **Factors affecting penetration:** Particle size, shape, charge, log P, surface coating; skin hydration, integrity, anatomical site; vehicle, occlusion, temperature.
- **Food nanotech relevance:** nAg, TiO₂, ZnO, nanoemulsions, nano-encapsulated nutraceuticals.
- **Regulatory framework:** EU 1935/2004, US FDA 21 CFR, EFSA guidance on nanotechnologies in food.
- **Toxicological endpoints to assess:** Cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, sensitization, genotoxicity, reproductive toxicity.
- **Hair follicles as reservoirs:** Follicular accumulation of 20–200 nm NPs creates long-term depots — a unique feature distinct from molecular skin absorption.
- **In-vitro test method:** Franz diffusion cell is the standard for measuring $K_p$ of nanomaterials through skin.
- **Mathematical safeguards:** Always check unit consistency (μm → cm = 10⁻⁴; minutes → seconds = 60).

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Skin Exposure to Nanoparticles**
*PEFTT522 — Nanotechnology in Food | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards and Risks of Exposure to Nanoparticles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT522 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazards and Risks of Exposure to Nanoparticles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Nanoparticle Hazard (Nanohazard)** refers to the intrinsic potential of an engineered nanomaterial (ENM) — typically defined as any intentionally produced material with one or more external dimensions ≤ **100 nm** — to cause adverse biological, chemical, or physical effects in living organisms (humans, animals, plants) or in ecological systems upon contact, uptake, or accumulation.

**Risk** in the nanotoxicology context is the quantitative probability that such a hazard will manifest under defined exposure conditions, expressed as the product of intrinsic hazard and the magnitude/duration of exposure:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Hazard}) \times f(\text{Exposure})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Examiner's Expectation):**
> *"A nanohazard is the capacity of a free nanoparticle (1–100 nm) to cross biological barriers — alveolar, intestinal, dermal, placental, or blood–brain — and generate toxicity via mechanisms that are absent or negligible for the same material in bulk form."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Cactus"

Imagine a sugar cube. It is harmless, smooth, and dissolves slowly in your tea. Now imagine grinding that same sugar into a powder a billion times finer — so fine that **a single grain is 1,000 times thinner than a human hair**. Each invisible grain is no longer smooth: at the nano-scale, the **surface is jagged, chemically aggressive, and reactive**, like a microscopic cactus.

These "nano-cacti":
- Slip past the body's defense filters (nose hair, mucus, skin),
- Pierce cell membranes like microscopic needles,
- Spark chemical chain reactions (oxidative stress) inside tissues that bulk sugar would never trigger.

This is the paradox of nanotechnology: **the smaller the particle, the greater the surface reactivity per unit mass**, and therefore the greater the potential biological hazard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why food nanotechnology is a special concern:** Ingested engineered nanoparticles (e.g., nano-TiO₂ as a whitener, nano-silver as an antimicrobial, nano-SiO₂ as an anti-caking agent) are *intentionally* added to food, meaning **exposure is direct, repeated, and consumer-wide** — unlike occupational exposure, which is limited to workers.

### 1.3 Key Defining Metrics (Bolded Constants & Thresholds)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nanoparticle size threshold | $d$ | **≤ 100 nm** | Regulatory boundary (EU, FDA, ISO) |
| Specific Surface Area | $SSA$ | **> 60 m²/g** | Defines "nano" reactivity range |
| Reference specific surface area | $SSA_{ref}$ | **> 60 m²/g** | EC Recommendation 2011/696/EU |
| Fiber aspect ratio (pathogenic threshold) | $L/d$ | **> 3 : 1** | WHO fiber paradigm |
| Biopersistence half-life | $t_{1/2}$ | **> 40 days (lung)** | Asbestos-like behaviour |
| ROS threshold (in vitro) | DCFH-DA | **> 2× control** | Oxidative stress marker |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Nanoparticle Hazard

The hazard potential of any engineered nanomaterial is governed by **five interlinked physico-chemical determinants** — these are favourite KTU 14-mark sub-questions.

#### Pillar 1: Particle Size & Specific Surface Area

As particle diameter $d$ decreases, the fraction of atoms exposed on the surface increases dramatically. For a sphere:

$$SSA = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}} = \frac{3}{\rho \cdot r} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$

where $\rho$ is bulk density (kg/m³), $d$ is diameter (m), and $SSA$ is in m²/kg.

> **Why this matters:** Reducing particle size from 10 µm → 10 nm increases SSA by a factor of **1,000**, dramatically increasing catalytic surface reactivity, dissolution rate, and protein-binding capacity.

#### Pillar 2: Surface Chemistry & Reactivity

- **Surface charge (Zeta potential, $\zeta$):** Particles with $\vert\zeta\vert > 30$ mV are more readily taken up by cells.
- **Hydrophobicity:** Increases membrane penetration and bioaccumulation.
- **Surface functional groups** (–COOH, –NH₂, –OH) dictate protein corona formation.

#### Pillar 3: Shape & Morphology (Fiber Paradigm)

High-aspect-ratio nanomaterials (HARN) such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) follow the **WHO fiber pathogenicity paradigm** (Stanton–Pott analogy):

$$\text{Pathogenicity} \propto L \times d^{2} \times \rho_{fiber} \times t_{biopersist}$$

where $L$ = length, $d$ = diameter, $\rho_{fiber}$ = fiber density, $t_{biopersist}$ = biopersistence time.

> **Real-world implication:** Long, thin, biopersistent nanotubes behave like asbestos and induce mesothelioma-like pathology.

#### Pillar 4: Composition & Dissolution

Some nanoparticles are "Trojan horse" carriers — relatively inert carriers (e.g., TiO₂) that dissolve in lysosomal fluid (pH ~4.5–5) releasing toxic ions:

$$\text{TiO}_{2(s)} + \text{H}^{+} \xrightarrow{\text{lysosome}} \text{Ti}^{4+}_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{ROS generation}$$

#### Pillar 5: Agglomeration & Protein Corona

In biological fluids, nanoparticles immediately adsorb proteins forming a "**protein corona**" which determines biological identity — often more than the synthetic core identity.

### 2.2 Routes of Exposure (KTU Diagram Favourite)

1. **Inhalation** — most studied; pulmonary deposition follows the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP) model**, with alveolar deposition maximal for **10–100 nm** particles.
2. **Ingestion** — *primary route for food-grade ENMs*; particles translocate via Peyer's patches in the gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT).
3. **Dermal** — only particles < **4 nm** (Borm rule) penetrate intact stratum corneum; larger particles enter via hair follicles / flexed skin.
4. **Parenteral / injection** — relevant for nanomedicine but rare for food.
5. **Translocation** — secondary; particles crossing the alveolar barrier reach systemic circulation, and may reach the **brain via the olfactory nerve** (nasal–brain axis).

### 2.3 Mechanisms of Toxicity (Mechanistic Cascade)

All major nanotoxicological pathways converge on **oxidative stress**:

$$\text{ENM} \rightarrow \text{ROS generation (O}_{2}^{\bullet-}, \text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{2}, \text{\textbullet OH)} \rightarrow \text{Lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, Inflammation} \rightarrow \text{Genotoxicity, Carcinogenicity, Fibrosis}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever asked "mechanism of nanotoxicity," always start with **ROS / oxidative stress**, then expand to **mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation (NF-κB pathway), lysosomal disruption, and genotoxicity**. This is the examiner's scoring pathway.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Rule for Tables:** Vertical pipe characters have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. Always use `\vert\zeta\vert` instead of $|\zeta|$.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Application in Nanohazard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Surface Area (sphere) | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ | $\rho$ = kg/m³, $d$ = m, SSA = m²/kg | Quantifies reactive surface per mass dose |
| 2 | Total Surface Area per mass | $A_{tot} = SSA \cdot m_{dose}$ | $m_{dose}$ in mg or kg | Converts mass dose → surface area dose |
| 3 | Stokes Settling Velocity | $v_{s} = \dfrac{\rho_{p} g d^{2}}{18 \eta}$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\eta$ = Pa·s | Predicts alveolar deposition |
| 4 | Brownian Diffusion Coefficient | $D = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta d}$ | $k_{B}$ = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, T in K | Mobility of 1–100 nm particles |
| 5 | Particle Number per unit mass | $N = \dfrac{6 m_{dose}}{\pi \rho d^{3}}$ | dimensionless, m in kg | Translates mass dose → number dose |
| 6 | Fiber Pathogenicity Index | $PI = L \cdot d^{2}$ | L, d in µm | WHO fiber paradigm |
| 7 | Risk Function | $R = H \times E$ | dimensionless | Hazard × Exposure |
| 8 | Margin of Exposure (MOE) | $MOE = \dfrac{BMDL}{E}$ | mg/kg·day | Higher MOE = safer |
| 9 | No Observed Adverse Effect Level | $NOAEL$ | mg/kg·day | Reference dose threshold |
| 10 | Surface Area Dose | $SAD = C_{mass} \cdot SSA$ | m²/mL or m²/kg | Recommended dose metric by Oberdörster |

### 2.5 Dose Metric Debate — Mass vs. Surface Area vs. Number

> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Which dose metric best predicts toxicity?

| Metric | Best Predicts Toxicity For | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass dose (mg/kg)** | Bulk chemicals | Traditional toxicology |
| **Surface area dose (m²/kg)** | Low-solubility nanoparticles (TiO₂, CNT) | **Oberdörster (2005)** showed pulmonary inflammation correlates with $SSA$, not mass |
| **Particle number dose (#/kg)** | Very small particles < 10 nm | Each particle carries its own dose |

> **Examiner's Verdict:** For insoluble, low-toxicity ENMs, **surface area dose is the gold standard** and is the most likely answer to a "discuss" question on dose metrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Specific Surface Area of a Spherical Nanoparticle

**Given:** A spherical nanoparticle of diameter $d$ and bulk density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Define geometric surface area of a sphere:**

$$A = 4\pi r^{2} = 4\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi d^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define volume and mass:**

$$V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{3} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$$

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Form the specific surface area ratio $SSA = A/m$:**

$$SSA = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{\rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify algebraically (cancelling $\pi$ and one $d$):**

$$SSA = \frac{\pi d^{2} \cdot 6}{\rho \cdot \pi d^{3}} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$$

> **Final boxed result:**
> $$\boxed{SSA = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d}}$$
> 
> **Units check:** $SSA$ in [m²/kg] = $\dfrac{1}{(\text{kg/m}^{3})(\text{m})} = \dfrac{1}{\text{kg/m}^{2}} = \text{m}^{2}/\text{kg}$ ✔

### 3.2 Derivation II: Comparison of Surface Area — Bulk vs. Nanoparticle

**Problem:** Compare the total surface area of 1 g of 10 µm particles vs. 1 g of 10 nm particles, assuming $\rho = 4{,}000$ kg/m³ (TiO₂).

**Step 1 — Compute SSA for the bulk (10 µm) particle:**

$$SSA_{bulk} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d_{bulk}} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$SSA_{bulk} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{6}{0.04} = 150 \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{kg} = 0.15 \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute SSA for the nanoparticle (10 nm):**

$$SSA_{nano} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{6}{4 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$SSA_{nano} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{kg} = 150 \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{SSA_{nano}}{SSA_{bulk}} = \frac{d_{bulk}}{d_{nano}} = \frac{10\,\mu m}{10\,nm} = 1000$$

> **Interpretation:** The same 1 g of TiO₂, when nano-sized, exposes **1,000 times more reactive surface** to biological systems. This is the *fundamental reason* nano-TiO₂ raises safety questions even though bulk TiO₂ is "Generally Recognised As Safe" (GRAS).

### 3.3 Derivation III: Particle Number in a Given Mass Dose

**Problem:** How many particles are in 1 mg of 50 nm TiO₂? ($\rho = 4{,}000$ kg/m³)

**Step 1 — Mass of a single particle:**

$$m_{p} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} = 4000 \times \frac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-9})^{3}}{6}$$

$$m_{p} = 4000 \times \frac{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-22}}{6} = 4000 \times 6.545 \times 10^{-23}$$

$$m_{p} \approx 2.618 \times 10^{-19} \;\text{kg per particle}$$

**Step 2 — Total mass:** $m_{dose} = 1 \text{ mg} = 10^{-6}$ kg

**Step 3 — Number of particles:**

$$N = \frac{m_{dose}}{m_{p}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{2.618 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.82 \times 10^{12}$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{N \approx 3.82 \times 10^{12} \text{ particles in 1 mg of 50 nm TiO}_2}$
> 
> *That is roughly **3.8 trillion** particles — a number-dose perspective explains why even trace food additives can produce massive particle exposures.*

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Severity of Common Food-Grade ENMs

| Nanomaterial | Food Application | Primary Hazard | Risk Class (EFSA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TiO₂ (E171)** | Whitener, opacifier | ROS, intestinal inflammation, possible carcinogen (IARC 2B) | High concern |
| **Nano-silver (AgNP)** | Antimicrobial packaging | Ion release (Ag⁺), argyria, microbiome disruption | High concern |
| **Nano-SiO₂ (E551)** | Anti-caking | Lung fibrosis, granuloma formation | Moderate–High |
| **Nano-ZnO** | UV protection, packaging | Zn²⁺ release, cytotoxicity | Moderate |
| **Nano-iron (Fe⁰)** | Iron fortification | Fenton reaction → OH· radical | Low–Moderate |
| **Lipid nanocapsules** | Nutrient delivery | Generally low; GRAS excipients | Low |
| **Chitosan NPs** | Edible coatings, antimicrobials | Low; biodegradable | Low |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Margin of Exposure (MOE) Calculation

**Scenario:** EFSA sets a Benchmark Dose Lower Bound (BMDL₁₀) of **5 mg/kg·day** for nano-TiO₂ from a chronic feeding study. The estimated daily dietary exposure is **0.1 mg/kg·day**.

**Solution:**

$$MOE = \frac{BMDL_{10}}{E} = \frac{5}{0.1} = 50$$

> **Interpretation:** MOE = 50 is **below the EFSA threshold of 10,000** for genotoxic carcinogens but **above 100**, which is the typical concern threshold for non-genotoxic substances. This places nano-TiO₂ in a *medium-priority risk* zone for further monitoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `route1`) — never reserved words.
- All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no `**bold**`, no italics, no HTML tables.
- One blank line before/after each `mermaid` block.

### 4.2 Diagram 1: Five Routes of Human Exposure to Food-Grade Nanoparticles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["ENM IN FOOD PRODUCT"] --> route1["ROUTE 1 INGESTION"]
    node1 --> route2["ROUTE 2 INHALATION DUST POWDER"]
    node1 --> route3["ROUTE 3 DERMAL SKIN CONTACT"]
    node1 --> route4["ROUTE 4 MUCOUS MEMBRANE EYE"]

    route1 --> destA["GASTRO INTESTINAL TRACT GALT PEYERS PATCHES"]
    route1 --> destB["SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION"]

    route2 --> destC["ALVEOLAR EPITHELIUM"]
    destC --> destB
    destC --> destD["OLFACTORY NERVE TO BRAIN"]

    route3 --> destE["HAIR FOLLICLE FLEXED SKIN"]
    route3 --> destB

    route4 --> destB

    destA --> destB
    destB --> destF["TARGET ORGANS LIVER SPLEEN KIDNEY BRAIN"]
    destF --> destG["BIOLOGICAL EFFECTS"]
    destG --> out1["ROS OXIDATIVE STRESS"]
    destG --> out2["INFLAMMATION"]
    destG --> out3["GENOTOXICITY"]
    destG --> out4["ORGAN DAMAGE"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 2: Mechanism Cascade of Nanoparticle Toxicity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["ENM ENTERS BODY"] --> stepB["PHYSICO CHEMICAL INTERACTION WITH BIOMOLECULES"]
    stepB --> stepC["PROTEIN CORONA FORMATION"]
    stepC --> stepD["CELLULAR UPTAKE ENDOCYTOSIS"]
    stepD --> stepE["LYSOSOMAL ENCAPSULATION"]
    stepE --> stepF["DISSOLVING IONS RELEASE OR FRAGMENTATION"]
    stepF --> stepG["ROS GENERATION O2 HO H2O2"]
    stepG --> stepH["OXIDATIVE STRESS"]
    stepH --> stepI["MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION"]
    stepH --> stepJ["DNA DAMAGE GENOTOXICITY"]
    stepH --> stepK["NF KB INFLAMMATION"]
    stepI --> stepL["APOPTOSIS NECROSIS"]
    stepJ --> stepL
    stepK --> stepM["FIBROSIS CARCINOMA"]
    stepL --> stepM
```

### 4.4 Diagram 3: Nano Risk Assessment Framework (EFSA / REACH Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["PHASE 1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION"] --> phase2["PHASE 2 DOSE RESPONSE ASSESSMENT"]
    phase2 --> phase3["PHASE 3 EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT"]
    phase3 --> phase4["PHASE 4 RISK CHARACTERIZATION"]
    phase4 --> phase5["PHASE 5 RISK MANAGEMENT DECISION"]

    phase1 --> sub1A["PHYSICO CHEMICAL CHARACTERIZATION"]
    phase1 --> sub1B["IN VITRO TOXICITY SCREENING"]
    phase1 --> sub1C["IN VIVO ANIMAL STUDIES"]

    phase2 --> sub2A["BMD BMDL CALCULATION"]
    phase2 --> sub2B["NOAEL LOAEL DETERMINATION"]
    phase2 --> sub2C["SURFACE AREA DOSE RESPONSE"]

    phase3 --> sub3A["DIETARY INTAKE ESTIMATION"]
    phase3 --> sub3B["MIGRATION FROM PACKAGING"]
    phase3 --> sub3C["AGGREGATION DISSOLUTION KINETICS"]

    phase4 --> sub4A["MARGIN OF EXPOSURE MOE"]
    phase4 --> sub4B["RISK QUOTIENT RQ"]
    phase4 --> sub4C["PROBABILISTIC MODELLING"]

    phase5 --> outA["ACCEPT RISK"]
    phase5 --> outB["RESTRICT USE"]
    phase5 --> outC["BAN OR REFORMULATE"]
    phase5 --> outD["POST MARKET MONITORING"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]
**Define the term "nanoparticle hazard" and list any four primary routes through which food-grade engineered nanoparticles can enter the human body.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **nanoparticle hazard** is the inherent capacity of an engineered nanomaterial (≤ 100 nm) to cause adverse biological effects in living systems due to its size-specific reactivity.

**Four primary routes of exposure:**
1. **Ingestion** — via food and drink containing food-grade ENMs (e.g., nano-TiO₂ E171).
2. **Inhalation** — through dust or aerosol during food processing/packaging.
3. **Dermal contact** — penetration via flexed skin or hair follicles (only < 4 nm for intact skin).
4. **Mucosal absorption** — across the eyes and gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT).

> *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 routes = 3 marks)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between "hazard" and "risk" in the context of nanotoxicology. Write the fundamental risk equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hazard** is the *intrinsic* property of a nanomaterial to cause harm (e.g., AgNP releasing Ag⁺ ions).
- **Risk** is the *probability* that this harm will occur under a specific exposure scenario.

**Fundamental risk equation:**

$$R = H \times E$$

where $H$ = Hazard, $E$ = Exposure (intensity × duration × frequency).

> A material may be **highly hazardous** but pose **low risk** if exposure is negligible, and vice-versa. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Nano-TiO₂ and Dietary Risk**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the physico-chemical determinants of nanoparticle hazard. (7 marks)

**(b)** With suitable derivations, explain why nano-sized TiO₂ exhibits a greater biological hazard than bulk TiO₂ of the same chemical composition. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Physico-Chemical Determinants of Nanoparticle Hazard (7 marks):**

The toxicity of an ENM is governed by **five interlocked determinants**:

1. **Particle size and surface area** (2 marks): As diameter $d$ decreases, specific surface area $SSA = 6/(\rho d)$ increases inversely. For 10 nm vs. 10 µm particles, SSA is 1,000× greater, intensifying catalytic activity, ROS generation, and protein binding.

2. **Shape and aspect ratio** (1.5 marks): High-aspect-ratio nanomaterials (HARNs) like CNTs mimic asbestos fibres; WHO fiber paradigm states that fibers with $L/d > 3$ and biopersistence > 40 days cause mesothelioma.

3. **Surface chemistry** (1.5 marks): Zeta potential $\vert\zeta\vert > 30$ mV promotes cellular uptake; hydrophobic surfaces penetrate lipid bilayers; functional groups (–COOH, –NH₂) define protein corona and biological identity.

4. **Composition and dissolution** (1 mark): Some particles are "Trojan horses" — TiO₂ and ZnO dissolve in lysosomal pH ~4.5, releasing Ti⁴⁺ / Zn²⁺ ions that disrupt cellular machinery.

5. **Agglomeration state** (1 mark): In food matrices, ENMs agglomerate, altering effective size and bioavailability.

---

**(b) Why Nano-TiO₂ is more hazardous than bulk TiO₂ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define particle diameter for both forms:**
- Bulk TiO₂: $d_{bulk} = 10\;\mu m = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Nano TiO₂: $d_{nano} = 10\;nm = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ m

**Step 2 — Compute specific surface area for bulk (with $\rho = 4{,}000$ kg/m³):**

$$SSA_{bulk} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot d_{bulk}} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10^{-5}} = 150 \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute specific surface area for nano:**

$$SSA_{nano} = \frac{6}{4000 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \;\text{m}^{2}/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Surface area ratio:**

$$\frac{SSA_{nano}}{SSA_{bulk}} = \frac{d_{bulk}}{d_{nano}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-8}} = 1000$$

> **Biological consequence:** A 1,000-fold larger reactive surface produces ~1,000× more ROS per unit mass, intensifying oxidative stress, inflammation, and potential carcinogenicity. IARC has classified TiO₂ as **Group 2B — possibly carcinogenic to humans**, and EFSA (2021) re-classified E171 (food-grade TiO₂) as **no longer safe** as a food additive — a landmark regulatory decision.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Dose Metrics & Risk Assessment**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Critically evaluate the three dose metrics (mass, surface area, and particle number) used in nanotoxicology, with examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Outline the four-step risk assessment framework for engineered nanomaterials in food, with the MOE formula and one numerical example. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Three Dose Metrics in Nanotoxicology (7 marks):**

1. **Mass dose (mg/kg·day)** (2 marks): The classical toxicological unit. Suitable for soluble ENMs that release ions (e.g., ZnO, Ag) since their toxicity is governed by dissolved ion mass. *Limitation:* fails to predict toxicity of insoluble low-solubility particles.

2. **Surface area dose (m²/kg)** (2.5 marks): Recommended by **Oberdörster et al. (2005)** for low-solubility nanoparticles (TiO₂, CNT, soot). Pulmonary inflammation in rats correlated linearly with $SSA$, not mass. *Example:* Two TiO₂ samples of equal mass dose but different particle sizes showed proportional inflammation only when doses were expressed as $m^{2}$.

3. **Particle number dose (#/kg)** (2.5 marks): Critical for ultra-fine particles < 10 nm where each particle delivers a discrete dose. *Example:* 1 mg of 50 nm TiO₂ contains ~$3.82 \times 10^{12}$ particles. Number-dose is also central to EU nanomaterial definition (2011/696/EU: $SSA > 60\;m^{2}/g$ implies number-based nano-identity).

---

**(b) Four-Step Risk Assessment Framework for ENMs in Food (7 marks):**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Hazard Identification** | Physico-chemical characterization, in vitro screening, in vivo animal studies | Toxicological profile |
| 2. **Dose–Response Assessment** | BMD/BMDL, NOAEL, LOAEL determination | Reference dose |
| 3. **Exposure Assessment** | Dietary intake estimation, migration kinetics, aggregation studies | Estimated human exposure |
| 4. **Risk Characterization** | MOE, Risk Quotient (RQ), probabilistic modelling | Final risk decision |

**MOE formula:**

$$MOE = \frac{BMDL_{10}}{E_{human}}$$

**Numerical example (1.5 marks):**
- $BMDL_{10} = 5$ mg/kg·day (from chronic rat study)
- Estimated human dietary exposure $E = 0.05$ mg/kg·day
- $MOE = 5 / 0.05 = 100$

> Since MOE = 100 is **below 10,000** (EFSA threshold for genotoxic substances) and **borderline for non-genotoxic** (threshold ≈ 100), this nanomaterial would require **risk management action** such as restriction or post-market monitoring.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating hazard and risk** (–1 mark): "Hazard = capacity to harm," "Risk = probability under exposure" — examiners deduct heavily if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion in $SSA$** (–1 mark): Always show $SSA$ in m²/kg and verify the dimensional match.
> 3. **Omitting the dose-metric debate** (–2 marks): In any 14-mark question on nanotoxicity, examiners expect a *comparative* discussion of mass, surface-area, and number dose — skipping this is a top failure point.
> 4. **Ignoring EFSA 2021 ruling on E171 (TiO₂)** (–1 mark): This is the *single most important* recent regulatory event in food nanotechnology; not knowing it is treated as a serious gap.
> 5. **Missing protein corona** (–1 mark): Any modern discussion of nanobio-interaction must mention the protein corona — its absence signals outdated knowledge.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Read twice before the exam.**

- **Definition:** ENM = any material with $\ge 1$ dimension $\le 100$ nm; **nanohazard** = capacity of such materials to cause biological harm.
- **Risk equation:** $R = H \times E$ — never confuse $H$ (intrinsic) with $R$ (probabilistic).
- **The 5 determinants of hazard:** Size / SSA, Shape / aspect ratio, Surface chemistry, Composition / dissolution, Agglomeration / protein corona.
- **Specific Surface Area formula (KTU favourite):** $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \cdot d}$ — derivation from first principles is expected.
- **Surface area ratio = diameter ratio** (for same mass, same density) — a 1,000× size reduction ⇒ 1,000× more reactive surface.
- **Number dose formula:** $N = \dfrac{6 m}{\pi \rho d^{3}}$ — 1 mg of 50 nm TiO₂ ≈ $3.8 \times 10^{12}$ particles.
- **Stokes settling** dominates above ~1 µm; **Brownian diffusion** dominates below ~100 nm — this is the basis of alveolar deposition in the 10–100 nm window.
- **WHO Fiber Paradigm:** $L/d > 3$ and biopersistence > 40 days = asbestos-like behaviour; CNTs fall in this category.
- **Toxicity mechanism cascade:** ENM → protein corona → uptake → lysosome → dissolution / ROS → oxidative stress → inflammation (NF-κB) + DNA damage → fibrosis / cancer.
- **Five exposure routes:** Inhalation, Ingestion (primary for food), Dermal (< 4 nm), Mucous, Parenteral.
- **Dose metric debate:**
  - Mass dose → soluble ENMs.
  - Surface area dose → insoluble low-solubility ENMs (Oberdörster 2005).
  - Number dose → ultrafine < 10 nm.
- **MOE threshold (EFSA):** < 10,000 = high concern (genotoxic); < 100 = high concern (non-genotoxic).
- **EFSA 2021 ruling on E171 (food-grade TiO₂):** *no longer considered safe* as a food additive — must be memorised for 14-mark questions.
- **High-risk food-grade ENMs (memorise):** TiO₂ (E171) → IARC 2B; Nano-Ag → Ag⁺ release; Nano-SiO₂ (E551) → granuloma; Nano-ZnO → Zn²⁺ cytotoxicity.
- **Regulatory threshold for "nano" (EU 2011/696/EU):** $SSA > 60\;m^{2}/g$ OR particle size $\le 100$ nm.
- **Most likely 14-mark question patterns for this topic:**
  1. "Discuss the factors influencing nanoparticle hazard" → write the 5 determinants.
  2. "Compare bulk vs. nano with derivation" → use the $SSA$ derivation.
  3. "Outline the risk assessment framework with MOE" → use the 4-step EFSA framework.
  4. "Explain ROS-based toxicity mechanism" → use the mechanistic cascade.
